description: Identify the bug in this attempt to avoid Copy-on-Write (COW) overhead.

options.text: COW strings were banned by the C++11 standard to ensure that 'operator[]' and iterators remain valid and thread-safe; this check is redundant in modern C++.
The 'is_shared()' method was removed in C++14.
Strings are always copied by value in C++.
COW is only used for strings larger than 1MB.

explanation: The C++11 standard requires that string elements be stored contiguously and that concurrent reads are safe. This effectively made COW implementations illegal for 'std::string'.
code: if (s.is_shared()) s.make_unique(); // Assuming legacy COW

description: Why does this 'std::string::append' call with a pointer range crash?

options.text: The pointer 'end' is calculated incorrectly; 's.data() + s.size()' is the correct boundary, but using pointers from different allocations in 'append' is UB.
Append only accepts iterators, not raw pointers.
If 'source' is modified during 'append', it invalidates the internal pointers.
The 'end' pointer must point to a null character.

explanation: The bug occurs if 'source' and the target string are the same object. If 'append' causes a reallocation, the 'begin' and 'end' pointers become dangling before the copy is complete.
code: s.append(s.data(), s.data() + s.size());

description: Identify the bug in this 'std::string::npos' check.

options.text: Using 'int' to store the result of 'find' leads to an error because 'npos' is the largest value of 'size_t'; when cast to 'int', it becomes -1, but the comparison might fail due to sign-extension rules.
Find returns 'false' on failure, not 'npos'.
Npos is a member of 'std::string_view', not 'std::string'.
The result must be compared using '==='.

explanation: 'size_t' is unsigned and usually 64-bit. 'int' is signed and usually 32-bit. Comparing a 32-bit -1 to a 64-bit 'npos' can yield inconsistent results depending on the compiler's promotion rules.
code:
int pos = s.find('z');
if (pos == std::string::npos) { ... }

description: What is the issue with this 'std::string' move assignment inside a 'noexcept' function?

options.text: Move assignment of a string is only guaranteed 'noexcept' if the allocators are equal or the allocator has 'propagate_on_container_move_assignment'.
Strings can never be move-assigned in noexcept blocks.
The string 's' must be cleared first.
Move assignment always allocates memory.

explanation: If two strings have different allocators and the allocator doesn't propagate, the move assignment must perform a copy-and-deallocate, which can throw an exception.

description: Identify the bug in this manual string null-termination.

options.text: Writing to 's[s.size()]' was undefined behavior until C++11; even now, you cannot modify the null terminator of a 'std::string' to anything other than 0.
Index 5 is the 6th character.
Strings are automatically terminated; manual termination deletes the string.
The character must be '\NULL'.

explanation: In C++11 and later, 's[s.size()]' returns a reference to the null terminator, but modifying it is illegal. The string manages its own terminator; users should only modify indices from 0 to size-1.
code:
std::string s = "hello";
s[5] = 'x';

description: Why does this 'std::string' constructor from an iterator range fail for 'char*' from a 'void*' buffer?

options.text: The constructor expects InputIterators; 'char*' works, but 'reinterpret_cast' inside a range-based constructor is not permitted.
Strings cannot be constructed from raw pointers in modern C++.
The pointer distance cannot be calculated for void types.
Both A and C are correct.

explanation: While 'char*' are valid iterators, the issue is often alignment or type-safety. However, the most direct 'bug' in C++ is that a range [start, end) must be reachable; if the pointers aren't from the same block, it's UB.

description: Identify the bug in this code that uses 'std::string::at()' for modification.

options.text: Actually, this code is correct, but 'at()' is significantly slower than '[]' in high-performance paths due to bounds checking.
At() returns a copy of the character, not a reference.
The 'at()' method is only for reading, not writing.
At() ignores the null terminator.

explanation: This is a 'performance' bug disguised as a code bug. In a 'hard' context, choosing 'at()' inside a tight loop is considered a flaw in systems programming due to the redundant branch overhead.

description: Why is 'std::string::max_size()' not a reliable indicator of how much memory you can allocate?

options.text: It returns the theoretical maximum based on the size type, but the actual limit is determined by the available heap memory and system address space.
It returns bits instead of bytes.
The value changes every time you append a string.
It is a private member in many STL implementations.

explanation: 'max_size()' is a constant limit of the container type. It doesn't know about current RAM availability or fragmentation. Attempting to allocate 'max_size()' will almost always throw 'std::bad_alloc'.
code: s.resize(s.max_size());

description: Identify the bug in this UTF-8 string length calculation.

options.text: 'std::string::size()' returns the number of bytes, not the number of characters; for UTF-8, multi-byte characters will result in an incorrect count.
Size() only works for ASCII strings.
Strings must be converted to 'UTF16' before calling size().
The count is always off by one due to the BOM.

explanation: 'std::string' is a byte container. It has no knowledge of UTF-8 encoding. A character like '©' takes 2 bytes, so 'size()' returns 2 for a 1-character string.
code:
std::string s = "©";
if (s.size() == 1) { ... }

description: What is the issue with using 'std::string::erase' with iterators from a different string?

options.text: It is undefined behavior; iterators are only valid for the specific container instance they were created from.
The compiler will automatically correct the iterator to the current string.
Erase only accepts integer indices.
It only works if the strings are the same length.

explanation: Passing an iterator from 's2' into 's1.erase()' is a severe bug. The behavior is undefined and usually results in a crash or memory corruption.
code: s1.erase(s2.begin());

description: Identify the bug in this string to integer conversion using 'sscanf'.

options.text: Passing 'std::string' directly to 'sscanf' is illegal; the function expects 'const char*', so 's.c_str()' must be used.
sscanf doesn't support the %d format.
Strings must be cast to 'int*'.
sscanf is a C++23 feature only.

explanation: 'sscanf' is a variadic C-function. It doesn't understand C++ objects. Passing 's' will pass the object's memory layout (including its pointers and size) instead of the string data.

description: Why does this 'std::string::replace' call with an iterator range crash?

options.text: The iterator range being replaced must belong to the same string object that 'replace' is called on.
Replace requires the new string to be smaller than the range.
The range must be sorted.
Iterators must be converted to 'const_iterator'.

explanation: You cannot use iterators from 's1' to define the range to replace in 's2'. This is undefined behavior.
code: s1.replace(s2.begin(), s2.end(), "new");

description: Identify the bug in this 'std::string' move construction logic.

options.text: Actually, the code is technically correct, but 's1' is now in an unspecified state and accessing it is a logic bug.
Move construction is not allowed for strings.
The 'std::move' function returns a constant reference.
Strings are always copied when using 'std::move'.

explanation: After 'std::string s2 = std::move(s1);', 's1' is 'valid but unspecified'. In most implementations, it is empty, but relying on it to be empty without checking is a bug.

description: What is the issue with this 'std::string' used as a buffer for 'recv'?

options.text: Writing directly to the buffer via '&s[0]' is only safe if you first 'resize()' the string to the expected buffer length; 'reserve()' is insufficient.
Strings cannot be used as network buffers.
The pointer must be 'const'.
Recv only accepts 'void**'.

explanation: 'reserve()' changes capacity, not size. Writing to '&s[0]' for 100 bytes when 'size()' is 0 is out-of-bounds access, even if 'capacity()' is 1000. C++ functions like 'size()' will still report 0 after the write.
code:
s.reserve(100);
recv(sock, &s[0], 100, 0);

description: Identify the bug in this use of 'std::string::insert' with a self-reference.

options.text: If 'insert' triggers a reallocation, the reference to 's[0]' becomes a dangling pointer before the insertion is performed.
You cannot insert a character at index 0.
Strings don't support inserting single characters.
The index must be a signed integer.

explanation: If 's' reallocates, 's[0]' (the character being inserted) is moved to a new memory location. The reference passed to 'insert' is now invalid.
code: s.insert(5, 1, s[0]);

description: Why is 'std::string::operator+' inside a loop for building a string considered a bug in large-scale systems?

options.text: It creates a new temporary string and copies the existing content in every iteration, resulting in O(N^2) time complexity.
It causes memory fragmentation that can't be cleaned up.
The '+' operator is not thread-safe.
It prevents the compiler from using SSO.

explanation: For N strings of length M, using '+' repeatedly takes $O(N^2 cdot M)$ time. Using 'std::string::append' or 'stringstream' takes $O(N cdot M)$.
code: for(auto& x : logs) report = report + x + "\n";

description: Identify the bug in this 'std::string' to 'bool' conversion.

options.text: Strings do not have an implicit conversion to bool; this code will not compile unless you use '!s.empty()'.
An empty string is always 'true' in C++.
It checks the pointer address of the string object.
Strings are only 'false' if they contain the word 'false'.

explanation: Unlike pointers or integers, 'std::string' cannot be used directly in an 'if' statement. You must call a method or use a comparison.
code:
std::string s = "data";
if (s) { ... }

description: What is the issue with using 'std::string::front()' on a string that was just moved from?

options.text: A moved-from string is usually empty; calling 'front()' on an empty container is undefined behavior.
Front() returns a constant pointer.
The character is replaced by 'X'.
Move makes all methods private.

explanation: Accessing elements of a moved-from string is unsafe because its size is likely zero.
code:
std::string s2 = std::move(s1);
char c = s1.front();

description: Identify the bug in this 'std::string::substr' call with a large index.

options.text: If the starting index is greater than 's.size()', the method throws 'std::out_of_range'. It does not return an empty string or clip to the end.
It returns the entire string instead.
The index must be a negative value to count from the end.
It returns 'std::string::npos'.

explanation: Substring has strict bounds checking for the start index. However, the length parameter *is* clipped if it exceeds the remaining characters.
code: s.substr(100, 5); // s.size() is 50

description: Why does 'std::string::pop_back' not return the character it removes?

options.text: To maintain strong exception safety; returning the object by value could throw an exception (though not for char), so the STL separates the 'back()' and 'pop_back()' operations.
It is a performance optimization for C-compatibility.
Strings are immutable in C++.
It is only used for clearing the string.

explanation: This is a design choice consistent across all STL containers (vector, list, etc.). You must call 'back()' to get the value before calling 'pop_back()'.
code: char c = s.pop_back(); // Compilation Error

description: Identify the bug in this function meant to traverse and print a linked list.

options.text: The loop condition should be 'while (temp->next != nullptr)'.
The 'temp' pointer is never updated, causing an infinite loop that prints the head value forever.
The 'temp' pointer must be deleted at the end of the function.
You cannot initialize a pointer to 'head'.

explanation: To move through a linked list, you must update the iterator pointer. Without 'temp = temp->next;', the pointer stays on the first node indefinitely.

description: Why will this code crash when the list is empty?

options.text: The 'temp' pointer is not constant.
The code fails to check if 'head' is nullptr before dereferencing it to access 'next', leading to a segmentation fault.

description: Why does this 'getNodeCountAtLevel' function fail?

options.text: It decrements 'level' but never checks if 'level == 0' to return 1; it keeps going until it hits null pointers.
Levels must be calculated using BFS.
The return type should be 'bool'.
You cannot count nodes at a level using recursion.

explanation: When 'level' reaches 0, it means we have reached the target depth. The function should return 1 at that point. Without that check, it just returns 0 + 0 + ... when it hits nulls.

description: Identify the bug in this function that checks if a Binary Tree is a valid Binary Search Tree (BST).

options.text: The function only checks if the current node's value is between its immediate children, but it fails to check if all nodes in the left subtree are smaller than the root.
The function returns true for null pointers, which is incorrect as null trees are not BSTs.
The return statement should use '||' instead of '&&'.
The recursive calls are missing the 'root->data' parameter.

explanation: A valid BST requires all nodes in the left subtree to be less than the root and all nodes in the right subtree to be greater. This code only performs a local check on the immediate children, which is insufficient.

description: Why does this 'Lowest Common Ancestor' (LCA) function for a BST fail?

options.text: It finds the ancestor of the values, but it doesn't check if the values actually exist in the tree.
The conditions are swapped: it goes right when both values are smaller than the root.
LCA cannot be found recursively in a BST.
The function returns the leaf node instead of the ancestor.

explanation: In a BST, if both 'p' and 'q' are smaller than the root, the LCA must be in the left subtree. If both are larger, it's in the right. This code incorrectly moves right when the values are smaller.

description: Identify the bug in this function meant to delete the entire tree from memory.

options.text: It uses Pre-order deletion, which causes a 'use-after-free' error when trying to access root->left and root->right after the root is deleted.
The base case should return 0.
Nodes must be set to nullptr before calling delete.
delete cannot be used inside a recursive function.

explanation: To safely delete a tree, you must use Post-order traversal (Left, Right, then Root). By deleting the root first, the pointers to the children are lost or become invalid.

description: What is the issue with this Level-order traversal using a Queue?

options.text: It fails to check if the root is null before pushing to the queue, causing a crash.
The queue should be a priority_queue to maintain level order.
It pushes the children in the wrong order (Right then Left).
The while loop condition is backwards.

description: Identify the bug in this code that finds the Diameter of a Binary Tree.

options.text: The diameter calculation is correct, but the helper function returns diameter instead of height, causing the recursive height calculations to be wrong.
Diameter is always 'height * 2'.
The function returns height instead of diameter.
The '+ 1' for the root is missing in the diameter comparison.

explanation: In a standard Diameter implementation, the recursive helper must return the *height* of the subtree so that the parent can calculate the diameter passing through it. This code is returning diameter, which breaks the logic.

description: Why does this function fail to correctly mirror a binary tree?

options.text: The temporary variable 'temp' is a pointer that gets updated, causing the left child to be overwritten by the right child twice.
Mirroring can only be done in-place using a stack.
It swaps the values of the nodes instead of the pointers.
Actually, the code is missing recursive calls to mirror the subtrees.

explanation: Swapping the left and right children of the root is only the first step. To mirror the entire tree, you must recursively call the mirror function on the left and right subtrees.

description: Identify the bug in this function that checks if a Binary Tree is 'Height-Balanced'.

options.text: The function checks the root's balance but fails to check if the left and right subtrees are also balanced.
The height difference allowed is 0, which is only for perfect trees.
It uses '&&' when it should use '||'.
Height-balanced check is only for BSTs.

explanation: A tree is balanced only if |height(left) - height(right)| <= 1 AND the left subtree is balanced AND the right subtree is balanced. This code only checks the first condition.

description: What is the issue with this 'In-order Successor' finding function in a BST?

options.text: If the target node has a right child, the successor is the leftmost node in that right subtree, but this code goes to the rightmost node.
The function returns the parent instead of the successor.
Successors can only be found in threaded binary trees.
The 'while' loop condition is reversed.

explanation: The in-order successor of a node with a right subtree is the node with the minimum value in that right subtree (the leftmost node). This code goes to the rightmost node, which is the maximum.

description: Identify the bug in this 'Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List' (in-place) logic.

options.text: The right subtree is lost because 'root->right = root->left' overwrites the pointer before it is saved or re-attached.
Flattening must follow In-order traversal.
Linked lists cannot be created from trees without extra memory.
The 'left' pointer is not set to nullptr, creating a non-linear structure.

explanation: To flatten the tree, you must attach the right subtree to the end of the flattened left subtree. This code simply overwrites 'root->right', losing the entire original right subtree.

description: Why does this 'Count Nodes at Distance K' function print the wrong values?

options.text: The function should decrement K until it reaches 0, but it never checks for K == 0 to print the node.
Distance K refers to levels, not nodes.
The base case returns after the first node at any level.
The distance K is relative to the leaves, not the root.

explanation: To find nodes at distance K from the root, you recurse and decrement K. When K reaches 0, you've reached the target level. This code lacks the 'if (K == 0)' print condition.

description: Identify the bug in this BST 'delete' function (case: node with two children).

options.text: It fails to recursively delete the successor from the right subtree after copying its value, leaving a duplicate in the tree.
Successors must be found in the left subtree instead.
The root cannot be deleted if it has two children.
Memory is not freed for the successor node.

explanation: When deleting a node with two children, you copy the successor's value to the current node. However, you must then call the delete function on the right subtree to remove that successor node from its original position.

description: What is the bug in this 'Path Sum' function (checking if a path from root to leaf equals target)?

options.text: It returns true for any node where the sum matches, even if it's not a leaf node.
The target sum should be increased in each recursive call.
It ignores the right subtree if the left subtree returns false.
The function fails if the path sum is negative.

explanation: A 'Path Sum' must end at a leaf node. This code only checks if the current node's data matches the remaining sum, but it doesn't verify if 'root->left' and 'root->right' are both null.

description: Identify the bug in this function designed to find the maximum sum of a path from root to leaf.

options.text: The function returns 'INT_MIN' for null pointers, which will corrupt the sum calculation for nodes with only one child.
The sum should be calculated using Level-order traversal.
It should use 'std::min' instead of 'std::max'.
The root's data is added multiple times.

explanation: If a node has only one child (e.g., Left), 'maxPath(root->right)' returns 'INT_MIN'. Adding 'root->data' to 'INT_MIN' results in an underflow or a very small number, causing the function to ignore valid paths.

description: Why does this 'Populate Next Right Pointers' function fail?

options.text: It only connects children of the same parent; it fails to connect the right child of one node to the left child of its sibling.
Next pointers are only for doubly linked lists.
The 'next' pointer is private and cannot be accessed.
The function must be iterative.

explanation: This code correctly sets 'root->left->next = root->right'. However, it doesn't handle the gap between siblings (e.g., 'root->right->next = root->next->left').

description: Identify the bug in this 'Is Symmetry' check function.

options.text: The recursive calls compare 'left->left' with 'right->left', but symmetry requires comparing 'left->left' with 'right->right' (mirror image).
Symmetry check requires In-order traversal.
It fails if the tree is an odd height.
The root's value must be checked twice.

explanation: A tree is symmetric if the left subtree is a mirror of the right subtree. This means 'left->left' must be compared to 'right->right', and 'left->right' to 'right->left'.

description: What is the bug in this 'Tree to Circular Doubly Linked List' conversion?

options.text: The 'head' and 'tail' logic is missing the final step of connecting the last node back to the first node to make it circular.
Binary trees cannot be converted to linked lists in-place.
The 'left' pointer should represent the 'next' element.
It uses a dummy node which causes a memory leak.

explanation: Standard tree-to-list functions often create the linear structure using In-order traversal. However, to make it a *circular* doubly linked list, you must explicitly set 'tail->right = head' and 'head->left = tail'.

description: Identify the bug in this function that checks if a Binary Tree is a 'Sum Tree'.

options.text: The function returns true if the current node satisfies the sum property, but it fails to check if all descendant nodes also satisfy the Sum Tree property.
A Sum Tree must only contain positive values.
The sum should include the parent's value.
Leaf nodes are not considered Sum Trees.

explanation: A Sum Tree is a tree where every node (except leaves) is the sum of its left and right subtrees. This code only checks the root level and forgets to recursively validate 'isSumTree(root->left)' and 'isSumTree(root->right)'.

description: Why does this 'Find Node in a Tree' function sometimes return nullptr even when the value exists?

options.text: The result of the left recursive search is returned immediately without checking if it's null, preventing the function from ever searching the right subtree if the left search fails.
It only works for balanced trees.
The comparison 'root->data == val' is only checked for the root.
The function should use Level-order traversal for searching.

explanation: In a general binary tree (not BST), you must search both subtrees. This code searches the left, and regardless of whether it finds the node or returns nullptr, it returns that result, skipping the right subtree.

description: Identify the bug in this function meant to print the 'Boundary' of a Binary Tree.

options.text: The code prints the left boundary and leaves correctly, but it will print leaf nodes twice if they are part of the left or right boundary.
Boundary traversal only works for BSTs.
The root node is printed three times.
The right boundary is printed in the wrong order (top to bottom).

explanation: Standard boundary traversal involves printing the left boundary, then all leaves, then the right boundary. If a node is both a leaf and a boundary node (like the bottom-left node), these separate functions will both print it.

description: What is the issue with this 'Serialize Binary Tree' function?

options.text: It uses '#' for null pointers but fails to provide a delimiter between node values, making it impossible to distinguish between '12, null' and '1, 2, null' during deserialization.
Serialization only works for complete binary trees.
It uses Pre-order, but only Post-order is serializable.
The string should be encoded in binary.

explanation: If nodes are '1' and '2', the output is '12#'. If a node is '12', the output is '12#'. Without a delimiter (like a comma), the deserializer cannot know if '12' is one node or two.

description: Identify the bug in this In-order traversal using a Stack (Iterative).

options.text: The 'curr' pointer is not updated to 'curr->right' after popping a node, leading to an infinite loop or missing nodes.
The stack should be initialized with 'root->left'.
It visits nodes in Pre-order because it prints before pushing the left child.
The while loop condition should be 'curr != nullptr'.

description: Why does this 'Check if Subtree' function fail?

options.text: It uses '==' to compare the roots, but it should use a helper function to verify if the entire structure and values of the subtrees are identical.
Subtrees can only be found in the left branch.
The comparison should be based on node addresses.
The function returns false if the root data doesn't match, skipping other possible subtree matches.

explanation: Comparing 'root == subRoot' only checks if they point to the same memory or (if overloaded) have the same root data. You must call 'isIdentical(root, subRoot)' to check the whole structure.

description: Identify the bug in this function meant to calculate the 'Maximum Width' of a binary tree.

options.text: It counts only the non-null nodes at each level, but the 'width' of a binary tree includes the null gaps between the leftmost and rightmost non-null nodes.
Width cannot be found using Level-order traversal.
The function returns the height instead of the width.
It doesn't handle negative node values.

explanation: In many coding platforms (like LeetCode), the 'Width' of a level is the number of nodes between the first and last non-null nodes, including nulls in between. This code just counts nodes present, which is 'Level Size'.

It returns the child instead of the root when both left and right calls are non-null.
The base case is wrong for single-node trees.

description: Identify the bug in this 'Post-order traversal' using two Stacks.

Two stacks are only for In-order traversal.
The first stack should be a queue.
The root is printed twice.

description: Why does this 'Check if Two Trees are Mirrored' function fail?

options.text: The recursive logic compares 'p->left' with 'q->left', which checks for identity, not mirror symmetry (which requires 'p->left' vs 'q->right').
Mirroring requires the data values to be negative.
The pointers 'p' and 'q' are compared instead of their data.
It fails if the trees have different heights.

description: Identify the bug in this 'Kth Smallest Element in BST' using a counter.

The function should use Pre-order traversal.

description: What is the bug in this 'Tree Path Sums' function (returning all paths as a list)?

Strings are too slow for path storage.

The leaf condition is missing.

description: Identify the bug in this 'Sorted Array to BST' conversion.

The mid calculation should be '(left + right) / 2'.
BSTs cannot be built from sorted arrays.

description: Identify true statements about Binary Tree storage.

If target > root, search only the right subtree.
Average search time is O(log N).

description: Which of the following are true regarding 'Empty' trees?

options.text: A tree with no nodes is an empty tree.
The root pointer of an empty tree is NULL.
An empty tree has height -1 (in many conventions).
An empty tree is still considered a valid tree structure.

description: Analyze the following recursive code for calculating the diameter of a binary tree. Which statements are true?

options.text: The diameter is the number of nodes on the longest path between any two leaves.
This specific implementation has a time complexity of O(N^2) because it calls height() inside diameter().
The longest path must always pass through the root node.
An O(N) solution exists by calculating height and diameter in a single bottom-up pass.

explanation: Diameter is the longest path between any two nodes. In a naive implementation, recalculating height at every node leads to quadratic time. Optimal solutions compute height and diameter simultaneously.

description: Regarding 'Threaded Binary Trees', which statements are correct?

options.text: They use NULL pointers to store 'threads' to inorder predecessors or successors.
They allow for inorder traversal without using a stack or recursion.
A 'Right Threaded' tree points NULL right pointers to the inorder successor.
They require an additional boolean flag in each node to distinguish between a child and a thread.

explanation: Threaded trees optimize traversal by utilizing wasted NULL space to point to the next/previous nodes in a specific traversal order (usually inorder).

description: Which are true about 'AVL Trees' and their balancing mechanism?

options.text: The balance factor of any node must be -1, 0, or 1.
A 'Left-Right' (LR) imbalance is fixed using a single rotation.
Insertion and Deletion are both O(log N) in the worst case.
AVL trees are more strictly balanced than Red-Black trees.

explanation: AVL trees maintain a height difference limit of 1. Complex imbalances (LR or RL) require double rotations. They provide faster lookups than Red-Black trees due to stricter balancing.

description: What are true regarding the conversion of a 'Binary Tree' to its 'Mirror Image'?

options.text: It involves swapping the left and right child pointers for every node.
A post-order traversal can be used to perform the swap from bottom-up.
The inorder traversal of a mirrored BST is sorted in descending order.
The mirroring process changes the total number of nodes in the tree.

explanation: Mirroring swaps subtrees. For a BST, which is normally sorted ascending via inorder traversal, mirroring reverses this relationship to descending.

description: Which statements about 'Red-Black Trees' are true?

options.text: The root and all leaves (NIL) are always black.
A red node cannot have a red child.
The path from root to any leaf must have the same number of black nodes.
They are typically used to implement 'std::map' and 'std::set' in C++.

explanation: Red-Black trees are self-balancing BSTs that use color rules to ensure the tree height remains logarithmic, even if less perfectly balanced than AVL trees.

description: In the context of 'Heaps' (represented as arrays), which are true for a Max-Heap?

options.text: The value of a parent is always greater than or equal to its children.
It is always a complete binary tree.
The maximum element is always at index 0 (if 0-indexed).
A Max-Heap is a sorted array.

explanation: Heaps maintain a partial order. While the max is at the root, the children themselves are not necessarily sorted relative to each other.

description: Identify true statements about 'Tree Construction' from traversal sequences.

options.text: A unique binary tree can be constructed from Inorder and Preorder.
A unique binary tree can be constructed from Inorder and Postorder.
A unique binary tree can be constructed from Preorder and Postorder.
For a BST, only the Preorder sequence is sufficient to reconstruct the tree.

explanation: Inorder is usually necessary to distinguish left from right subtrees. However, for a BST, the inorder is simply the sorted preorder, so preorder alone suffices.

description: Regarding 'Lowest Common Ancestor' (LCA) in a BST, which logic is correct?

options.text: If both nodes are smaller than root, LCA is in the left subtree.
If both nodes are larger than root, LCA is in the right subtree.
If one is smaller and one is larger, the root is the LCA.
LCA must be one of the two input nodes if one is an ancestor of the other.

explanation: LCA in a BST is the first node whose value lies between the two target values during a top-down search.
code: if (n1 < root->val && n2 < root->val) return findLCA(root->left, n1, n2);

description: What are true about 'Morris Traversal'?

options.text: It performs inorder traversal in O(N) time.
It uses O(1) extra space.
It temporarily modifies the tree by creating threads.
It is safer for multi-threaded environments than standard recursion.

explanation: Morris traversal uses 'threads' (pointing right-child NULLs to inorder successors) to traverse without a stack, achieving constant space. It restores the tree to its original state before finishing.

description: Identify true statements about 'Balanced Binary Trees'.

options.text: An AVL tree is a balanced binary tree.
The height of a balanced tree with N nodes is O(log N).
Balance is defined by the height difference between left and right subtrees.
A complete binary tree is always balanced.

explanation: Balanced trees prevent the O(N) worst-case performance of skewed trees by keeping subtrees roughly equal in height.

description: Regarding 'B-Trees', which statements are correct?

options.text: They are used heavily in database indexing and file systems.
Each node can contain multiple keys and more than two children.
All leaf nodes must be at the same depth.
They are specifically designed to minimize disk I/O.

explanation: B-Trees are broad and shallow, making them perfect for storage systems where reading a large block (node) is efficient but seeking many different locations is slow.

description: Analyze the 'Delete' operation in a BST. Which scenarios are true?

options.text: If the node is a leaf, it can be removed immediately.
If the node has one child, the child replaces the node.
If the node has two children, it is replaced by its inorder successor or predecessor.
The inorder successor of a node with a right child is the leftmost node in that right subtree.

explanation: Deleting a node with two children requires finding a 'replacement' value that maintains the BST property, which is always the inorder successor or predecessor.

description: Which are true about 'Trie' (Prefix Tree) data structures?

options.text: They are used for efficient string searching and autocomplete.
The path from root to node represents a prefix of a word.
Search time depends on the length of the string, not the number of strings.
They consume more memory than a hash map in many cases.

explanation: Tries offer O(L) search time where L is word length. They store common prefixes only once, but each node requires a large array/map for potential child characters.

description: In the following 'Level Order' implementation, why is 'size' calculated inside the loop?

options.text: To process the tree level by level.
To distinguish between nodes of different levels.
Without it, the loop would just be a generic BFS.
To allow adding level-specific markers (like newline).

description: Regarding 'Expression Trees', which are true?

options.text: Leaves are operands (numbers/variables).
Internal nodes are operators (+, -, , /).
A post-order traversal yields the Postfix expression.
An in-order traversal (with parentheses) yields the Infix expression.

explanation: Expression trees represent algebraic structures. Traversal order determines the notation (Pre-order = Prefix, Post-order = Postfix).

description: Identify true statements about 'Full' vs 'Complete' vs 'Perfect' Binary Trees.

options.text: Every Perfect tree is a Full tree.
Every Perfect tree is a Complete tree.
Every Complete tree is a Full tree.
Every Full tree is a Complete tree.

explanation: Perfect is the strictest (all levels full). Complete allows the last level to be partially filled (left-aligned). Full just requires 0 or 2 children. A complete tree might have a node with 1 child, failing the 'Full' requirement.

description: What is true about 'Binary Search Tree' (BST) validation?

options.text: A node must be greater than its left child and smaller than its right child.
A node must be within a range (min, max) defined by its ancestors.
Checking only immediate children is insufficient.
In-order traversal should produce a strictly increasing sequence.

explanation: A node must be larger than everything in its left subtree, not just the root of that subtree. Passing ranges (min, max) down recursively is the standard validation check.

description: Regarding 'Segment Trees', which statements are correct?

options.text: They are used for range queries (Sum, Min, Max) in O(log N).
They allow for point updates in O(log N).
They are typically represented using an array of size 4N.
They are only useful for static data that never changes.

explanation: Segment trees are dynamic structures that handle both range queries and updates efficiently. 4N is the safe upper bound for the array size given N elements.

description: What are true about 'Splay Trees'?

options.text: They are self-adjusting binary search trees.
Recently accessed elements are moved to the root via 'splaying'.
Worst-case time complexity for a single operation is O(log N).
Amortized time complexity for operations is O(log N).

explanation: Splay trees don't guarantee balance for every operation (worst case O(N)), but they guarantee that a sequence of operations averages out to logarithmic time.

description: Which are true regarding the 'Number of Binary Trees' that can be formed with N nodes?

options.text: The number of unique structures is given by the N-th Catalan Number.
For N=3, there are 5 unique structures.
The formula is (2n)! / ((n+1)! * n!).
If nodes are labeled, the number of trees is higher.

explanation: Catalan numbers define the count of unlabeled binary tree shapes. If nodes have specific labels (e.g., 1, 2, 3), each shape can be filled in N! ways.

description: Regarding 'Heapsort' and tree structures, which are true?

options.text: Build-Heap takes O(N) time.
Each extraction (pop) takes O(log N) time.
Total sorting time is O(N log N).
It is an in-place sorting algorithm.

explanation: Heapsort builds a heap in linear time and then extracts elements one by one, swapping the root with the last leaf to maintain the array in-place.

description: Identify correct statements about 'Fenwick Trees' (Binary Indexed Trees).

options.text: They are used primarily for prefix sum queries and point updates.
They use less memory than Segment Trees.
Both query and update are O(log N).
They are easier to implement than Segment Trees.

explanation: BITs use an array of the same size as the data (N) and clever bit manipulation to handle prefix sums, making them more memory efficient than Segment Trees.

description: What are true about 'Huffman Coding' trees?

options.text: They are used for lossless data compression.
More frequent characters are closer to the root.
It is an optimal prefix code.
The tree is built using a Min-Priority Queue.

explanation: Huffman builds a tree from the bottom up by merging the two least frequent nodes. This ensures the most frequent characters have the shortest bit-paths.

description: Which statements are true about 'Self-Balancing BSTs'?

options.text: They ensure O(log N) operations even in the worst case.
They perform rotations during insertion and deletion.
AVL, Red-Black, and Treaps are examples.
They require additional metadata (height, color, or priority) in each node.

explanation: To maintain balance, these trees store extra info to trigger rotations when the tree structure becomes too lopsided.

description: Regarding 'Binary Tree Vertical Order' traversal, which are true?

options.text: It requires assigning a horizontal distance (HD) to each node.
Nodes with the same HD are printed in the same vertical line.
Left child has HD = parentHD - 1; Right child has HD = parentHD + 1.
It can be implemented using a Map and a Queue.

explanation: Vertical order treats the tree as a grid. By tracking the horizontal offset from the root, we can group nodes that overlap vertically.
code:
map<int, vector<int>> hdMap;
// root HD = 0

description: What happens during a 'Left Rotation' in a balanced tree?

options.text: The right child of the pivot becomes the new parent.
The original parent becomes the left child of the new parent.
The left subtree of the right child is moved to the parent's right child.
Height of the right subtree is reduced.

explanation: Rotations shift nodes up and down to rebalance height. A left rotation 'pulls up' the right side of the tree.
code:
x->right = y->left;
y->left = x;

description: Identify true statements about 'Binary Tree Views'.

options.text: Top view consists of the first node encountered for each HD.
Bottom view consists of the last node encountered for each HD.
Right view is the last node of each level in a level-order traversal.
Left view is the first node of each level.

explanation: Views represent what is 'visible' from a certain perspective. Vertical order logic (HD) is used for top/bottom views, while level-order logic is used for left/right views.

description: Regarding 'K-th Smallest Element' in a BST, which are true?

options.text: It can be found using an in-order traversal.
The K-th node visited during in-order traversal is the result.
It takes O(N) in the worst case.
If nodes store subtree sizes, it can be found in O(Height).

explanation: In-order traversal visits nodes in ascending order. By keeping a counter, we find K. If augmented with subtree sizes, we can skip subtrees to find it in logarithmic time.

description: What are true about 'Threaded Binary Tree' deletion?

options.text: It is more complex than standard binary tree deletion.
Threads must be updated to maintain the inorder predecessor/successor links.
It may require identifying whether a link is a thread or a child before removal.
It requires O(1) space.

explanation: Deletion involves reconnecting both standard child pointers and the threads that 'point back' to the ancestor being removed.

description: Regarding 'M-way Search Trees', which are true?

options.text: A node can have up to M children.
A node can store up to M-1 keys.
Binary search trees are a special case where M=2.
They allow for faster branching and less height than binary trees.

explanation: M-way trees generalize the BST. By allowing more keys per node, the tree becomes wider but much shorter, reducing the number of steps to find a leaf.

description: Analyze the provided AVL tree rotation logic. Which statements are true regarding the preservation of tree properties during a 'Double Rotation'?

options.text: A Left-Right (LR) rotation is performed when a node is inserted into the right subtree of a left child.
An LR rotation consists of a Left Rotation on the child followed by a Right Rotation on the parent.
A double rotation is required when the insertion happens in an 'inner' grandchild's subtree.
After a successful LR rotation, the new root of the subtree is the node that was previously the left child.

explanation: Double rotations (LR and RL) handle 'zig-zag' imbalances. For LR, the left child's right subtree is 'heavy'. We first rotate the child left to make it 'left-heavy', then rotate the parent right to balance.

description: Regarding the 'Van Emde Boas' tree (vEB) data structure, which statements are correct?

options.text: It supports operations like successor and predecessor in O(log log U) time, where U is the universe size.
The space complexity is O(U), which can be quite large.
It requires the keys to be integers within a fixed range [0, U-1].
It is based on a recursive decomposition of the universe into sqrt(U) clusters.

explanation: vEB trees are extremely fast for bounded integer sets. They work by splitting bits of the key to index into clusters of clusters, resulting in double-logarithmic time complexity.

description: Analyze the following snippet for 'Morris Inorder Traversal'. Which statements are true about its state during execution?

options.text: The 'thread' is created from the inorder predecessor's right pointer to the current node.
If 'pre->right' is already equal to 'curr', it indicates the left subtree has been fully processed.
It achieves O(1) space by temporarily violating the acyclic property of the tree.
The algorithm restores all NULL pointers before termination.

explanation: Morris traversal finds the 'inorder predecessor' (rightmost node in the left subtree) and uses its empty right pointer to store a return path. This creates a temporary cycle that is removed once the node is visited.

description: Regarding 'Suffix Trees' and their construction via Ukkonen's Algorithm, which are true?

options.text: Ukkonen's algorithm builds the tree in O(N) time for a string of length N.
It utilizes 'suffix links' to jump between nodes during construction.
A suffix tree of a string contains all its suffixes as paths from root to leaves.
The space complexity of a suffix tree is O(N^2) in the worst case.

explanation: Ukkonen's is a linear-time, online algorithm. By using clever edge-labeling (storing indices instead of substrings) and suffix links, it reduces space complexity to O(N).

description: In a 'B+ Tree' compared to a standard 'B-Tree', which statements regarding structure and efficiency are true?

options.text: Internal nodes in B+ trees only store keys, while B-trees store both keys and data pointers.
B+ trees have all leaf nodes connected via a linked list for efficient range scans.
All search keys are duplicated in the leaf nodes of a B+ tree.
B-trees generally provide faster search for keys found near the root.

explanation: B+ trees are optimized for block storage. Because internal nodes are smaller (no data), more keys fit in a block, leading to higher fan-out. The linked leaves make range queries very fast.

description: Consider 'Cartesian Trees'. Which properties are true?

options.text: It is a binary tree derived from a sequence of numbers.
It maintains the heap property (e.g., parent is min/max of children).
An in-order traversal of the tree recovers the original sequence.
They are used in O(1) Range Minimum Query (RMQ) preprocessing.

explanation: A Cartesian tree is a hybrid: the keys follow the sequence order (inorder), while the values follow the heap property. They link RMQ problems to Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA) problems.

description: Regarding '2-3-4 Trees' and their relationship to Red-Black Trees, which are true?

options.text: A 2-3-4 tree can be mapped directly to a Red-Black tree.
A 3-node (2 keys, 3 children) is represented by a black parent and one red child.
A 4-node (3 keys, 4 children) is represented by a black parent and two red children.
Both structures maintain perfect black balance (all paths from root to NIL have same black nodes).

explanation: Red-Black trees are binary representations of 2-3-4 trees. Colors represent the grouping of nodes in the 2-3-4 structure. This mapping is why RB-trees are balanced.

description: Analyze the 'Heavy-Light Decomposition' (HLD) of a tree. Which statements are true?

options.text: It partitions the edges of a tree into disjoint paths (heavy paths).
A path from root to any node crosses at most O(log N) light edges.
It allows for path queries and updates in O(log^2 N) using Segment Trees.
A 'heavy' edge is the one pointing to the child with the largest subtree.

explanation: HLD is an advanced technique to handle tree paths. By organizing heavy paths into segments, complex path operations are reduced to a logarithmic number of segment tree operations.

description: What is true about 'Link-Cut Trees'?

options.text: They support dynamic connectivity in a forest of trees.
The primary operations are 'link(u, v)' and 'cut(v)'.
Operations take amortized O(log N) time.
They are internally implemented using Splay Trees (preferred paths).

explanation: Link-Cut trees maintain a forest of trees where edges can be added or removed. They use Splay trees to represent 'preferred paths', enabling logarithmic dynamic updates.

description: Regarding 'Centroid Decomposition' of a tree, which statements are true?

options.text: A centroid is a node whose removal splits the tree into components of size <= N/2.
Every tree has at least one and at most two centroids.
Recursive decomposition results in a 'Centroid Tree' of height O(log N).
It is used to solve divide-and-conquer problems on paths in O(N log N).

explanation: Centroid decomposition is a powerful tool for tree-based path problems. By repeatedly finding centroids, we build a hierarchy that allows us to process paths effectively.

description: Analyze the complexity of 'Building a Fenwick Tree' (BIT). Which are true?

options.text: Calling 'update' N times results in O(N log N) construction.
It can be built in O(N) time using a linear pass.
The O(N) construction involves adding the current element to its immediate parent.
A BIT can only be built from an array of non-negative integers.

explanation: While N updates take O(N log N), a linear-time construction is possible by propagating prefix sums to the parent index i + (i & -i) for each node.

description: Regarding 'K-D Trees' (K-Dimensional), which statements are correct?

options.text: They are space-partitioning trees for organizing points in K-dimensional space.
Each level cycles through the dimensions used to split the data (e.g., x, then y, then x).
Nearest neighbor search has an average complexity of O(log N).
In the worst case, searching for the nearest neighbor can be O(N).

explanation: K-D trees extend binary trees to multidimensional space. While average search is efficient, high dimensionality (curse of dimensionality) can lead to linear search time.

description: What are true about 'Splay Tree' amortized analysis via the Potential Method?

options.text: The potential function is often defined as the sum of logs of subtree sizes (ranks).
Individual operations like Zig, Zig-Zig, and Zig-Zag are analyzed separately.
The amortized cost of an 'access' operation is O(log N).
Splay trees are 'statically optimal' for any distribution of accesses.

explanation: The analysis uses node ranks to prove that even though one splay might be O(N), the net potential decrease keeps the average cost at O(log N). They are also conjectured to be 'dynamically optimal'.

description: Regarding 'Treaps' (Tree + Heap), which properties are true?

options.text: Nodes have both a key (for BST order) and a priority (for Heap order).
Priorities are typically assigned randomly to keep the tree balanced.
The expected height of a Treap is O(log N).
A Treap is uniquely determined by its keys and priorities.

explanation: Treaps use random priorities to mimic the structure of a tree built by inserting keys in a random order, providing probabilistic balance without complex rotation logic.

description: Identify true statements about 'Binary Tree Serialization' and deserialization.

options.text: Pre-order traversal with 'NULL' markers is sufficient for any binary tree.
Post-order traversal with 'NULL' markers is also sufficient.
Without 'NULL' markers, both Inorder and (Pre/Post)order are needed.
Level-order serialization is easier to implement iteratively using a Queue.

explanation: Serialization preserves structure. Markers (like # or NULL) for empty children allow a single traversal to represent the full tree structure unambiguously.

description: Regarding 'Ternary Search Trees' (TST) vs Tries, which statements are true?

options.text: TST nodes have 3 children: low, high, and equal.
TSTs are more space-efficient than standard Tries when the alphabet is large.
Searching in a TST is O(L + log N) where L is string length.
TSTs maintain BST order for characters at the same position.

explanation: TSTs combine the search efficiency of Tries with the space efficiency of BSTs. Instead of 26 pointers, they use 3, splitting based on whether the current character is less, equal, or greater than the node's character.

description: What are the true statements about 'AA Trees'?

options.text: They are a variation of Red-Black trees that simplify the balancing logic.
They only allow right-leaning red nodes (represented as level increments).
Balancing is done using two simple operations: Skew and Split.
The height is guaranteed to be O(log N).

explanation: Arne Andersson (AA) trees eliminate many of the cases found in Red-Black trees by enforcing that red nodes can only be right children, resulting in simpler code for Skew (right rotation) and Split (left rotation).

description: Regarding 'Segment Tree' with Lazy Propagation, which statements are true?

options.text: It allows range updates (e.g., add X to [L, R]) in O(log N).
Update info is stored in a 'lazy' array and pushed down only when needed.
Without lazy propagation, range updates would take O(N) time.
It can handle non-commutative operations if handled carefully.

explanation: Lazy propagation avoids updating all leaves. Instead, it marks a node as 'needing update' and defers the work until a search actually reaches that node's subtree.

description: Analyze 'Palindromic Trees' (Eertree). Which are true?

options.text: They are used to find all distinct palindromic substrings in a string.
The tree has two roots: one for even-length and one for odd-length palindromes.
Total nodes are at most N+2 where N is the string length.
Construction time is O(N) amortized.

explanation: Eertrees are specialized for palindromes. The two roots handle the parity of the length. Suffix links and character edges allow for linear time construction.

description: Identify true statements about 'Tree Isomorphism' algorithms.

options.text: Two rooted trees are isomorphic if they can be made identical by reordering children.
Isomorphism for rooted trees can be solved in O(N) using tree hashing/canonical forms.
The algorithm involves recursively encoding the structure of subtrees.
Unrooted tree isomorphism is solved by finding the centroids first.

explanation: Tree isomorphism is easier than general graph isomorphism. By encoding subtrees (e.g., using parentheses or hashes) and sorting them, we can determine if structures are identical.

description: Analyze the 'LCA' (Lowest Common Ancestor) Binary Lifting technique. Which are true?

options.text: It uses a table of size N log N to store 2^k-th ancestors.
Preprocessing takes O(N log N) time.
Each query takes O(log N) time.
It is based on the idea of jumping up the tree in powers of 2.

explanation: Binary lifting is a standard way to solve LCA queries. By pre-calculating jumps of 1, 2, 4, 8... steps, we can move up the tree very quickly to find the meeting point.

description: Regarding 'Interval Trees', which statements are correct?

options.text: They store intervals and allow finding all intervals that overlap with a query point/interval.
The tree is usually a balanced BST where nodes are sorted by the low point of intervals.
Each node also stores the maximum high point in its subtree.
Search time is O(log N + m) where m is the number of results.

explanation: Interval trees augment BSTs with subtree max values. This allows the search to prune branches that cannot possibly contain an overlapping interval.

description: What are true regarding 'Euler Tour Tree' (ETT)?

options.text: It represents a tree as the sequence of nodes visited during a DFS.
It is used for dynamic tree problems (link, cut, path queries).
It is generally more flexible than Link-Cut Trees for subtree queries.
Subtree operations are mapped to contiguous ranges in the Euler sequence.

explanation: ETT maps subtrees to ranges in a sequence. By using a balanced BST (like Splay/Treap) on this sequence, we can perform dynamic tree operations efficiently.

description: In '2D Segment Trees', which statements about complexity are true?

options.text: They are used for range queries on a 2D grid.
Memory complexity is O(N^2) or O(N log^2 N) depending on implementation.
Query time is O(log^2 N).
They can be thought of as a segment tree where each node is another segment tree.

explanation: 2D segment trees extend the concept to rectangles. They are powerful but memory-intensive, as each node in the primary tree (X-dimension) stores a full tree for the Y-dimension.

description: Regarding 'Radix Trees' (Patricia Tries), which are true?

options.text: They are space-optimized tries where edges with only one child are merged.
They reduce the number of internal nodes significantly.
They are commonly used in IP routing tables.
Search time is O(Key Length).

explanation: Radix trees (Practical Algorithm to Retrieve Information Coded in Alphanumeric) compress chains of single-child nodes into single edges, saving memory and potentially reducing search depth.

description: What are true about 'R-Trees'?

options.text: They are used for spatial access methods (geo-data, bounding boxes).
Nodes represent 'Minimum Bounding Rectangles' (MBR) of their children.
They are balanced, similar to B-Trees.
A query point can be contained in multiple sibling MBRs, requiring multiple paths to be searched.

explanation: R-Trees handle multidimensional objects (not just points). Unlike B-trees, branches can overlap spatially, which means a search might not be a single path down the tree.

description: Regarding 'Finger Trees', which statements are correct?

options.text: They provide amortized O(1) access to 'fingers' (usually the ends of the sequence).
They are fundamental to many functional data structures in Haskell.
They are based on a recursive 2-3 tree structure.
They support splitting and merging in O(log N).

explanation: Finger trees are flexible, high-performance functional sequences. By providing fast access to ends, they can efficiently implement deques, priority queues, and more.

description: Identify true statements about 'Scapegoat Trees'.

options.text: They are self-balancing BSTs that do not store any extra info (like height/color) in nodes.
Balance is maintained by rebuilding entire subtrees when they become too deep.
The 'scapegoat' is the highest ancestor that is not weight-balanced.
Worst-case time complexity for search is O(log N).

explanation: Scapegoat trees use the 'brute force' of rebuilding to stay balanced. They only need to know the total size of the tree and a depth limit. While amortized cost is log N, search is always log N due to the depth limit.

description: Regarding the 'M-way Tree' deletion complexity, which are true?

options.text: Deleting a key might trigger a 'merge' or 'redistribution' of keys.
A node is considered 'underflowed' if it has fewer than ceil(M/2) children.
B-Tree deletion is O(log N).
Deletion from a leaf is simpler than from an internal node.

explanation: B-trees (and M-way trees) must maintain occupancy. Deletion is complex because removing a key can cascade balances up the tree, potentially reducing the height if the root empties.

description: What are true about 'Crit-bit Trees'?

options.text: They are a type of compact radix tree (binary trie).
They only store nodes for 'critical bits' where keys in the set differ.
They are highly efficient for variable-length strings and prefixes.
They are immune to certain types of side-channel attacks due to constant structure.

explanation: Crit-bit trees are very space-efficient. They only branch at the bit position where two strings first differ, avoiding redundant nodes and making them ideal for large string sets.

description: Which of the following are the primary components of a Graph data structure?

options.text: Vertices (also called Nodes).
Edges (the links between nodes).
A mandatory Root node.
Arrows for directed graphs.

explanation: A graph consists of a set of vertices and a set of edges. Unlike trees, graphs do not require a root node, though directed graphs use arrows to show edge direction.

description: Regarding 'Undirected Graphs', which statements are true?

options.text: Edges have no specific direction.
If there is an edge between A and B, you can go from A to B and B to A.
The adjacency matrix is always symmetric.
Edges are represented as ordered pairs (u, v).

explanation: In undirected graphs, edges are bidirectional. This symmetry is reflected in the adjacency matrix where matrix[i][j] always equals matrix[j][i].
code:
adj[u][v] = 1;
adj[v][u] = 1;

description: What is true about the 'Adjacency Matrix' representation?

options.text: It uses a 2D array of size V x V.
It is very space-efficient for sparse graphs.
Checking if an edge exists between two nodes is O(1).
Adding a new vertex is an expensive O(V^2) operation.

explanation: Adjacency matrices provide fast edge lookups but waste space ($O(V^2)$) if the graph has very few edges (sparse).
code:
int adj[V][V];
if(adj[i][j] == 1) // Edge exists

description: Identify the characteristics of an 'Adjacency List'.

options.text: It uses an array of lists or vectors.
It is more space-efficient for sparse graphs than a matrix.
Finding all neighbors of a node is efficient.
Checking if a specific edge (u, v) exists takes O(1) always.

explanation: Adjacency lists store only existing edges, saving memory. Finding neighbors is fast, but checking for a specific edge might require traversing a list ($O(degree(u))$).
code:
vector<int> adj[V];
adj[u].push_back(v);

description: Which of these are standard Graph Traversal algorithms?

options.text: Breadth-First Search (BFS)
Depth-First Search (DFS)
In-order Traversal
Level-order Traversal (similar to BFS)

explanation: BFS and DFS are the core ways to navigate a graph. In-order is specific to trees; level-order is essentially BFS applied to a tree.

description: What are true about 'Directed Acyclic Graphs' (DAG)?

options.text: Edges have direction.
They contain no cycles.
They can be used to represent prerequisites or dependencies.
Topological sorting is only possible on DAGs.

explanation: A DAG is a directed graph with no paths that start and end at the same vertex. This property allows for topological ordering.

description: Identify true statements about Breadth-First Search (BFS).

options.text: It uses a Queue data structure.
It explores neighbors level by level.
It finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph.
It uses a Stack data structure.

explanation: BFS radiates outward from a source using a queue to ensure nodes are visited in order of their distance from the start.

description: Identify true statements about Depth-First Search (DFS).

options.text: It can be implemented using recursion.
It uses a Stack (implicitly or explicitly).
It explores as far as possible along a branch before backtracking.
It always finds the shortest path.

explanation: DFS goes deep into the graph. It uses a stack (or recursion stack) but does not guarantee the shortest path like BFS does.

description: What does the 'Degree' of a vertex represent?

options.text: In an undirected graph, the number of edges connected to it.
In a directed graph, the sum of In-degree and Out-degree.
The value stored inside the node.
The number of paths passing through the node.

explanation: Degree counts incident edges. In directed graphs, we distinguish between edges coming in (In-degree) and going out (Out-degree).

description: Which of these describe a 'Connected Graph' (Undirected)?

options.text: There is a path between every pair of vertices.
It consists of only one connected component.
Every node must have an edge to every other node.
Starting a traversal from any node will visit all nodes.

explanation: A graph is connected if you can reach any vertex from any other vertex. It does not need to be 'Complete' (edges between all pairs).

description: Regarding 'Weighted Graphs', which statements are true?

options.text: Each edge has an associated numerical value (weight).
Weights can represent distance, cost, or time.
They can be directed or undirected.
Weights must always be positive.

explanation: Weighted graphs assign values to edges. While many algorithms (like Dijkstra) assume non-negative weights, graphs can technically have negative weights.
code:
struct Edge { int to, weight; };
vector<Edge> adj[V];

description: What is a 'Cycle' in a graph?

options.text: A path that starts and ends at the same vertex.
A path where no edges or vertices (except start/end) are repeated.
A graph with no edges.
A loop containing at least one edge.

explanation: A cycle is a closed path. In simple graphs, it usually refers to a path where only the start and end vertices are the same.

description: Which statements are true about a 'Complete Graph'?

options.text: Every pair of distinct vertices is connected by a unique edge.
A complete graph with n vertices has n(n-1)/2 edges.
It is often denoted as K_n.
It is always a tree.

explanation: Complete graphs have maximum possible connectivity. Because they are full of cycles (unless n < 3), they are generally not trees.

description: When implementing BFS, why is a 'visited' array necessary?

options.text: To prevent infinite loops in graphs with cycles.
To ensure each node is processed exactly once.
To keep track of the distance from the source.
To store the data of the vertices.

explanation: Since graphs can have cycles, without a 'visited' check, the algorithm would keep adding the same nodes to the queue forever.

description: Which are true about 'Self-loops' and 'Multi-edges'?

options.text: A self-loop is an edge from a vertex to itself.
Multi-edges are multiple edges between the same two vertices.
A 'Simple Graph' allows neither self-loops nor multi-edges.
Adjacency matrices can easily represent multi-edges using weights.

explanation: Simple graphs are restricted to one edge between distinct nodes. Multi-graphs allow more complexity. Matrices can store counts in cells to represent multi-edges.

description: In a directed graph, what does 'In-degree' mean?

options.text: The number of edges pointing toward a vertex.
The number of edges pointing away from a vertex.
For a source node, the in-degree is usually 0.
It can be found by summing a column in an adjacency matrix.

explanation: In-degree is incoming traffic. In a matrix [row][col], row is source and col is destination, so summing a column (fixed destination) gives In-degree.

description: Identify true statements about 'Path' and 'Walk' in graph theory.

options.text: A 'Walk' is any sequence of vertices and edges.
A 'Path' is a walk where no vertices are repeated.
Every path is a walk.
A walk must repeat at least one edge.

explanation: A walk is the most general term for moving through a graph. A path is a specific type of walk with no repetitions.

description: What is true about 'Bipartite Graphs'?

options.text: Vertices can be divided into two independent sets.
No two vertices within the same set are connected.
They contain no odd-length cycles.
They can be colored using only 2 colors.

explanation: Bipartite graphs can be split into two groups such that all edges go between groups, never within one. This is equivalent to being 2-colorable.

description: Which are true about 'Disconnected Graphs'?

options.text: They contain at least two vertices with no path between them.
They have multiple connected components.
BFS starting from one node might not visit all nodes.
They are always directed.

explanation: Disconnected graphs have 'islands' of connectivity. A single traversal won't bridge the gap between separate components.

description: In C++, which STL containers are commonly used for adjacency lists?

options.text: vector<vector<int>>
vector<int> adj[V]
list<int> adj[V]
map<int, vector<int>>

explanation: Vectors are preferred for their speed and contiguous memory. Maps are useful when node IDs are non-consecutive or strings.
code: vector<vector<int>> adj(V);

description: What are true about 'Sparse' vs 'Dense' graphs?

options.text: Sparse graphs have few edges relative to the number of vertices.
Dense graphs have many edges, close to V^2.
Adjacency lists are better for sparse graphs.
Adjacency matrices are often preferred for very dense graphs.

explanation: Density refers to how many possible edges actually exist. The choice of data structure depends heavily on this factor for efficiency.

description: Which of the following describes a 'Tree' in graph theory terms?

options.text: A connected graph with no cycles.
A graph with V vertices and V-1 edges that is connected.
Any directed acyclic graph.
A graph where there is exactly one path between any two vertices.

explanation: Trees are a special, minimally connected subset of graphs. A DAG is not necessarily a tree because it could be disconnected or have multiple paths between nodes.

description: Regarding 'Graph Representation' overhead, which are true?

options.text: Matrix: Space is O(V^2).
List: Space is O(V + E).
List: Adding an edge is O(1).
Matrix: Removing a vertex is O(V^2).

explanation: Matrices have fixed size overhead. Lists scale with the number of edges. Removing a vertex from a matrix involves shifting or reallocating the 2D space.

description: Identify true statements about 'Source' and 'Sink' nodes.

options.text: A source node has an In-degree of 0.
A sink node has an Out-degree of 0.
They are only relevant in directed graphs.
Every graph must have at least one source.

explanation: Sources and sinks are direction-based concepts. While finite DAGs must have them, cyclic graphs might not.

description: What happens during 'Backtracking' in DFS?

options.text: The algorithm returns from a recursive call.
It explores other unvisited neighbors of a previous node.
The current node is removed from the active stack.
The 'visited' status of the node is always set back to false.

explanation: Backtracking is the 'going back' phase when a branch is fully explored. In standard DFS traversal, we don't unmark nodes as visited (that is specific to path-finding).

description: Which are true about 'Graph Components'?

options.text: A connected component is a maximal connected subgraph.
In a directed graph, we have 'Strongly Connected Components' (SCC).
Each vertex belongs to exactly one connected component.
Components are only found in weighted graphs.

explanation: Components partition the graph into pieces where nodes can reach each other. This is a fundamental structural property of any graph.

description: Identify true statements regarding 'Adjacency' and 'Incidence'.

options.text: Two vertices are 'adjacent' if there is an edge between them.
An edge is 'incident' to the vertices it connects.
Adjacency involves node-to-node relationships.
Incidence involves node-to-edge relationships.

explanation: Adjacent means neighbors; incident means the edge 'touches' the node. These are the formal terms for graph connectivity.

description: What are true about 'Transposing' a directed graph?

options.text: All edge directions are reversed.
The adjacency matrix is transposed (rows become columns).
The number of vertices remains the same.
If a path A->B existed, a path B->A exists in the transposed graph.

explanation: Transposing flips the graph. This is a key step in algorithms like Kosaraju's for finding strongly connected components.

description: Which of these describe a 'Simple Path'?

options.text: No vertices are repeated.
No edges are repeated.
It can be a cycle if start and end are the same.
It is the shortest possible path.

explanation: Simple paths avoid 'circles' or 'backtracking' within the sequence of nodes. They are not necessarily the shortest path.

description: Regarding 'Graph Search' complexity, which are true?

options.text: BFS/DFS take O(V + E) time with adjacency lists.
BFS/DFS take O(V^2) time with adjacency matrices.
The time complexity is independent of the number of edges.
Space complexity is O(V) for the visited array and stack/queue.

explanation: With a matrix, you must check all V possible destinations for every vertex. With a list, you only check existing edges, making it much faster for typical graphs.

description: Regarding 'Dijkstra's Algorithm', which statements are true about its constraints and behavior?

options.text: It finds the shortest path from a single source to all other vertices.
It works correctly even if the graph contains negative edge weights.
It typically uses a Priority Queue to pick the next vertex with the smallest distance.
Its time complexity with a binary heap is O((V+E) log V).

explanation: Dijkstra's algorithm is a greedy algorithm for single-source shortest paths. It fails with negative weights because it assumes once a vertex is visited, its shortest path is finalized. A priority queue ensures we always expand the 'closest' frontier.
code:
priority_queue<pair<int, int>, vector<pair<int, int>>, greater<pair<int, int>>> pq;
pq.push({0, source});

description: Which statements are true about 'Topological Sorting'?

options.text: It is a linear ordering of vertices such that for every directed edge uv, u comes before v.
It can be implemented using Kahn's algorithm (based on In-degree).
A graph may have multiple valid topological sorts.
It can be used to detect cycles in a directed graph.

explanation: Topological sort is only possible for DAGs. If Kahn's algorithm cannot process all vertices (because no vertex has an in-degree of 0), a cycle must exist.
code: if (in_degree[v] == 0) queue.push(v);

description: Regarding 'Minimum Spanning Trees' (MST), which of these are true?

options.text: Kruskal's algorithm uses a Disjoint Set Union (DSU) to prevent cycles.
Prim's algorithm grows the MST by adding the cheapest edge from a visited to an unvisited node.
An MST must include all vertices of the graph.
The total weight of an MST is always unique, even if the edges are not.

explanation: Kruskal's sorts edges and uses DSU. Prim's grows a single tree. While there can be multiple MST structures (if edge weights are identical), the total minimum weight remains the same.

description: Which are true about 'Bellman-Ford Algorithm' compared to Dijkstra?

options.text: It can handle negative edge weights.
It can detect negative weight cycles.
It has a better time complexity than Dijkstra, O(V+E).
It works by relaxing all edges V-1 times.

explanation: Bellman-Ford is slower ($O(V 	imes E)$) but more robust, as it can detect if a graph contains a negative cycle by trying to relax edges a V-th time.

description: Regarding 'Strongly Connected Components' (SCC) in directed graphs, which are true?

options.text: In an SCC, every vertex is reachable from every other vertex in the same component.
Kosaraju's algorithm finds SCCs using two DFS passes.
Tarjan's algorithm finds SCCs in a single DFS pass.
A DAG has exactly one SCC.

explanation: SCCs are groups of mutually reachable nodes. In a DAG, there are no cycles, so every individual node is its own SCC (unless the DAG has V nodes, then it has V SCCs).

description: What are the properties of an 'Articulation Point' in an undirected graph?

options.text: Removing it increases the number of connected components.
It is also known as a 'cut vertex'.
It can be found in O(V+E) time using DFS discovery and low-link values.
The root of a DFS tree is an articulation point if it has at least two children.

explanation: Articulation points identify vulnerabilities in connectivity. DFS low-link values allow us to see if a subtree can 'reach back' to an ancestor without using the parent node.

description: Regarding 'Floyd-Warshall Algorithm', which statements are true?

options.text: It finds shortest paths between all pairs of vertices.
It uses dynamic programming.
The time complexity is O(V^3).
It requires the adjacency list representation for efficiency.

explanation: Floyd-Warshall is best implemented with an adjacency matrix. It iterates through all possible intermediate nodes 'k' to see if path i->k->j is shorter than i->j.
code: dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]);

description: Which of these are true about 'Eulerian Paths and Circuits' in undirected graphs?

options.text: An Eulerian Circuit exists if every vertex has an even degree.
An Eulerian Path exists if exactly zero or two vertices have an odd degree.
An Eulerian Circuit must visit every edge exactly once.
An Eulerian Circuit must visit every vertex exactly once.

explanation: Eulerian properties concern edges. Hamiltonian properties concern vertices. An Eulerian circuit visits every edge and returns to the start.

description: Analyze 'Disjoint Set Union' (DSU) with optimizations. Which are true?

options.text: Path compression makes the 'find' operation nearly O(1) amortized.
Union by Rank or Size prevents the tree from becoming skewed.
It is commonly used in Kruskal's MST algorithm.
It cannot be used for directed graphs.

explanation: DSU manages equivalence classes. While usually applied to undirected components, it's a general-purpose set data structure, though it doesn't represent edge direction.

description: Identify true statements regarding 'Cycle Detection'.

options.text: In undirected graphs, DFS detects a cycle if it encounters a visited node that is not the parent.
In directed graphs, a cycle exists if DFS encounters a node currently in the recursion stack.
BFS can detect cycles in undirected graphs.
DAGs are graphs where cycle detection algorithms always return false.

explanation: For undirected graphs, a simple 'visited' check (excluding the immediate parent) works. For directed graphs, we must distinguish between 'visited' (done) and 'in-stack' (active in current path).

description: Which are true about 'Bridges' in a graph?

options.text: A bridge is an edge whose removal increases the number of connected components.
An edge (u, v) is a bridge if there is no other path between u and v.
A cycle cannot contain a bridge.
Bridges can be found using the same DFS logic as articulation points.

explanation: Bridges are critical links. If an edge is part of a cycle, it can't be a bridge because the cycle provides an alternative path.

description: What are the characteristics of 'Adjacency Multilists'?

options.text: They represent each edge only once, even in an undirected graph.
Each edge node contains pointers to the next edge for both connected vertices.
They are more memory efficient than standard adjacency lists for undirected graphs.
They are primarily used in directed graphs.

explanation: Standard lists store (u,v) and (v,u) separately. Multilists store one edge object that is linked into both u's list and v's list.

description: Regarding 'Transitive Closure' of a graph, which are true?

options.text: It is a graph where an edge exists between u and v if there is a path from u to v in the original.
It can be computed using the Floyd-Warshall algorithm.
For a graph with V vertices, the transitive closure matrix is V x V.
It is always a complete graph.

explanation: Transitive closure represents 'reachability'. It is only a complete graph if the original graph was a single strongly connected component.

description: What is true about 'Graph Coloring'?

options.text: The Chromatic Number is the minimum number of colors needed to color a graph.
Finding the Chromatic Number is an NP-hard problem.
A graph with no edges has a chromatic number of 1.
Bipartite graphs always have a chromatic number of 2.

explanation: Coloring requires that no two adjacent vertices share a color. Bipartite graphs are the specific class of graphs that are 2-colorable.

description: Regarding 'Johnson's Algorithm' for all-pairs shortest paths, which are true?
