explanation: Doubly linked lists add a 'prev' pointer, increasing memory usage but allowing you to traverse backwards and delete nodes in O(1) time without searching from the head.

description: Identify valid ways to represent an 'empty' Linked List in TypeScript.

options.text: Assign the head property to 'null'.
Assign the head property to 'undefined'.
Maintain a 'size' property and set it to 0.
The head points to a 'dummy' node with no data.

explanation: Setting head to 'null' (or 'undefined' if typed so) is the standard indicator. Many implementations also track 'size' for O(1) length checks.

description: What are common pitfalls when implementing a linked list 'remove' operation?

options.text: Forgetting to handle the case where the list is empty.
Not updating the 'head' if the first node is removed.
Attempting to access '.next' on a 'null' reference.
Accidentally creating a memory leak by not manually calling 'free()'.

explanation: Edge cases like removing the head or an empty list are frequent sources of errors. Note that JS/TS has automatic garbage collection, so 'free()' is not used.

description: Which methods are typical for a Linked List class implementation?

options.text: append(value) - adds to the end.
prepend(value) - adds to the start.
insertAt(index, value) - adds at a specific position.
slice(start, end) - a native linked list method.

explanation: Append, prepend, and insertAt are standard operations. 'slice' is a native Array method and is not usually a built-in linked list operation unless custom-built.

description: Regarding 'Circular Linked Lists', which statements are true?

options.text: The last node points back to the head node.
There is no node in the list with a 'next' value of 'null'.
A 'while(current !== null)' loop will cause an infinite loop.
They are only possible with Doubly Linked Lists.

explanation: Circular lists loop back. Because they never hit 'null', standard 'null-check' traversals will run forever. They can be either singly or doubly linked.

description: What is the result of the following code snippet?

options.text: The list contains 20 then 10.
The head points to the node with value 20.
The list contains 10 then 20.
The node with 20 has a 'next' pointer to the node with 10.

explanation: The code creates a node (10), then creates a second node (20). By setting 'node2.next = node1', it places 20 before 10 in the logical chain.

description: Which of the following are true regarding the time complexity of Linked List operations?

options.text: Accessing the i-th element is O(N).
Search for a value is O(N).
Insertion at the head is O(1).
Deletion of the last node in a singly linked list is O(1).

explanation: Access and Search are linear. Prepending is constant. Deleting the tail in a singly linked list is O(N) because you must traverse to find the node *before* the tail.

description: In TypeScript, what type would you likely use for the 'next' property of a node?

options.text: ListNode | null
ListNode | undefined
any
ListNode

explanation: Since the last node must indicate the end of the list, the type must allow for a 'empty' state, usually 'null' or 'undefined'.

description: Which statements correctly describe the 'Tail' of a linked list?

options.text: It is the last node in the list.
Its 'next' property is always 'null' (in a standard non-circular list).
Keeping a separate 'tail' pointer makes 'append' O(1).
The tail is the same as the head in a list with one node.

explanation: The tail marks the end. Optimization with a tail pointer allows for instant appends, and in a single-node list, head and tail point to the same object.

description: When deleting a node from a singly linked list, which information is absolutely necessary?

options.text: The pointer to the node to be deleted.
The pointer to the node *before* the one to be deleted.
The total size of the list.
The head of the list (to find the previous node).

explanation: To delete a node, you must bridge its predecessor to its successor. This requires knowing the previous node, which usually requires starting from the 'head' to find it.

description: What is true about 'Sentinel' or 'Dummy' nodes in linked list implementations?

options.text: They help simplify edge cases for head insertion and deletion.
They do not store actual data used by the program.
They are required by the TypeScript compiler.
A dummy node is usually placed at the start of the list.

explanation: Dummy nodes act as placeholders so that the actual first node has a 'previous' node, allowing the code to treat all nodes (including the first) with the same logic.

description: Identify true statements regarding 'memory' in TypeScript Linked Lists.

options.text: Nodes are objects stored on the heap.
Each node has a memory overhead due to storing a pointer/reference.
Memory for nodes is contiguous (stored next to each other).
Deleting a reference allows memory to be reclaimed by the garbage collector.

explanation: Linked lists are non-contiguous. Each node is an object, and the 'pointers' are references that occupy extra space compared to array elements.

description: Which of these are correct for finding the length of a linked list?

options.text: Iterate through the list and increment a counter (O(N)).
Return a 'length' property that is updated during every push/pop (O(1)).
Use the 'list.length' native property.
Linked lists do not have a length.

explanation: Unlike Arrays, custom Linked Lists don't have a native 'length'. You must either calculate it via traversal or maintain a counter variable.

description: What happens if you set 'node1.next = node1'?

options.text: The node points to itself.
It creates a circular dependency.
Traversing the list will result in an infinite loop.
It is a valid way to terminate a list.

explanation: Self-referencing creates a loop. Any traversal logic that looks for 'null' will never find it and will cycle on this node indefinitely.

description: Which of the following describes the 'Singly Linked List' structure?

options.text: Nodes only know about the next node.
It requires the least amount of extra memory for links.
It is difficult to traverse backwards.
It is the most complex form of a linked list.

explanation: Singly linked lists are the simplest form, using only one reference per node. This makes them memory efficient but limits movement to one direction.

description: Regarding 'Garbage Collection' in this snippet, what happens to the node with value 10?

options.text: It becomes eligible for garbage collection.
It is still accessible via 'n1'.
It is permanently removed from memory immediately.
It is no longer part of the list starting from 'head'.

explanation: Since 'n1' still holds a reference to the node, it is NOT eligible for garbage collection. However, it is logically detached from the 'head' chain.

description: Identify valid TypeScript 'Node' class designs for a generic Linked List.

options.text: class Node<T> { value: T; next: Node<T> | null; }
interface Node { data: any; next: Node; }
type Node<T> = { value: T; next: Node<T> | null };
class Node { value: number; next: null; }

explanation: A and C are correct. B is incorrect because 'next' must be nullable to terminate. D is incorrect because 'next' is hardcoded to null, preventing links.

description: In a Linked List, 'Searching' for a value vs 'Accessing' by index both have O(N) complexity. Why?

options.text: Both require starting from the head and following pointers.
Nodes are not stored in contiguous memory.
You cannot 'jump' to a memory address without a reference.
TypeScript imposes a search limit on objects.

explanation: Because nodes are linked by references rather than indices in a block of memory, the only way to reach the 'i-th' or find 'X' is to walk through all previous nodes.

description: Which statements are true regarding the deletion of the 'Head' node?

options.text: Update head to be 'head.next'.
It is an O(1) operation.
It requires traversing the entire list.
It is handled automatically by the 'delete' keyword.

explanation: Removing the first node is instant (O(1))—you simply move the head pointer to the second node. The 'delete' keyword in JS/TS is for object properties, not for freeing memory.

description: What happens if a linked list node's 'next' property is never initialized in TypeScript?

options.text: It will be 'undefined' by default (unless specified otherwise).
It will cause a compile-time error if the type doesn't allow 'undefined'.
It will automatically point to the head.
It will be 'null' by default.

explanation: In TypeScript classes, uninitialized properties are 'undefined' at runtime. If your type is 'Node | null', the compiler will force you to initialize it.

description: Which of these describe 'Linear Data Structures'?

options.text: Linked Lists
Arrays
Trees
Queues

explanation: Linear structures have elements in a sequence. Trees are hierarchical (non-linear).

description: Identify true statements about 'memory leaks' specifically in Linked Lists.

options.text: Losing the reference to the 'head' while nodes still point to each other.
Creating a circular reference where no node is reachable from the root scope.
Forgetting to set 'node.next = null' after removing it from the list.
Setting 'head = null' on a list with 100 nodes.

explanation: Modern GC handles circular references (mark-and-sweep). A leak only occurs if the nodes are still reachable from the global/root scope. Detaching the head is generally safe if no other variables point to the nodes.

description: In a singly linked list, why is 'insertAt(0, val)' faster than 'insertAt(N, val)'?

options.text: Inserting at index 0 doesn't require any traversal.
Inserting at index N requires traversing all nodes (O(N)).
The head pointer is always available.
TypeScript optimizes index 0 operations.

explanation: Because we have a direct reference to the head, index 0 is O(1). To reach index N, we must iterate through the entire list.

description: Which are true regarding 'null' vs 'undefined' for the 'next' pointer?

options.text: 'null' is often used to represent an intentional absence of a next node.
'undefined' usually means the pointer hasn't been set yet.
TypeScript can check for both if the type is 'Node | null | undefined'.
They are functionally identical for terminating a list traversal.

explanation: While semantically different (intentional vs accidental), both act as falsy values that break a 'while(current)' loop.

description: Identify correct statements regarding 'Node' immutability in TypeScript.

options.text: You can use 'readonly' on node properties to create an immutable list.
An immutable list requires creating new nodes for every change.
Functional languages often use immutable linked lists.
TypeScript does not support 'readonly' for linked lists.

explanation: Immutability is common in functional programming. To 'change' an immutable list, you create a new node and point it to the existing (shared) tail of the old list.

description: Regarding the 'Tortoise and Hare' (Floyd's Cycle-Finding) algorithm in TypeScript, which statements are correct?

options.text: It uses two pointers moving at different speeds to detect a cycle.
If a cycle exists, the fast pointer will eventually equal the slow pointer.
It requires O(N) additional space to track visited nodes.
It can be used to find the middle of a linked list in a single pass.

explanation: Floyd's algorithm is highly efficient because it uses O(1) space. By moving the 'hare' twice as fast as the 'tortoise', it will either hit null (no cycle) or eventually lap the tortoise (cycle detected).

description: Analyze the following 'Reverse' logic for a singly linked list. Which statements are true?

options.text: The time complexity is O(N).
The space complexity is O(1) for an iterative approach.
It requires three pointers: previous, current, and next.
The final 'prev' pointer becomes the new head of the list.

explanation: Reversing a list in-place involves flipping the 'next' pointers. You need a reference to the previous node to point back to, and a reference to the 'original next' node so you don't lose the rest of the list during the flip.

description: Regarding 'Doubly Linked Lists' and node removal, which statements are accurate?

options.text: You can remove a node in O(1) if you have a direct reference to it.
You must traverse from the head to find the previous node.
You must update the 'prev' pointer of the next node.
You must update the 'next' pointer of the previous node.

explanation: Because each node knows its predecessor, you can 'unhook' a node instantly by linking its neighbors to each other, bypassing the need for a linear search.

description: Which of the following are true about 'Recursion' in Linked List operations?

options.text: A recursive 'reversePrint' uses O(N) space due to the call stack.
Recursive solutions are often cleaner but can lead to Stack Overflow for very large lists.
Recursive list operations always have O(1) time complexity.
The base case usually involves checking if the node is 'null'.

explanation: Recursion is a natural fit for the recursive structure of a linked list, but it carries a memory cost (O(N) stack frames) that can be problematic for massive data sets.

description: Identify true statements about the 'Sentinel' (Dummy) node pattern in TypeScript.

options.text: It eliminates the need for special 'head === null' checks during insertion.
The 'true' head of the list is accessed via 'dummy.next'.
It is useful when merging two sorted lists.
It must contain a value of the same type 'T' as other nodes.

explanation: Sentinels provide a consistent 'previous' node even for the first element. In TypeScript, if the data is required in the constructor, you might pass a dummy value or make the data property optional in the node class.

description: Analyze the problem of finding the 'N-th node from the end'. Which strategies are valid?

options.text: Two passes: first count the length, then traverse (Length - N) steps.
One pass: use two pointers with an N-node gap between them.
One pass: store every node in an array and access by index.
One pass: use a hash map to map indices to node references.

explanation: While all are technically valid, the 'two-pointer' approach is the most efficient (O(N) time, O(1) space). The array/map approaches use O(N) space.

description: Regarding 'Cycle Detection', if two pointers (slow/fast) meet at node 'K', how do you find the start of the cycle?

options.text: Keep one pointer at 'K' and reset the other to the 'head'.
Move both pointers one step at a time until they meet again.
Move the fast pointer to the head and the slow pointer to 'K'.
The meeting point 'K' is always the start of the cycle.

explanation: Based on the mathematical proof of Floyd's algorithm, the distance from the head to the cycle start is equal to the distance from the meeting point to the cycle start.

description: Which of these are true regarding 'Intersection' of two singly linked lists?

options.text: If two lists intersect, they share all nodes from the intersection point to the end.
Intersection is defined by nodes having the same 'data' value.
Intersection is defined by nodes having the same memory reference (identity).
It can be found by calculating the length difference and aligning the starting points.

explanation: In linked lists, intersection is structural. Once two 'next' pointers point to the same node, the subsequent paths are identical.

description: What are true about 'Unrolled Linked Lists'?

options.text: Each node stores an array of elements instead of a single element.
They improve cache performance by grouping elements together.
They are more memory efficient in terms of pointer overhead.
Searching is always O(1).

explanation: Unrolled lists combine the benefits of arrays (locality) and lists (insertion). They reduce the number of pointers stored but searching is still O(N) overall.

description: Identify true statements about 'Merging two sorted linked lists'.

options.text: It can be done iteratively using a dummy head (O(N+M) time, O(1) space).
It can be done recursively (O(N+M) time, O(N+M) stack space).
The iterative approach modifies the existing nodes' 'next' pointers.
Merging always requires creating entirely new node objects.

explanation: Merging is typically done in-place by 'weaving' the two lists together, which is highly efficient in terms of memory.

description: What are true about 'XOR Linked Lists' in a TypeScript context?

options.text: They store the XOR of the 'prev' and 'next' addresses in a single field.
They are difficult to implement in TypeScript/JS because of garbage collection and memory abstraction.
They use half the memory for pointers compared to standard doubly linked lists.
They are natively supported by the V8 engine.

explanation: XOR lists are a C/C++ trick. Since JS/TS does not allow direct manipulation of memory addresses (pointers) as numbers, you cannot perform bitwise XOR on them.

description: Regarding 'Garbage Collection' (GC) of circular references in TypeScript, which are true?

options.text: Modern 'Mark and Sweep' GC can reclaim circular lists if they are unreachable from the root.
Circular references always cause memory leaks in modern browsers.
Reference counting (used in older IE) would fail to reclaim a circular list.
You must manually break the circle before setting 'head = null'.

explanation: Circular references are only a problem for very primitive GC strategies. Modern engines trace reachability from global/stack roots; if the whole 'island' is unreachable, it's deleted.

description: Identify true statements about the '.sort()' operation for a Linked List.

options.text: Merge Sort is often preferred for linked lists because it doesn't require random access.
Quick Sort is less efficient for lists due to poor pivot selection without indexing.
Sorting a linked list is O(N log N) time complexity.
You can sort a linked list in O(1) space using iterative Merge Sort.

explanation: Linked lists provide a unique advantage for Merge Sort (O(1) auxiliary space by relinking), whereas arrays require O(N) space to merge.

description: In a 'Self-Organizing' linked list, which heuristics are common to speed up search?

options.text: Move-to-Front: Move the accessed node to the head.
Transpose: Swap the accessed node with its predecessor.
Count: Keep nodes sorted by their access frequency.
Binary Search: Split the list in half at each step.

explanation: Self-organizing lists adapt to access patterns. Binary search is impossible on a standard linked list because finding the middle is O(N).

description: What are true about 'Skip Lists'?

options.text: They use multiple layers of pointers to 'skip' over nodes.
Search time complexity is O(log N) on average.
They are a probabilistic data structure.
They are easier to implement than balanced trees like Red-Black trees.

explanation: Skip lists provide tree-like performance (Log N) using randomized layers of pointers, making them a popular alternative to complex balanced search trees.

description: Regarding 'Deleting a node' given only a pointer to that node (and it's not the tail), which are true?

options.text: Copy the data from the next node into the current node.
Set current.next = current.next.next.
It is an O(1) operation.
It is an O(N) operation.

explanation: This is a classic trick. You don't actually delete the node you're on; you effectively 'turn it into' the next node and delete the next one instead.

description: Analyze 'Palindrome' detection in a singly linked list. Which are true?

options.text: A stack can be used to compare the first half with the second half (reversed).
The second half of the list can be reversed in-place to compare (O(1) space).
It is an O(N) time complexity problem.
A palindrome check requires O(N log N) time.

explanation: Palindrome checking is linear. The most efficient way is finding the middle, reversing the second half, and comparing node by node.

description: Which of these are true for 'Flattening' a multi-level linked list?

options.text: It can be solved using a Queue (BFS-like) to process levels.
It can be solved using recursion (DFS-like).
The complexity is proportional to the total number of nodes.
Flattening always results in a sorted list.

explanation: Flattening is a structural transformation. It depends on whether you want to preserve level-order or depth-order, and the complexity is O(Total Nodes). Sorting is a separate step.

description: Regarding 'Copying' a linked list with 'Random' pointers, which are true?

options.text: A hash map can store the original-node to clone-node mapping.
An O(1) space approach involves interleaving clones between original nodes.
Clone nodes are linked: curr.next.random = curr.random.next.
It is an O(N^2) complexity problem.

explanation: Deep copying with random pointers is O(N). Interleaving (A -> A' -> B -> B') is the clever way to find the 'random' destination in O(1) space.

description: In TypeScript, how can you make a Linked List 'Iterable' for use in 'for...of' loops?

options.text: Implement the '[Symbol.iterator]' method.
Define a 'next()' method that returns { value, done }.
Extend the built-in Array class.
Use a generator function (yield).

explanation: To follow the iteration protocol, you must implement Symbol.iterator. Generator functions are a very concise way to do this.

description: Which are true regarding 'Swapping nodes' (not just data) in a singly linked list?

options.text: You must track the 'prev' nodes for both target nodes.
Special care is needed if the nodes are adjacent.
The 'head' might need to be updated if one node was the head.
It is always O(1) time.

explanation: Swapping nodes involves updating 4 pointers (2 'next' pointers and 2 'previous' nodes pointing to the new targets). It is O(N) because you must find the nodes and their predecessors first.

description: What are true about 'Partitioning' a linked list around value X?

options.text: Nodes smaller than X should come before nodes greater than or equal to X.
Stable partitioning preserves the original relative order of nodes.
It can be achieved by creating two separate lists and joining them.
It requires O(N) extra space.

explanation: Partitioning is best done by maintaining two tails (one for the 'less' list, one for the 'greater' list) and linking the nodes as you traverse the original list (O(1) extra space).

description: Regarding 'K-reverse' (reversing groups of size K), which are true?

options.text: It is an O(N) time complexity problem.
It can be solved recursively.
The last group might not be reversed if its size is < K.
It requires O(N) extra space.

explanation: K-reverse is a standard problem combining reversal logic with recursion or an outer loop. It is O(N) time and can be O(1) space if done iteratively.

description: Identify true statements about 'LRU Cache' implementation using Linked Lists.

options.text: A Doubly Linked List is used to track the order of usage.
A Hash Map is used to provide O(1) access to nodes.
When an item is accessed, it is moved to the head.
When the cache is full, the tail node is evicted.

explanation: The DLL + Hash Map combo is the gold standard for LRU. The list maintains order (Head=MRU, Tail=LRU), and the map prevents O(N) search times.

description: In the 'Josephus Problem', which linked list type is most natural?

options.text: Singly Circular Linked List
Doubly Circular Linked List
Standard Singly Linked List
Standard Doubly Linked List

explanation: The problem describes people in a circle. A circular list naturally handles the 'wrap-around' logic without needing extra modulo checks or resetting pointers to head manually.

description: Regarding 'Subtracting two numbers' represented as linked lists, which are true?

options.text: You must handle a 'borrow' logic similar to 'carry' in addition.
The lists should ideally be reversed before starting (if stored MSB first).
Leading zeros in the result should be removed.
The time complexity is O(max(N, M)).

explanation: Subtraction is more complex than addition because order matters (A-B != B-A) and borrowing can ripple through multiple nodes.

description: What are true regarding 'Persistent' (Immutable) linked lists?

options.text: Adding a new head creates a new list that shares the tail with the old list.
Both the old and new versions of the list remain valid and unchanged.
Structural sharing saves memory.
Modifying a node in the middle requires copying the entire list.

explanation: Immutability allows sharing. If you have list [2,3,4] and add 1 at the head, the new list is [1] -> [old head]. Modifying the middle only requires copying the nodes *before* the change.

description: Identify correct statements about 'Doubly Linked List' circular logic.

options.text: head.prev points to the tail.
tail.next points to the head.
It allows O(1) access to both ends and O(1) traversal in both directions.
A sentinel node is often used where sentinel.next = head and sentinel.prev = tail.

explanation: Circular DLLs are extremely symmetrical. A common optimization is having one 'sentinel' node that acts as both the 'before head' and 'after tail' boundary.

description: Which are true regarding 'Cycle length' in a linked list?

options.text: Once pointers meet at 'K', keep one fixed and move the other until it returns to 'K' to count length.
Cycle length can be found using the fast/slow pointer meeting logic.
The cycle length is always equal to the total number of nodes.
It is O(N) time complexity to find the length.

explanation: Finding the length is a follow-up to detection. Once you're inside the loop, counting how many steps it takes to return to the same node gives the cycle size.

description: Regarding 'TypeScript generics' with Linked Lists, which are true?

options.text: Generic lists allow for type safety across different data types.
Constraints (extends) can limit the data to objects with certain properties.
A 'LinkedList<string>' cannot accept a number value.
The 'next' pointer type should also use the same generic 'T'.

explanation: Generics are the standard way to build reusable data structures in TypeScript, ensuring that the 'data' type is consistent throughout the entire chain.
code: class Node<T> { data: T; next: Node<T> | null; }

description: Analyze the provided 'Merge Sort' implementation for a singly linked list in TypeScript. Which statements are true regarding its mechanics and complexity?

options.text: The 'split' phase typically utilizes the Slow/Fast pointer approach to find the midpoint in O(N).
Unlike array-based Merge Sort, list-based Merge Sort can achieve O(1) auxiliary space (excluding recursion stack).
The total time complexity is O(N log N) in all cases.
It is generally more efficient than Quick Sort for linked lists because it doesn't require random access.

explanation: Merge Sort is ideal for linked lists because it relies on sequential access. By relinking existing node pointers rather than allocating new arrays, it achieves high space efficiency. The recursion stack depth remains O(log N).

description: Regarding 'WeakRef' and 'FinalizationRegistry' in modern TypeScript/JS (ES2021+) for custom Linked Lists, which are true?

options.text: WeakRef allows holding a reference to a node without preventing its garbage collection.
FinalizationRegistry can be used to perform cleanup when a node is reclaimed.
Using WeakRef for 'next' pointers is the standard way to build a functional list.
Dereferencing a WeakRef via .deref() might return 'undefined' if the node was collected.

explanation: WeakRef is an advanced memory management tool. It's useful for caches or specialized structures but dangerous for 'next' pointers because nodes could disappear mid-traversal if not strongly referenced elsewhere.
code:
const ref = new WeakRef(node);
const actualNode = ref.deref();

description: Consider an 'Unrolled Linked List' where each node contains a fixed-capacity array. Which statements are true?

options.text: It significantly reduces cache misses by improving spatial locality.
Insertion and deletion in the middle of a block are O(BlockSize).
The pointer overhead is reduced relative to the number of elements.
It is technically a hybrid between a Linked List and a B-Tree leaf.

explanation: Unrolled lists group elements into buffers (arrays). This leverages CPU caches effectively and reduces the total number of 'next' pointers stored. Block operations involve shifting elements within the array.

description: Analyze the 'Deep Copy' problem for a list with 'Random' pointers. Which statements are true about the O(1) space (excluding output) weaving technique?

options.text: The algorithm first inserts clone nodes directly after their original counterparts.
Random pointers of clones are set using: 'curr.next.random = curr.random?.next ?? null'.
The original list must be restored by 'unweaving' the nodes at the end.
This technique avoids the O(N) space requirement of a Hash Map.

explanation: Weaving (A -> A' -> B -> B') allows the new nodes to find their 'random' targets by looking at the 'next' of the original's random target, eliminating the need for an external mapping table.

description: Regarding 'LRU Cache' implementation with a Doubly Linked List (DLL) and a Map, which are true?

options.text: The Map stores the 'key' and a 'reference to the node' for O(1) access.
The DLL maintains the 'recency' order (Head = MRU, Tail = LRU).
Promoting a node to the head involves O(1) pointer updates.
The 'capacity' check and eviction are both O(1) operations.

explanation: The combination of these two structures provides the best of both worlds: the Map provides fast lookups, and the DLL allows fast re-ordering and eviction without linear scans.

description: In a 'Skip List', which of the following statements regarding complexity and structure are accurate?

options.text: Searching, insertion, and deletion are O(log N) on average.
The height of each node is determined probabilistically (usually by coin flips).
It is essentially a layered collection of linked lists with 'express lanes'.
The worst-case time complexity for a search is O(N).

explanation: Skip lists use randomization to maintain balance. While they provide logarithmic performance on average (similar to balanced trees), a 'bad' set of random heights could lead to a linear search in the absolute worst case.

description: Regarding 'Persistent' (Immutable) Linked Lists in functional programming patterns, which are true?

options.text: Prepending a node creates a new list head while sharing the original tail (Structural Sharing).
Updating a node in the middle requires 'Path Copying' of all nodes from the head to that node.
They are inherently thread-safe because data is never modified in place.
Garbage collection in JS/TS is particularly efficient at handling shared persistent structures.

explanation: Structural sharing is the core of immutability. You only copy what changes (the path to the change); the rest of the list structure is reused by the new version, saving memory and time.

description: Analyze the behavior of 'Circular Doubly Linked Lists' with a single Sentinel node. Which are true?

options.text: An empty list is represented by the sentinel pointing to itself (next and prev).
This structure removes all 'null' checks from the insertion and deletion logic.
The 'head' is technically 'sentinel.next' and the 'tail' is 'sentinel.prev'.
It requires more memory than a standard DLL due to the extra sentinel node.

explanation: The Sentinel-based circular DLL is the cleanest implementation. Since 'next' and 'prev' are always valid objects (even if they point to the sentinel), the logic doesn't need to branch for nulls.

description: What is the result of applying 'Tail Call Optimization' (TCO) to a recursive linked list traversal in TypeScript?

options.text: It converts the recursive calls into a loop, preventing Stack Overflow.
It requires the recursive call to be the final action in the function.
It is currently only supported by a limited number of JS engines (primarily Safari/JSC).
It reduces the space complexity from O(N) to O(1).

explanation: TCO is a part of the ES6 spec, but implementation is poor across engines (except Safari). It allows the engine to reuse the current stack frame for the next call, making recursion as safe as a while-loop.

description: Regarding 'Memory Layout' and performance, why is an Array often faster than a Linked List in modern V8 engines?

options.text: CPU Prefetching works better with the contiguous memory of an array.
Linked list nodes are separate objects, causing pointer-chasing and cache misses.
Arrays in V8 can be optimized into 'Hidden Classes' for specific element kinds.
Arrays use significantly less memory per element (no pointer overhead).

explanation: Modern hardware is designed for spatial locality. Arrays allow the CPU to predict and pre-load data into the cache. Linked lists jump around memory addresses, forcing the CPU to wait for RAM more often.

description: In the 'Josephus Problem', why is a Circular Linked List the mathematically 'pure' implementation?

options.text: The elimination process naturally cycles through the participants.
It avoids using modulo arithmetic to 'reset' an index.
Deletion of a node automatically links the next person to the previous person.
It can be solved in O(N) time using this structure.

explanation: Circular lists model the 'ring' perfectly. While O(N) mathematical solutions exist using DP/recursion, the list implementation is O(N*M) but provides a literal simulation of the process.

description: Regarding 'Garbage Collection' in a Doubly Linked List with circular references, which are true?

options.text: V8's Mark-and-Sweep algorithm handles these cycles correctly.
If the 'head' is unreachable from the root, the entire list is reclaimed.
Circular references 'leak' memory only if using primitive reference counting.
You must set every 'next' and 'prev' to null to free the list.

explanation: JS engines do not use simple reference counting. They trace paths from global/stack roots. A 'floating island' of nodes that point to each other but not to a root will be successfully deleted.

description: Which are true about 'Self-Organizing' lists and the 'Move-to-Front' (MTF) heuristic?

options.text: MTF achieves near-O(1) search time for highly skewed access patterns.
It is an online algorithm that adapts to changing request frequencies.
In the worst case (reverse order access), it is no better than a standard list.
It requires O(1) additional space per node to implement.

explanation: MTF exploits 'temporal locality'. If you access an item, you're likely to access it again soon. By moving it to the front, you optimize future searches for that item.

description: When implementing 'Add Two Numbers' (where lists represent digits in normal order), which are true?

options.text: The lists must be reversed first or solved using recursion/stacks.
Digits must be added from right to left to handle the 'carry' correctly.
The resulting list may have one more node than the longest input list.
Normal order addition is significantly more complex than reverse order addition.

explanation: Since addition starts at the 'units' place (tail), and linked lists only have a 'head', you either reverse the lists to align units at the head or use a stack to process them in reverse.

description: Regarding 'K-reverse' logic in a singly linked list, which are true?

options.text: It involves reversing chunks of K nodes and connecting them.
The 'tail' of one reversed group must point to the 'head' of the next group.
It can be solved recursively by returning the new head of the remaining list.
The time complexity is O(N) and space can be O(1) iterative.

explanation: K-Reverse is a common complex problem. It requires reversing a sub-segment, tracking the new head, and maintaining the linkage to the rest of the list recursively or iteratively.

description: Analyze the 'Reorder List' problem (L0 → Ln → L1 → Ln-1 → ...). Which steps are required?

options.text: Find the middle of the list (Slow/Fast pointers).
Reverse the second half of the list.
Interleave (merge) the two halves node by node.
The operation can be performed in O(N) time and O(1) space.

explanation: This specific reordering is a sequence of three primitive list operations. By splitting, reversing the tail, and then weaving, you achieve the result without extra memory.

description: Identify true statements regarding 'Flattening' a list where each node has a 'child' pointer to another list.

options.text: A recursive Depth-First approach is commonly used.
An iterative approach using a Tail pointer can achieve O(N) time.
Flattening converts a 2D-like structure into a linear 1D structure.
It is essentially a tree traversal serialized into a list.

explanation: Flattening can be done by taking a child list and appending it to the end of the current 'next' chain, then continuing the traversal. It effectively flattens a hierarchy.

description: Regarding 'Sorted Merge' of multiple (K) linked lists, which strategies are true?

options.text: Using a Min-Priority Queue of heads achieves O(N log K) time.
Divide and Conquer (merging lists in pairs) also achieves O(N log K).
Iteratively merging one by one into a single list is O(N * K).
The Priority Queue approach uses O(K) extra space.

explanation: N is the total number of nodes. K-way merge is much more efficient using a heap or divide-and-conquer strategy compared to the naive iterative approach.

description: Which are true about 'Partitioning' a list around value X while maintaining 'Stability'?

options.text: Stability means nodes with the same value category stay in their relative order.
It requires maintaining two separate head/tail pairs for 'less' and 'greater'.
It is performed in O(N) time and O(1) extra space.
Dummy nodes (Sentinels) are highly effective for simplifying the list merges.

explanation: Stable partitioning ensures that if node A came before B in the original list and both are < X, A still comes before B. Using two temporary sub-lists is the standard O(1) space method.

description: Analyze 'Cycle Detection' return values. If you need to return the 'Node' where the cycle starts, which logic is true?

options.text: After fast and slow meet, reset slow to head.
Move both one step at a time until they meet again.
The node they meet at is the start of the cycle.
This relies on the distance formula: 2(D + S) = D + S + nC.

explanation: This is the mathematical extension of Floyd's. The distance from the head to the start of the cycle is the same as the distance from the first meeting point to the start of the cycle (mod cycle length).

description: Identify true statements about 'Bitwise' pointer storage (XOR Lists) in TypeScript.

options.text: They cannot be implemented directly because JS references are not memory addresses.
XORing two JS objects results in 'NaN' or an error.
They are a C-specific optimization to save 4-8 bytes per node.
A workaround in TS would require an external array acting as 'Memory'.

explanation: XOR lists require performing math on pointers. TypeScript/JavaScript abstracts memory entirely, so you don't have access to the raw address needed for the XOR operation.

description: What are the characteristics of 'Self-Balancing' linked structures like 'Skip Lists'?

options.text: They maintain a sorted order of elements.
The number of levels follows a geometric distribution.
They provide a trade-off between implementation simplicity and efficiency.
They are highly suitable for concurrent access (lock-free patterns).

explanation: Skip lists are easier to implement than Red-Black trees and are very popular in databases (like Redis) because they allow for easy lock-free concurrent implementations.

description: Regarding 'Copying' a linked list with random pointers without a Map, which are true?

options.text: It modifies the original list temporarily.
It works in O(N) time.
It is less readable but more memory efficient than the Map approach.
It involves 'interweaving' clone nodes between original nodes.

explanation: By putting the clone A' between original A and B, A'.random can be found as A.random.next. This clever manipulation uses the list itself as the 'hash map'.

description: In TypeScript, what are true regarding 'Generator Functions' for list traversal?

options.text: They allow for 'Lazy Evaluation' of the list.
They enable the list to be used in 'for...of' and spread '...' operations.
They provide O(1) space traversal as they don't create intermediate arrays.
The 'yield' keyword pauses execution and returns the current value.

explanation: Generators are a modern way to implement the iterator protocol. They provide a stream of values one by one, making them memory efficient for large structures.

description: Analyze the 'Length' calculation of a circular linked list. Which logic is correct?

options.text: Use a 'do...while' loop to ensure the first node is processed.
Stop the loop when 'current === head' after the first step.
A standard 'while(current !== null)' will loop forever.
An empty list case (head === null) must be handled separately.

explanation: Circular lists require a boundary check against the 'head' rather than 'null'. Starting at head and checking 'until head is reached again' is the standard algorithm.

description: Regarding 'Node Swapping' in a singly linked list without swapping data, which are true?

options.text: You must identify the 'prevX' and 'prevY' nodes.
The 'next' pointers of four nodes may need updating.
If X is the head, the 'head' pointer must be updated to Y.
If X and Y are adjacent, special logic is required to avoid self-referencing.

explanation: Swapping nodes involves careful 're-wiring'. If they are adjacent, the 'prev' of one is the other node itself, which can create a cycle if not handled with precise pointer assignment order.

description: Which of these describe 'Double-Ended' queues (Deques) implemented via DLL?

options.text: They support O(1) push and pop from both front and back.
They require maintaining both 'head' and 'tail' pointers.
A Doubly Linked List is the most efficient base structure.
They are the underlying structure for most 'Stack' and 'Queue' polyfills.

explanation: Deques need bidirectional efficiency. A DLL provides O(1) removal from the tail (which a singly linked list cannot do without a full traversal), making it the perfect fit.

description: Regarding 'Rotate List' by K positions, which logic is accurate?

options.text: K should be taken modulo the length (K = K % length).
The tail of the list is connected to the original head.
The list is broken at the (Length - K)-th node.
The new head is the node after the break point.

explanation: Rotating involves making the list circular, then breaking the circle at the correct offset to establish a new head and tail.

description: Identify true statements about 'Memory' and 'Garbage Collection' for Linked Lists in TypeScript.

options.text: Each node is an object, meaning it has a header overhead (typically 12-16 bytes in V8).
Small objects like nodes can trigger frequent 'Minor GC' (Scavenge) cycles.
Large linked lists can contribute to 'Memory Fragmentation'.
Local variables holding a 'current' pointer prevent GC of the current node.

explanation: Memory management is more than just 'nulling pointers'. Every node object carries engine-specific metadata, and the sheer volume of small objects can affect GC performance and heap layout.

description: Regarding 'Intersection' of two lists, which are true?

options.text: A Hash Set of node references can find the intersection in O(N+M) time.
Aligning start points by length difference finds it in O(N+M) time and O(1) space.
The two-pointer switching technique (A->B, B->A) also finds it in O(1) space.
If two lists intersect, 'tailA === tailB' must be true.

explanation: Intersection means the 'next' pointers eventually merge into a single shared path. This means both lists will terminate at the exact same physical tail node.

description: Which of the following statements correctly describe a 'Root' node in a TypeScript tree structure?

options.text: It is the topmost node of the tree.
It is the only node in a tree that has no parent.
A single tree can have multiple root nodes.
Every node in a tree is a descendant of the root.

explanation: By definition, a tree is a hierarchical structure with exactly one root node. All other nodes are connected via edges and originate from this single starting point.
code: const root: TreeNode = new TreeNode(1);

description: Identify the characteristics of a 'Leaf' node in a Binary Tree.

options.text: It has no children.
Its 'left' and 'right' properties are both null.
It is also known as an 'external node'.
A leaf node must always be at the maximum depth of the tree.

explanation: A leaf node is any node that does not have any children. While many leaves are at the bottom level, a node with no children at a higher level is still a leaf node.
code: if (node.left === null && node.right === null) { /* Leaf node */ }

description: Regarding 'Binary Trees', which statements are correct?

options.text: Each node can have at most two children.
A node can have zero, one, or two children.
Every node must have exactly two children.
The subtrees are usually referred to as 'left' and 'right'.

explanation: Binary trees restrict the branching factor to two. This doesn't mean nodes must have two children, only that they cannot exceed two.

description: Which of the following are standard Depth-First Search (DFS) traversals for a tree?

options.text: Pre-order
In-order
Post-order
Level-order

explanation: Pre-order, In-order, and Post-order are DFS strategies that explore branches as deeply as possible. Level-order is a Breadth-First Search (BFS) strategy.

description: What are the properties of a 'Binary Search Tree' (BST)?

options.text: The left subtree contains nodes with values less than the parent.
The right subtree contains nodes with values greater than the parent.
In-order traversal of a BST results in a sorted array.
Searching in a balanced BST has O(log N) time complexity.

explanation: BSTs maintain a specific order: Left < Root < Right. This ordering allows for efficient searching and provides sorted data during an in-order traversal.

description: Identify true statements about the 'Height' and 'Depth' of a tree node.

options.text: Depth is the number of edges from the root to the node.
Height is the number of edges on the longest path from the node to a leaf.
The depth of the root node is 0.
The height of a leaf node is 0.

explanation: Depth measures 'top-down' distance from the root. Height measures 'bottom-up' distance to the furthest leaf.

description: What is true about 'Level-order' traversal?

options.text: It visits nodes level by level from top to bottom.
It typically uses a Queue data structure.
It is a form of Breadth-First Search (BFS).
It visits all children of a node before moving to its siblings.

explanation: Level-order (BFS) processes all nodes at the current depth before moving deeper, which is why a FIFO queue is used to track pending nodes.
code: const queue: TreeNode[] = [root];

description: In the context of 'Complete Binary Trees', which statements are true?

options.text: Every level, except possibly the last, is completely filled.
All nodes in the last level are as far left as possible.
It can be efficiently represented using an array.
Every node must have exactly two children.

explanation: Complete trees are filled level by level. Because they are packed to the left, they map perfectly to arrays (where child indices are 2i+1 and 2i+2).

description: Regarding 'Post-order' traversal (Left, Right, Root), which are true?

options.text: The root node is visited last.
It is useful for deleting a tree (children before parents).
It visits leaf nodes before the root.
The first node visited is always the root.

explanation: Post-order ensures that a node is processed only after all its descendants. This is essential for operations like calculating subtree sizes or freeing memory.

description: Which of these are correct definitions for tree properties?

options.text: Siblings are nodes that share the same parent.
An ancestor is any node on the path from root to that node.
The degree of a node is the number of children it has.
A forest is a collection of disjoint trees.

explanation: These are standard terms. Degree in trees usually refers to 'out-degree' (children), and a forest is simply a set of multiple trees.

description: What are true about 'Full Binary Trees'?

options.text: Every node has either 0 or 2 children.
No node has exactly one child.
All leaf nodes must be at the same depth.
It is also known as a 'proper' binary tree.

explanation: A full (or proper) tree prohibits nodes with only one child. It does not require all levels to be full (that would be a Perfect tree).

description: Identify valid 'In-order' traversal (Left, Root, Right) characteristics.

options.text: It visits the left subtree, then the node, then the right subtree.
It produces values in ascending order for a BST.
It produces values in descending order for a BST.
The root is visited somewhere in the middle (unless the tree is skewed).

explanation: In-order traversal is the key to retrieving sorted data from a Binary Search Tree because it follows the logical order of the values.

description: Regarding tree storage, which are true about 'Sequential' (Array) representation?

options.text: If a parent is at index 'i', the left child is at '2i + 1'.
If a parent is at index 'i', the right child is at '2i + 2'.
It is very space-efficient for sparse trees.
It is most efficient for Complete Binary Trees.

explanation: Array storage is perfect for complete trees because there are no gaps. For sparse or skewed trees, many array slots would remain empty, wasting memory.

description: What happens when you search for a value in a standard (non-BST) Binary Tree?

options.text: The time complexity is O(N) in the worst case.
You must potentially visit every node.
You can use either DFS or BFS for the search.
The time complexity is always O(log N).

explanation: Without the sorting property of a BST, a binary tree is unordered. To find a value, you must perform an exhaustive search, which is linear (O(N)).

description: Identify true statements about the 'Recursive' nature of trees.

options.text: Every child node can be viewed as the root of its own subtree.
Recursive algorithms are a natural fit for tree traversals.
A tree is either empty or consists of a root and its subtrees.
Recursive traversals use the system call stack.

explanation: Trees are defined recursively. Each node is effectively the root of a smaller tree, which is why recursion is the most common way to process them.

description: Regarding 'Perfect Binary Trees', which are true?

options.text: All internal nodes have exactly two children.
All leaf nodes are at the same level.
The number of nodes is 2^(height + 1) - 1.
A perfect tree is also a full tree and a complete tree.

explanation: Perfect trees are the most symmetric. Every level is full, and every node has 0 or 2 children, making it a subset of both full and complete trees.

description: Which of these are common real-world applications of tree data structures?

options.text: File systems (folders and files).
DOM (Document Object Model) in browsers.
Organizational charts.
Sequential undo-redo history.

explanation: Trees represent hierarchies. Undo-redo is usually a linear stack, but file systems, the DOM, and company hierarchies are classic tree examples.

description: What is true about the 'size' of a tree?

options.text: It is the total number of nodes in the tree.
Size of tree = Size(left) + Size(right) + 1.
Size of an empty tree is 0.
Size is the same as the height.

explanation: Size is a count of elements. Height is a measure of depth. The recursive formula for size counts all children plus the root node itself.

description: Identify true statements about 'Skewed Binary Trees'.

options.text: All nodes have only one child (except the leaf).
They effectively behave like a Linked List.
Search time complexity is O(N).
They are highly balanced.

explanation: Skewed trees lean entirely left or right. This 'worst-case' structure loses the benefits of branching, leading to linear O(N) performance.

description: Regarding 'Pre-order' traversal (Root, Left, Right), which are true?

options.text: The root is always the first node visited.
It is useful for creating a copy of the tree.
It is used to evaluate prefix expressions.
It visits leaf nodes before visiting the root.

explanation: Pre-order visits the 'parent' before its 'children'. This top-down sequence is perfect for duplicating a tree structure exactly.

description: What are valid TypeScript types for a Binary Tree node?

options.text: class TreeNode { val: number; left: TreeNode | null; right: TreeNode | null; }
interface ITreeNode { val: any; left?: ITreeNode; right?: ITreeNode; }
type TNode = { val: string, children: TNode[] };
enum NodeType { Root, Leaf }

explanation: A and B correctly define binary nodes with pointers. C defines a general (N-ary) tree node. D is just an enumeration, not a node structure.

description: Which of the following are true regarding 'Binary Search' vs 'Binary Tree Search'?

options.text: Binary Search requires a sorted array.
Binary Search Tree search requires a BST.
Both have average time complexity of O(log N).
Both require visiting every element in the worst case.

explanation: Both techniques use the 'halving' principle. However, if an array is sorted, Binary Search is O(log N). If a BST is balanced, search is O(log N). Exhaustive O(N) only happens in skewed trees or unsorted arrays.

description: Regarding 'Level' in a tree, identify true statements.

options.text: The root node is at level 0 (or 1).
Nodes at the same level are siblings or cousins.
The maximum number of nodes at level 'L' is 2^L (starting at L=0).
Level is the same as the node's degree.

explanation: Level represents depth. In a binary tree, the capacity for nodes doubles at every level (1, 2, 4, 8...).

description: What is true about 'Ancestors' in a tree?

options.text: A node's parent is an ancestor.
The root is an ancestor of every node (except itself).
Ancestors are nodes found on the path from the node to the root.
A node's children are its ancestors.

explanation: Ancestors are the 'lineage' above a node. Pointers in trees usually go from parent to child, but the ancestor relationship follows the path back to the root.

description: Which statements are correct regarding 'Path' in a tree?

options.text: A path is a sequence of nodes and edges connecting nodes.
There is exactly one path between any two nodes in a tree.
The length of a path is the number of edges it contains.
Paths in a tree can be circular (cycles).

explanation: Trees are acyclic. The 'exactly one path' property is what distinguishes trees from general graphs.

description: What are true about 'N-ary Trees' compared to Binary Trees?

options.text: N-ary trees allow nodes to have more than two children.
Binary trees are a specific type of N-ary tree where N=2.
In TypeScript, children are often stored in an array: `children: Node[]`.
N-ary trees cannot be traversed using DFS.

explanation: N-ary trees generalize the branching factor. They support all standard traversals, though In-order is less clearly defined than it is for binary trees.

description: Identify true statements about the 'Root-to-Leaf' path.

options.text: The root is the first node on the path.
The leaf is the last node on the path.
The number of such paths equals the number of leaves in the tree.
All root-to-leaf paths must have the same length.

explanation: Every leaf defines a unique path from the root. These paths can vary in length unless the tree is 'Perfect'.

description: Regarding 'Edge' in a tree with N nodes, which are true?

options.text: There are exactly N-1 edges.
Each edge connects exactly two nodes.
Every node except the root has exactly one incoming edge.
The number of edges is equal to N.

explanation: In any tree, the number of edges is always one less than the number of nodes because every node (except the root) is pointed to by exactly one parent.

description: What is true about 'Searching' in a Binary Search Tree (BST)?

options.text: If target < current.val, go left.
If target > current.val, go right.
If target === current.val, value is found.
You must always check both left and right subtrees.

explanation: Search in a BST is efficient because you can discard half the remaining tree at each step based on the comparison, rather than checking both sides.

description: Identify correct statements about 'Empty Trees' in TypeScript.

options.text: An empty tree is often represented by a 'null' root pointer.
The height of an empty tree is often defined as -1.
The size of an empty tree is 0.
Recursive functions must handle the empty case to avoid errors.

explanation: Null is the standard way to represent 'nothing' in a tree structure. Handling the null case is the base case for almost all recursive tree algorithms.

description: Regarding 'Balanced Binary Search Trees' (like AVL or Red-Black trees), which statements are correct?

options.text: They ensure the height of the tree remains O(log N).
Insertion, deletion, and search all have a worst-case complexity of O(log N).
Balance is maintained through 'rotations' during insertion and deletion.
An AVL tree is more strictly balanced than a Red-Black tree.

explanation: Self-balancing trees prevent the 'skewed' worst-case scenario. AVL trees maintain a height difference of at most 1, making them more rigid (and faster for lookups) than Red-Black trees, which allow for a slightly more relaxed balance.

description: Analyze the 'Binary Heap' data structure (implemented as a tree). Which of the following are true?

options.text: It is a complete binary tree.
In a Max-Heap, the root node contains the maximum value in the tree.
The parent of a node at index 'i' in an array is at index 'floor((i-1)/2)'.
Searching for a specific value in a Heap is O(log N).

explanation: Heaps are great for priority access (O(1) for min/max), but since they don't maintain a total order like BSTs, searching for a random value still takes O(N) time.

description: Which statements are true regarding 'Tries' (Prefix Trees)?

options.text: They are used for efficient retrieval of strings with a common prefix.
The time complexity to search for a word is O(L), where L is the length of the word.
Each node usually represents a single character.
They are always more space-efficient than a Hash Map for storing strings.

explanation: Tries excel at prefix matching and autocomplete. However, they can be memory-heavy because each node stores multiple pointers (one for each possible character in the alphabet).

description: Regarding 'Segment Trees', which of the following are accurate?

options.text: They are used for performing range queries (e.g., sum, min, max) on an array.
Range updates can be optimized using 'Lazy Propagation'.
Building a segment tree from an array of size N takes O(N) time.
Both point updates and range queries take O(log N) time.

explanation: Segment trees pre-calculate values for intervals. This allows them to answer range-based questions very quickly. Lazy propagation allows them to handle range updates without updating every individual leaf immediately.

description: Identify true statements about the 'Lowest Common Ancestor' (LCA) in a tree.

options.text: In a BST, the LCA of two nodes is the first node whose value is between the two nodes.
It is the deepest node that is an ancestor of both target nodes.
Every pair of nodes in a rooted tree has exactly one LCA.
Finding the LCA in a general binary tree takes O(N) time without preprocessing.

explanation: LCA is a fundamental tree problem. In BSTs, you can find it by traversing based on the value range. In general trees, you typically use recursion or path tracking.

description: What are true regarding 'B-Trees' and 'B+ Trees' used in databases?

options.text: They are self-balancing search trees designed for block storage.
They allow for more than two children per node (high fan-out).
In a B+ Tree, all data is stored in the leaf nodes.
B-Trees maintain a constant O(1) depth.

explanation: These trees minimize disk reads by being 'flat and wide'. Their height is logarithmic (O(log N)), but with a very large base, making them extremely shallow even for millions of records.

description: Regarding 'Morris Traversal', which statements are correct?

options.text: It performs tree traversal in O(1) auxiliary space.
It uses 'Threaded Binary Tree' concepts to avoid recursion/stacks.
It temporarily modifies the tree structure by creating 'threads' back to ancestors.
It is significantly faster than standard recursive traversal.

explanation: Morris Traversal is a clever algorithm that uses NULL pointers of leaf nodes to point back to the next node in the traversal order. While it saves space, it is usually slightly slower due to the overhead of creating and removing these temporary threads.

description: Analyze the 'Huffman Coding' tree. Which statements are correct?

options.text: It is used for lossless data compression.
Characters with higher frequencies are closer to the root.
It is a full binary tree (every internal node has two children).
The path from the root to a leaf defines the binary code for a character.

explanation: Huffman trees use variable-length prefix codes. By placing frequent characters near the root (shorter paths), the overall size of the encoded data is minimized.

description: Regarding 'Fenwick Trees' (Binary Indexed Trees), which are true?

options.text: They are used for efficient prefix sum queries and point updates.
They require less memory than a Segment Tree.
The time complexity for both update and prefix sum is O(log N).
They can only be used for range sum queries, not range minimum queries.

explanation: Fenwick trees are elegant and memory-efficient (using exactly N space). While they are perfect for prefix sums, they are generally not suitable for Range Minimum/Maximum queries because the 'min' operation isn't easily reversible.

description: Which of these are true about 'Expression Trees'?

options.text: Leaves are operands (numbers/variables).
Internal nodes are operators (+, -, *, etc.).
Post-order traversal results in the Postfix notation (Reverse Polish Notation).
In-order traversal results in the Infix notation (with proper parentheses).

explanation: Expression trees are a standard way to represent mathematical formulas. The way you walk through the tree (traversal) determines how the formula is written out string-wise.

description: Regarding 'Red-Black Trees', which specific properties are enforced?

options.text: Every node is either red or black.
The root and all NIL leaves are black.
A red node cannot have a red child.
Every path from a node to its descendant NIL leaves contains the same number of black nodes.

explanation: These four rules ensure the tree remains approximately balanced. Specifically, they guarantee that the longest path is no more than twice the length of the shortest path.

description: What is true about 'Threaded Binary Trees'?

options.text: They use NULL pointers to store references back to in-order predecessors/successors.
They allow for faster in-order traversal without using a stack or recursion.
They increase the memory footprint of each node to store extra boolean flags.
They are exclusively used for Max-Heaps.

explanation: Threading essentially 'paves' a path through the tree for traversal. Flags are needed to distinguish whether a pointer is a standard 'child' pointer or a 'thread'.

description: Regarding the 'Diameter' of a binary tree, which statements are true?

options.text: It is the length of the longest path between any two nodes in the tree.
The longest path must always pass through the root node.
It can be calculated in O(N) time.
The diameter is equal to Height(left) + Height(right) + 2 for the root node's path.

explanation: The diameter is the 'width' of the tree. While often passing through the root, the diameter could exist entirely within one of the subtrees if that subtree is very long and narrow.

description: Identify true statements about 'AVL Trees'.

options.text: The balance factor of any node can only be -1, 0, or +1.
A 'Right Rotation' is used when a node becomes left-heavy.
Searching is generally faster in AVL trees than in Red-Black trees.
They require more rotations during insertion/deletion than Red-Black trees.

explanation: AVL trees have a stricter balance requirement than Red-Black trees. This makes lookups faster (tighter height) but updates slower (more frequent rebalancing).

description: Which are true regarding 'N-ary Tree' traversal (N > 2)?

options.text: Pre-order and Post-order traversals are well-defined.
In-order traversal is not uniquely defined without specifying which child to visit when.
Level-order traversal still uses a Queue.
N-ary trees cannot be represented using arrays.

explanation: In-order (Left-Root-Right) doesn't naturally map to 3+ children. N-ary trees can be represented in arrays using specific formulas or by converting them into a 'Binary' representation (First Child, Next Sibling).

description: Regarding 'K-D Trees' (K-Dimensional Trees), what is true?

options.text: They are space-partitioning trees used for organizing points in K-dimensional space.
Each level of the tree splits the data based on a different dimension.
They are useful for nearest neighbor search and range searches.
They are exclusively used for 2D graphics.

explanation: K-D trees extend binary search concepts to multiple axes (X, Y, Z, etc.). They are used in fields ranging from robotics to astronomy to find points close to a query location efficiently.

description: What is true about 'Splay Trees'?

options.text: They are self-balancing BSTs that move frequently accessed nodes closer to the root.
They do not guarantee a strictly balanced height (O(log N)) for every operation.
The amortized time complexity of operations is O(log N).
They use a process called 'Splaying' during every access.

explanation: Splay trees are 'self-adjusting'. While a single operation could be O(N), the most used items stay at the top, leading to excellent average performance for real-world access patterns.

description: Regarding 'Treaps' (Tree + Heap), identify true statements.

options.text: Each node has a key (for BST order) and a randomly assigned priority (for Heap order).
The tree structure is randomized, providing O(log N) expected height.
They are easier to implement than AVL or Red-Black trees.
They are guaranteed to be O(log N) height in the absolute worst case.

explanation: Treaps use probability to stay balanced. By using random priorities, it is mathematically 'extremely unlikely' for the tree to become unbalanced, though not strictly impossible.

description: Which are true regarding 'Suffix Trees'?

options.text: They contain all suffixes of a given string.
They allow for O(M) pattern matching, where M is the length of the pattern.
Ukkonen's Algorithm can build a suffix tree in O(N) time.
They are primarily used for numeric sorting.

explanation: Suffix trees are high-performance string processing structures. They are used for finding repetitive patterns, longest common substrings, and complex genomic sequence analysis.

description: What happens in a 'Cartesian Tree'?

options.text: It is built from a sequence of numbers.
An in-order traversal of the tree recovers the original sequence.
The tree follows the heap property based on node values.
It is used in solving the Range Minimum Query (RMQ) problem.

explanation: Cartesian trees link sequences and heaps. Finding the LCA of two nodes in a Cartesian tree built from an array is equivalent to finding the minimum value between those two indices in the array (RMQ).

description: Regarding 'Van Emde Boas' (vEB) trees, which statements are true about their complexity and constraints?

options.text: They support successor and predecessor operations in O(log log U) time, where U is the universe size.
The space complexity is O(U), which is generally considered large.
They require keys to be integers within a fixed range [0, U-1].
They are built by recursively splitting the universe into sqrt(U) clusters.

explanation: vEB trees achieve double-logarithmic time by using a recursive structure that divides the bit-representation of keys. While extremely fast, the O(U) space requirement makes them impractical for very large, sparse universes.

description: Analyze 'Heavy-Light Decomposition' (HLD) on a tree. Which of the following are true?

options.text: It decomposes a tree into a set of disjoint paths.
The number of 'light' edges on a path from the root to any leaf is O(log N).
It allows for path updates and queries in O(log^2 N) time when combined with a Segment Tree.
An edge is 'heavy' if it connects a parent to the child with the largest subtree.

explanation: HLD is an advanced technique for path operations. By ensuring we only cross a logarithmic number of 'light' paths, we can map tree paths to contiguous segments in an array, enabling efficient range queries.

description: Which statements accurately describe 'Link-Cut Trees'?

options.text: They represent a forest of trees and support dynamic 'link' and 'cut' operations.
Internally, they use Splay Trees to represent 'preferred paths'.
All primary operations take O(log N) amortized time.
They can be used to find the LCA of two nodes in a dynamic tree environment.

explanation: Link-Cut trees are a highly advanced dynamic data structure. By partitioning the tree into preferred paths stored as auxiliary Splay trees, they maintain tree connectivity and path information under constant structural changes.

description: Regarding 'Centroid Decomposition', which of the following are true?

options.text: A centroid is a node whose removal splits the tree into components of size at most N/2.
A tree can have at most two centroids.
The resulting 'Centroid Tree' hierarchy has a height of O(log N).
It is used to solve 'Divide and Conquer' problems on tree paths in O(N log N).

explanation: Centroid decomposition allows us to process paths by repeatedly finding the 'center' of subtrees. The logarithmic height of the decomposition tree ensures that each node is processed a logarithmic number of times.

description: Examine 'Suffix Automata' vs 'Suffix Trees'. Which statements are correct?

options.text: A Suffix Automaton is the smallest DFA that recognizes all suffixes of a string.
Both can be constructed in O(N) time for a fixed alphabet.
A Suffix Automaton uses O(N) space and has at most 2N-1 states.
A Suffix Tree is essentially a compressed Trie of all suffixes.

explanation: While they serve similar purposes for string matching, the Automaton is a graph that is often more space-efficient and simpler to implement than Ukkonen's algorithm for Suffix Trees.

description: Regarding 'AA Trees' (Arne Andersson trees), which statements are true?

options.text: They are a variation of Red-Black trees that only allow right-leaning red nodes.
They simplify rebalancing logic by using only two operations: 'Skew' and 'Split'.
Nodes store a 'level' instead of a color.
They do not guarantee O(log N) height.

explanation: AA trees are designed to be easier to implement than standard Red-Black trees. By enforcing that only right children can be at the same 'level' as their parent, they eliminate several balancing cases while maintaining strict O(log N) performance.

description: In '2D Segment Trees', what is true about their complexity?

options.text: Space complexity is O(N log N) if using dynamic allocation or O(N^2).
Query time for a sub-rectangle is O(log^2 N).
A 2D segment tree is essentially a segment tree where each node is another segment tree.
They are the most space-efficient way to handle 2D range sum queries.

explanation: 2D segment trees are powerful for rectangular queries but are memory-intensive. For simple sum queries, a 2D Fenwick tree or a Prefix Sum Matrix is usually preferred for better space efficiency.

description: Regarding 'Euler Tour Trees' (ETT), which statements are correct?

options.text: They represent a tree as a sequence of nodes visited during a DFS traversal.
They can handle 'subtree' queries more easily than Link-Cut Trees.
Dynamic updates (link/cut) involve splitting and merging sequences in O(log N).
They are typically implemented using a Splay Tree or Treap over the Euler sequence.

explanation: ETT maps the tree structure to a linear sequence. This makes subtree operations (which correspond to contiguous ranges in the tour) much simpler to implement compared to preferred-path structures.

description: Analyze 'Scapegoat Trees'. Which of the following statements are correct?

options.text: They are self-balancing BSTs that do not store any extra information (like height) in nodes.
They maintain balance by rebuilding an entire subtree when a 'weight-balance' property is violated.
The amortized cost of insertion and deletion is O(log N).
The search complexity is O(log N) in the worst case.

explanation: Scapegoat trees use the 'brute force' of rebuilding subtrees to stay balanced. They are unique because they achieve balance without the memory overhead of colors, heights, or pointers to parents.

description: Regarding 'Radix Trees' (PATRICIA Tries), what are the advantages?

options.text: They are space-optimized Tries where nodes with only one child are merged.
They are particularly efficient for long strings with shared prefixes.
Searching, insertion, and deletion are O(K), where K is the length of the key.
They are commonly used in IP routing tables.

explanation: Radix trees compress the structure of a Trie by collapsing chains of single-child nodes. This reduces the total number of nodes and edges, making them faster and more memory-efficient for sparse string sets.

description: Identify true statements about 'Finger Trees'.

options.text: They provide amortized O(1) access to elements near the 'fingers' (ends).
They are purely functional data structures.
They are used as the basis for general-purpose sequences in languages like Haskell.
They support splitting and merging in O(log N) time.

explanation: Finger trees are highly versatile. By 'hanging' the tree from its ends, they provide constant time access to the front and back while maintaining logarithmic performance for mid-tree operations.

description: Regarding 'Treaps' and their uniqueness, which are true?

options.text: A Treap is uniquely determined by its (key, priority) pairs if all priorities are distinct.
The expected depth of any node is O(log N).
They support O(log N) 'split' and 'merge' operations.
The priorities must follow the Heap property (usually a Max-Heap).

explanation: Treaps provide a way to build a balanced tree using randomization. Because they are uniquely determined by their priorities, they behave exactly like a BST into which keys were inserted in a random order.

description: Which statements are correct about 'LCT' (Link-Cut Tree) 'Access' operation?

options.text: It makes a path from the root to the target node a 'preferred path'.
It is the building block for all other Link-Cut operations.
After 'access(v)', the node 'v' becomes the root of its auxiliary Splay tree.
The amortized cost of an access operation is O(log N).

explanation: The 'access' operation is the core of Link-Cut trees. It restructures the auxiliary trees so that the path from the root to the node is represented by a single Splay tree, with the target node at the 'bottom' of that path.

description: In 'Ternary Search Trees' (TST), which statements are correct?

options.text: Each node has three children: 'less', 'equal', and 'greater'.
They are more space-efficient than Tries for large alphabets.
Searching is O(L + log N), where L is the length of the string.
They combine the properties of BSTs and Tries.

explanation: TSTs avoid the large array of pointers found in Trie nodes. Instead, they use a BST structure at each character level, branching based on character comparisons. This makes them much leaner for wide alphabets like Unicode.

description: Analyze 'R-Trees' used for spatial indexing. Which are true?

options.text: They group nearby objects and represent them by their 'Minimum Bounding Rectangles' (MBR).
Nodes can overlap, which means a search may require exploring multiple paths.
They are balanced trees, similar to B-Trees.
They are used for both point and area queries.

explanation: R-trees are the standard for geo-spatial data. Unlike BSTs, where one path is taken, R-tree searches might need to branch into multiple MBRs if the query region overlaps with multiple sibling nodes.

description: Regarding 'Order Statistic Trees', what extra info is stored?

options.text: Each node stores the size of its subtree.
It allows finding the 'i-th' smallest element in O(log N) time.
It allows finding the 'rank' of an element in O(log N) time.
It is usually implemented by augmenting a balanced BST.

explanation: By augmenting a BST with subtree sizes, we can efficiently 'index' the tree. This turns a search tree into a sequence-like structure that still supports logarithmic updates.

description: What are the true statements about 'Palindromic Trees' (Eertree)?

options.text: They find all distinct palindromic substrings in O(N) time.
The tree has two roots representing palindromes of even and odd lengths.
Each node (except roots) represents a unique palindrome.
The number of nodes is at most N+2.

explanation: Eertrees are a specialized structure for palindrome analysis. They use 'suffix links' (pointing to the longest palindromic suffix) to achieve linear construction time.

description: Regarding the 'Matrix Tree Theorem', what does it count?

options.text: The number of spanning trees in a graph.
It uses the 'Laplacian Matrix' of the graph.
It involves calculating the determinant of a cofactor of the matrix.
It only works for undirected graphs.

explanation: This theorem bridges graph theory and linear algebra. By computing the determinant of a reduced Laplacian matrix, one can find the exact count of all possible spanning trees for a graph.

description: Which statements are true about 'Crit-bit Trees'?

options.text: They are a type of space-efficient bitwise Trie.
They only store internal nodes for 'critical bits' where keys differ.
They are immune to certain buffer overflow attacks compared to standard Tries.
Search time depends on the length of the keys in bits.

explanation: Crit-bit trees (a variant of Radix/Patricia trees) are popular in security-conscious networking code. They avoid storing full keys in internal nodes, branching only where bits actually diverge.

description: In 'Dynamic Programming' on Trees, which are true?

options.text: It usually involves a DFS traversal.
States are typically defined as 'DP[u]' representing a value for the subtree rooted at u.
It can solve the 'Maximum Independent Set' problem on trees in O(N).
It requires the tree to be binary.

explanation: Tree DP is a powerful technique. Since trees have no cycles and a clear parent-child hierarchy, many NP-hard problems on general graphs (like Max Independent Set) become solvable in linear time on trees.
