description: Which of the following are the fundamental components used to define a Graph in TypeScript?

options.text: Vertices (Nodes)
Edges (Links)
A required Root node
Arrows (for directed graphs)

explanation: A graph is defined as a set of vertices and edges. Unlike trees, graphs do not require a root node. In directed graphs, edges have a specific direction often represented by arrows.

description: Regarding 'Undirected Graphs', which statements are correct?

options.text: Edges have no orientation (direction).
If an edge exists between A and B, you can travel from A to B and B to A.
The adjacency matrix is always symmetric.
Edges are represented by ordered pairs (u, v).

explanation: In undirected graphs, edges are bidirectional. This symmetry means that if node i is connected to j, then j is connected to i, making the adjacency matrix symmetric across the diagonal.

description: What are true about the 'Adjacency Matrix' representation of a graph?

options.text: It uses a 2D array of size V x V.
Checking if an edge exists between two nodes takes O(1) time.
It is space-efficient for sparse graphs.
Adding a new vertex is an expensive O(V^2) operation.

explanation: Adjacency matrices provide constant time edge lookups. However, they consume O(V^2) space regardless of how many edges exist, making them inefficient for sparse graphs with few connections.

description: Identify the characteristics of an 'Adjacency List'.

options.text: It is usually implemented as an array of arrays or a Map.
It is more space-efficient than a matrix for sparse graphs.
Finding all neighbors of a specific vertex is efficient.
Checking for a specific edge (u, v) is always O(1).

explanation: Adjacency lists store only existing edges. While they save space and are great for finding neighbors, checking for a specific edge might require traversing a list of neighbors, which is O(degree(u)).
code:
const adjList = new Map<number, number[]>();
adjList.set(1, [2, 3]);

description: Which of these are standard algorithms for traversing a graph?

options.text: Breadth-First Search (BFS)
Depth-First Search (DFS)
Binary Search
In-order Traversal

explanation: BFS and DFS are the primary ways to visit all nodes in a graph. In-order traversal is specific to trees, and Binary Search is for sorted arrays.

description: Regarding Breadth-First Search (BFS), which statements are true?

options.text: It uses a Queue (FIFO) data structure.
It explores neighbors layer by layer (outward from source).
It finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph.
It is typically implemented using recursion.

explanation: BFS radiates from the start node using a queue. Because it explores all nodes at distance 1, then distance 2, etc., it naturally finds the shortest path in terms of edge count.

description: Regarding Depth-First Search (DFS), which statements are correct?

options.text: It can be implemented using a Stack (LIFO).
It can be implemented naturally using recursion.
It explores as far as possible along a branch before backtracking.
It always finds the shortest path.

explanation: DFS dives deep into the graph. It uses the call stack (via recursion) or an explicit stack but does not guarantee the shortest path.

description: What are true about 'Directed Acyclic Graphs' (DAG)?

options.text: Edges have directions.
There are no cycles (loops).
They are commonly used to represent dependencies or task scheduling.
Topological sorting can be performed on them.

explanation: A DAG is a directed graph where you cannot start at any vertex and follow directed edges back to the same vertex. This property allows for a linear ordering of tasks (topological sort).

description: What does the 'Degree' of a vertex refer to?

options.text: In an undirected graph, the number of edges incident to it.
In a directed graph, the sum of 'in-degree' and 'out-degree'.
The weight of the vertex.
The distance of the vertex from the root.

explanation: Degree is a count of connections. For directed graphs, we specify 'in' (coming in) and 'out' (going out), while for undirected, it's just the total count.

description: Identify true statements about 'Weighted Graphs'.

options.text: Each edge has an associated numerical value (weight).
Weights can represent distance, cost, or time.
A graph without weights is essentially a weighted graph where all weights are 1.
Weights must always be positive integers.

explanation: Weighted graphs quantify connections. While many algorithms (like Dijkstra) assume positive weights, graphs can have negative or floating-point weights depending on the application.

description: Which of these describe a 'Connected' undirected graph?

options.text: There is a path between every pair of vertices.
The graph consists of a single connected component.
Every vertex must be connected directly to every other vertex.
Starting BFS from any node will visit all nodes.

explanation: Connectivity means you can get from A to B for any A and B. It does not require direct edges (which would be a 'Complete' graph).

description: What is true about 'Cycles' in a graph?

options.text: A cycle is a path that starts and ends at the same vertex.
A 'Self-loop' is a cycle of length 1.
A tree is a connected graph with no cycles.
Cycles are only possible in directed graphs.

explanation: Cycles are loops. Trees are specifically defined as acyclic connected graphs. Cycles occur in both directed and undirected graphs.

description: Regarding graph representation in TypeScript, which are common practices?

options.text: Using an object or Map as an adjacency list: `Map<K, V[]>`.
Representing vertices as class instances.
Using a 1D array to represent a flattened matrix.
Using a Linked List to store vertices.

explanation: Maps and objects are the most common way to build adjacency lists in TS. Class instances help if vertices have complex metadata.

description: Identify true statements about the 'Visited' set in graph traversals.

options.text: It prevents the algorithm from processing the same node multiple times.
It is essential for preventing infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
It can be implemented using a `Set<T>` or a boolean array.
It is not needed for DAGs.

explanation: Since graphs can have cycles or multiple paths to the same node, a 'visited' tracker ensures each node is processed once, even in DAGs.
code: const visited = new Set<number>();

description: What are true about 'Complete Graphs'?

options.text: Every pair of distinct vertices is connected by a unique edge.
A complete graph with n vertices has n(n-1)/2 edges (undirected).
They are represented as K_n in graph theory.
A complete graph is always a tree.

explanation: Complete graphs have maximum connectivity. Because they are full of cycles (for n > 2), they are almost never trees.

description: In a directed graph, what does 'In-degree' represent?

options.text: The number of edges pointing towards a vertex.
The number of edges pointing away from a vertex.
For a source node, the in-degree is usually 0.
It can be found by summing a column in an adjacency matrix.

explanation: In-degree is incoming traffic. In a matrix where rows are sources and columns are destinations, the sum of a column gives the in-degree of that vertex.

description: Which of these are true about 'Path' vs 'Walk'?

options.text: A 'Walk' is any sequence of vertices and edges.
A 'Path' is a walk where no vertices are repeated.
Every path is a walk.
A walk cannot have repeating edges.

explanation: A walk is the most general term. A path is a specific type of walk that is 'simple' (no vertex repetition). Walks can repeat edges and vertices.

description: Regarding 'Disconnected' graphs, which are true?

options.text: They contain at least two connected components.
A single traversal (BFS/DFS) will not visit all vertices.
There exists at least one pair of vertices with no path between them.
They are only possible in directed graphs.

explanation: Disconnected graphs have 'islands'. One traversal starting from one island cannot jump to another. This is common in both undirected and directed graphs.

description: What is true about 'Self-loops' and 'Multi-edges'?

options.text: A self-loop is an edge from a vertex to itself.
Multi-edges are multiple edges between the same two vertices.
A 'Simple Graph' allows neither self-loops nor multi-edges.
They are illegal in all graph theory.

explanation: While 'Simple Graphs' forbid them, 'Multigraphs' and 'Pseudographs' use them to represent complex relationships (like multiple bus routes between two stops).

description: Regarding the complexity of graph traversals with an Adjacency List, which are true?

options.text: Time complexity is O(V + E).
Space complexity is O(V) for the visited set and recursion/queue.
Complexity depends on the number of edges.
Time complexity is O(V^2).

explanation: With an adjacency list, we visit every vertex once and check every edge once, resulting in linear O(V+E) time. O(V^2) is typical for Adjacency Matrices.

description: Regarding 'Dijkstra's Algorithm' in TypeScript, which statements are true about its constraints and implementation?

options.text: It finds the shortest path from a single source to all other vertices.
It works correctly even if the graph contains negative edge weights.
It typically uses a Priority Queue to efficiently select the next minimum distance node.
Its time complexity with a binary heap is O((V + E) log V).

explanation: Dijkstra's is a greedy algorithm. It fails with negative weights because it assumes that once a node is 'visited', its shortest path is finalized—an assumption broken by negative edges. Using a Priority Queue ensures we always expand the frontier from the nearest discovered node.
code: const pq = new PriorityQueue<Node>((a, b) => a.dist < b.dist);

description: Identify true statements about 'Topological Sorting' for directed graphs.

options.text: It is a linear ordering of vertices such that for every directed edge (u, v), u comes before v.
It can only be performed on Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs).
Kahn's algorithm uses in-degrees and a queue to produce the sort.
A graph can have multiple valid topological orderings.

explanation: Topological sorting is essential for task scheduling. If a graph has a cycle, there is no valid linear ordering. Kahn's algorithm works by repeatedly removing nodes with an in-degree of 0.

description: Regarding 'Minimum Spanning Trees' (MST), which of the following are true?

options.text: Kruskal's algorithm uses a Disjoint Set Union (DSU) to efficiently detect cycles.
Prim's algorithm grows a single tree by adding the cheapest edge to an unvisited vertex.
An MST must connect all vertices with the minimum possible total edge weight.
If all edge weights are unique, the MST is also unique.

explanation: MST algorithms are used for network design. Kruskal's is edge-based (sorting edges first), while Prim's is vertex-based (starting from a node and expanding). Unique weights guarantee a single possible MST structure.

description: How does 'Bellman-Ford' compare to Dijkstra's algorithm?

options.text: Bellman-Ford can handle negative edge weights.
Bellman-Ford can detect the presence of negative weight cycles.
Bellman-Ford is faster than Dijkstra for dense graphs.
Bellman-Ford works by relaxing all edges exactly V-1 times.

explanation: Bellman-Ford is more robust but slower (O(V*E)). It works by iteratively improving path estimates. A V-th iteration that still results in a shorter path indicates a negative cycle exists.

description: Regarding 'Strongly Connected Components' (SCC) in directed graphs, which are correct?

options.text: In an SCC, every vertex is reachable from every other vertex in that component.
Kosaraju's algorithm finds SCCs using two separate DFS passes.
Tarjan's algorithm finds SCCs in a single DFS pass using discovery and low-link values.
A Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) always has exactly one SCC.

explanation: SCCs identify cycles and clusters in directed graphs. In a DAG, there are no cycles, so every individual vertex is its own SCC (meaning a DAG with V vertices has V SCCs).

description: What is true about 'Articulation Points' and 'Bridges' in an undirected graph?

options.text: An articulation point is a vertex whose removal increases the number of connected components.
A bridge is an edge whose removal increases the number of connected components.
Both can be found in O(V + E) time using a single DFS traversal.
A node in a cycle cannot be an articulation point.

explanation: These represent 'single points of failure' in network connectivity. DFS allows us to calculate low-link values to see if a subgraph has any back-edges to ancestors; if not, the node/edge is critical.

description: Analyze the 'Floyd-Warshall' algorithm. Which statements are true?

options.text: It solves the 'All-Pairs Shortest Path' problem.
It uses dynamic programming.
The time complexity is O(V^3).
It is best suited for sparse graphs with an Adjacency List.

explanation: Floyd-Warshall is a dense graph algorithm typically implemented using an adjacency matrix. It iterates through every possible intermediate node 'k' to see if path i -> k -> j is shorter than the current i -> j.
code: dist[i][j] = Math.min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]);

description: Which are true regarding 'Eulerian' paths and circuits?

options.text: An Eulerian Circuit visits every edge exactly once and returns to the start.
In an undirected graph, an Eulerian Circuit exists if every vertex has an even degree.
An Eulerian Path exists if exactly zero or two vertices have an odd degree.
Eulerian properties are identical to Hamiltonian properties.

explanation: Eulerian is about edges; Hamiltonian is about vertices. A connected undirected graph has an Eulerian circuit if all vertices have even degrees, ensuring you can 'enter' and 'exit' every node until all edges are used.

description: Identify true statements about 'Disjoint Set Union' (DSU/Union-Find) with optimizations.

options.text: 'Path Compression' makes future 'find' operations faster.
'Union by Rank' or 'Union by Size' keeps the tree structure flat.
With both optimizations, operations take nearly constant O(α(V)) time.
DSU can only be used for tree data structures, not general graphs.

explanation: DSU is the engine behind Kruskal's MST and is used to track connected components in a graph. Path compression flattens the tree during searches, while Union by Rank ensures smaller trees are attached under larger ones.

description: Analyze 'A* Search' and its heuristic function h(n). Which are correct?

options.text: If h(n) is 'admissible', A* is guaranteed to find the shortest path.
An admissible heuristic never overestimates the actual cost to the goal.
If h(n) is always 0, A* becomes Dijkstra's algorithm.
A* is generally more efficient than BFS for finding paths in large grids.

explanation: A* is an 'informed' search. By using a heuristic (like Manhattan distance) to estimate the remaining distance, it prunes the search space. If the heuristic is 0, the algorithm doesn't 'know' anything about the goal and behaves like Dijkstra.

description: Regarding 'Graph Coloring', which statements are correct?

options.text: The 'Chromatic Number' is the minimum number of colors needed to color a graph so no adjacent vertices share a color.
A Bipartite graph always has a chromatic number of 2.
Finding the Chromatic Number of a general graph is an NP-hard problem.
Any planar graph can be colored with at most 4 colors.

explanation: Graph coloring has many applications in register allocation and map coloring. The Four Color Theorem for planar graphs and the definition of Bipartite graphs (2-colorable) are fundamental results.

description: What are true regarding 'Maximum Flow' in networks?

options.text: The Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem states that the max flow equals the capacity of the minimum cut.
Ford-Fulkerson is a method for finding max flow using augmenting paths.
Edmonds-Karp is an implementation of Ford-Fulkerson that uses BFS to find paths.
Flow through an edge can exceed its capacity if there is no other path.

explanation: Network flow models liquid through pipes or data through cables. Edmonds-Karp ensures a polynomial time complexity by always picking the shortest path (in terms of number of edges) via BFS.

description: Identify true statements about 'Graph Isomorphism'.

options.text: Two graphs are isomorphic if they have the same number of vertices and edges.
Isomorphism means there is a bijection between vertex sets that preserves adjacency.
If two graphs have different degree sequences, they cannot be isomorphic.
Isomorphism testing is a known polynomial-time problem (P).

explanation: Isomorphic graphs are structurally identical but may look different. While same V, E, and degrees are necessary, they aren't sufficient. The exact complexity of the Graph Isomorphism problem is a famous open question, residing between P and NP-Complete.

description: Regarding 'Johnson's Algorithm' for all-pairs shortest paths, which are correct?

options.text: It is more efficient than Floyd-Warshall for sparse graphs.
It uses reweighting to handle negative edges without changing shortest paths.
It uses both Bellman-Ford and Dijkstra's algorithm.
It works by running Dijkstra V times after reweighting the graph.

explanation: Johnson's uses Bellman-Ford once to find 'potentials' for each vertex, then reweights all edges to be non-negative. This allows running the faster Dijkstra's algorithm from every node as a source.

description: Which statements are true about 'Small World' networks (Social Graphs)?

options.text: They typically have a low average path length between any two nodes.
They often show a high 'Clustering Coefficient'.
They usually follow a 'Power Law' degree distribution (Scale-free).
They are best represented using a dense adjacency matrix.

explanation: Social networks are sparse (most people don't know each other) but have 'hubs' (celebrities/influencers) that bridge gaps, leading to the 'six degrees of separation' phenomenon. Adjacency lists are much better for storing these sparse structures.

description: Regarding 'Bipartite Matching' using the 'Hopcroft-Karp' algorithm, which are true?

options.text: It finds the maximum matching in a bipartite graph.
It is faster than the standard Ford-Fulkerson approach for matching.
It uses both BFS and DFS concepts to find multiple augmenting paths per phase.
The time complexity is O(E * sqrt(V)).

explanation: Hopcroft-Karp is an optimized matching algorithm. By finding a maximal set of shortest augmenting paths in each phase (using BFS then DFS), it reaches the maximum matching significantly faster than simple flow algorithms.

description: Analyze 'Bidirectional BFS'. Which statements are correct?

options.text: It runs two simultaneous searches: one forward from the start and one backward from the goal.
It can drastically reduce the number of explored nodes in large graphs.
It requires the goal state to be known explicitly.
The search terminates when the two frontiers intersect.

explanation: Instead of searching one large circle of radius D (area πD²), bidirectional BFS searches two smaller circles of radius D/2 (area 2 * π(D/2)²), which is roughly half the search area.

description: What are true regarding 'Planar Graphs' and Euler's Formula?

options.text: A graph is planar if it can be drawn in a 2D plane with no edges crossing.
Euler's Formula for connected planar graphs is: V - E + F = 2.
A simple planar graph with V >= 3 vertices can have at most 3V - 6 edges.
The complete graph K5 is a planar graph.

explanation: Planarity is a topological property. K5 (5 nodes, all connected) and K3,3 (utility graph) are the fundamental non-planar graphs according to Kuratowski's theorem.

description: Regarding 'Graph Centrality' measures, which descriptions are correct?

options.text: 'Degree Centrality' identifies nodes with the most direct connections.
'Betweenness Centrality' identifies nodes that act as bridges along shortest paths.
'Closeness Centrality' identifies nodes that can reach all other nodes quickly.
'PageRank' is a form of centrality used by search engines.

explanation: Centrality measures 'importance'. Degree is simple connection count; Betweenness counts how much 'traffic' passes through a node; Closeness is based on average distance; PageRank evaluates importance based on links from other important nodes.

description: What is true about 'Hamiltonian' paths and circuits?

options.text: A Hamiltonian Path visits every vertex exactly once.
Finding a Hamiltonian Path is an NP-complete problem.
The 'Traveling Salesperson Problem' (TSP) is the weighted version of the Hamiltonian Circuit problem.
Any graph with an Eulerian Circuit also has a Hamiltonian Circuit.

explanation: While Eulerian paths (edges) are easy to find, Hamiltonian paths (vertices) are computationally difficult (NP-Complete). There is no simple relationship between the two; a graph can have one, both, or neither.

description: Analyze the provided implementation of Tarjan's algorithm for finding Strongly Connected Components (SCCs). Which statements are true?

options.text: The 'lowLink' value represents the smallest discovery time reachable from the node in the current DFS tree, including nodes on the stack.
A node is the root of an SCC if and only if its discovery time equals its lowLink value.
Nodes are pushed onto a stack and popped only when an SCC root is identified during the backtracking phase.
It requires two DFS passes, unlike Kosaraju's algorithm which requires only one.

explanation: Tarjan's algorithm is a single-pass DFS-based approach. It uses discovery times and 'low-link' values to identify the roots of SCCs. Nodes are kept on a stack to track members of the current potential component.

description: Regarding 'Dinic's Algorithm' for Maximum Flow, which statements are true about its performance and structure?

options.text: It uses 'Level Graphs' constructed via BFS to find the shortest augmenting paths.
It finds 'Blocking Flows' within each level graph using DFS.
The worst-case time complexity on general graphs is O(V^2 E).
On unit networks (all capacities are 1), it runs in O(E * min(V^(2/3), E^(1/2))).

explanation: Dinic's is a sophisticated max-flow algorithm. It improves upon Edmonds-Karp by processing multiple paths simultaneously in layers (Level Graphs) and finding a blocking flow, which leads to better theoretical and practical performance.

description: In the context of 'Planar Graphs' and Kuratowski's Theorem, which of the following are true?

options.text: A graph is planar if and only if it does not contain a subgraph homeomorphic to K5 or K3,3.
For any simple planar graph with V >= 3, the number of edges E satisfies E <= 3V - 6.
The 'dual graph' of a planar graph is also guaranteed to be planar.
Checking if a graph is planar can be done in O(V) time using algorithms like Boyer-Myrvold.

explanation: Planarity is a deep topological property. Kuratowski's identifies the core forbidden structures. The linear edge-bound and linear-time planarity testing are key results for algorithmic graph theory.

description: Regarding the 'Blossom Algorithm' for Maximum Matching in general graphs, which are correct?

options.text: It handles 'odd cycles' by shrinking them into a single super-node (a blossom).
It is needed because standard alternating path methods fail when an odd cycle is encountered.
The algorithm runs in O(V^2 E) or better time complexity.
It is only applicable to bipartite graphs.

explanation: Edmonds' Blossom algorithm is the classic solution for maximum matching in general (non-bipartite) graphs. The 'blossom' technique correctly navigates the parity issues caused by odd cycles.

description: Analyze '2-SAT' problems and their reduction to Graph Theory. Which statements are true?

options.text: A 2-SAT instance is modeled as an 'Implication Graph' where each clause (A v B) becomes (!A -> B) and (!B -> A).
The instance is unsatisfiable if and only if some variable X and its negation !X are in the same SCC.
It can be solved in linear O(V + E) time using SCC algorithms like Tarjan's or Kosaraju's.
A valid assignment can be found by processing SCCs in reverse topological order.

explanation: 2-SAT is a subset of boolean satisfiability that is efficiently solvable. By building an implication graph, we can use graph connectivity to determine if any logical contradictions (X being both true and false) exist.

description: Regarding 'Minimum Cut' algorithms, which statements are accurate?

options.text: The Stoer-Wagner algorithm finds the global minimum cut in an undirected graph without using max-flow.
The Karger's algorithm is a randomized approach that finds the global min-cut by repeatedly contracting edges.
An s-t minimum cut is always unique for any given graph.
The capacity of a cut is the sum of the weights of the edges crossing from the source set to the sink set.

explanation: Min-cut identifies the most vulnerable links in a network. Stoer-Wagner and Karger's focus on the global min-cut (any split), while max-flow algorithms typically find the s-t min-cut (split between two specific nodes).

description: Identify true statements about 'Heavy-Light Decomposition' (HLD) on a tree.

options.text: It allows for path queries and path updates in O(log^2 N) time.
It linearizes a tree into an array such that any path consists of O(log N) contiguous segments.
A 'heavy edge' connects a node to its child with the largest number of descendants.
It is used to transform tree path problems into segment tree or Fenwick tree problems.

explanation: HLD is a powerful technique for tree data structures. By carefully selecting 'heavy' paths, any path from root to leaf can be represented by a small number of array intervals, allowing standard array structures to handle tree queries.

description: Regarding 'Link-Cut Trees', which statements are correct?

options.text: They support dynamic tree operations (link, cut, path aggregates) in amortized O(log N) time.
The underlying tree is partitioned into 'preferred paths'.
Preferred paths are stored in auxiliary 'Splay Trees', keyed by depth.
They are specifically designed to handle undirected graph connectivity without deletions.

explanation: Link-Cut trees are the gold standard for dynamic tree problems. They maintain a forest of trees that can change structure (linking subtrees or cutting edges) while providing fast path-based queries.

description: What is true about 'Chu-Liu/Edmonds' Algorithm'?

options.text: It finds the 'Minimum Arborescence' (MST for directed graphs) from a root.
It works by greedily picking the cheapest incoming edge for each node.
Cycles formed during the greedy phase are 'contracted' into super-nodes recursively.
It is identical to Prim's algorithm in terms of edge selection.

explanation: Standard MST algorithms (Prim/Kruskal) fail on directed graphs because they don't account for the requirement that every node must have exactly one parent. Edmonds' algorithm specifically addresses cycle resolution in directed trees.

description: Identify true statements about 'Centroid Decomposition' of a tree.

options.text: A centroid is a node whose removal leaves components of size no more than N/2.
The decomposition creates a 'Centroid Tree' with a guaranteed height of O(log N).
It is used for solving tree problems involving path properties (e.g., counting paths with length K).
Finding the centroid of a tree takes O(N log N) time.

explanation: Centroid decomposition is a divide-and-conquer strategy for trees. By finding the centroid in O(N) and recurring, we build a hierarchy that allows us to process all paths effectively in O(N log N) total time.

description: Regarding 'Graph Dominators' in a flowgraph, which are correct?

options.text: Node 'd' dominates 'n' if every path from the start node to 'n' must pass through 'd'.
The 'Immediate Dominator' (idom) is the unique dominator of 'n' closest to 'n'.
Dominator trees are used in compiler optimizations (e.g., SSA form).
Lengauer-Tarjan is an efficient O(E alpha(V)) algorithm to find all dominators.

explanation: Dominance is a foundational concept for analyzing control flow. It allows compilers to understand the structure of loops and dependencies by identifying 'bottleneck' nodes in the execution path.

description: In the context of 'Random Graphs' (Erdős–Rényi G(n, p)), which are true?

options.text: At p = 1/n, a 'Giant Component' of size O(n) typically emerges.
The connectivity threshold for a graph to become connected is at p = (ln n) / n.
For very small p, the graph is almost certainly a collection of disjoint trees.
The degree distribution follows a Poisson distribution for large n.

explanation: Random graph theory studies phase transitions. As p increases, the structural properties of the graph shift dramatically at specific 'threshold' probabilities.

description: Which statements are correct regarding 'Graph Treewidth'?

options.text: It measures how 'tree-like' a graph is.
Many NP-hard problems (like Max Clique) become solvable in polynomial time on graphs with fixed treewidth.
A tree has a treewidth of 1.
The treewidth of a complete graph Kn is n - 1.

explanation: Treewidth is a parameter from Graph Minor theory. If a graph has low treewidth, it can be decomposed into a 'tree' of clusters, allowing dynamic programming techniques to work efficiently.

description: Regarding 'K-th Shortest Path' algorithms, which are true?

options.text: Yen's algorithm finds K-th shortest simple paths in O(K V (E + V log V)).
Eppstein's algorithm finds K-th shortest paths (potentially with cycles) in O(E + V log V + K).
Finding the K-th shortest 'simple' path is significantly harder than allowing cycles.
Dijkstra can find the K-th shortest path by allowing each node to be visited K times.

explanation: The 'simple path' constraint (no repeated vertices) makes K-th shortest path computation much more complex. Eppstein's algorithm is a remarkable result that uses a persistent heap to find paths in near-optimal time.

description: Analyze 'Expander Graphs'. Which properties are correct?

options.text: They are sparse graphs with high connectivity.
The 'Spectral Gap' between the largest and second-largest eigenvalues of the adjacency matrix is large.
Random walks on expander graphs converge very quickly to the stationary distribution.
Every subset of vertices has a large number of neighbors outside the subset.

explanation: Expanders are highly important in computer science (cryptography, coding theory, and network design). They look like random graphs in terms of connectivity but can be constructed deterministically.

description: Regarding 'Gomory-Hu Trees', what do they represent?

options.text: They represent all possible s-t minimum cuts in an undirected graph.
The min-cut between any u and v is the minimum weight edge on the path u-v in the Gomory-Hu tree.
Constructing it requires only V - 1 max-flow computations.
It exists for both directed and undirected graphs.

explanation: Gomory-Hu trees are a powerful compression of the cut structure of a graph. Instead of computing V^2 flows, we only need V flows to determine the bottleneck capacity between every single pair of nodes.

description: In the context of 'Network Simplex', which statements are correct?

options.text: It is a specialized version of the Simplex algorithm for Minimum Cost Network Flow.
It maintains a 'Spanning Tree Basis' of the graph.
It is often faster in practice than general linear programming solvers for flow problems.
It is guaranteed to be a polynomial-time algorithm in all theoretical models.

explanation: Network Simplex is highly efficient for flow problems. While theoretically exponential (like general Simplex), its practical performance and specialized graph-based logic make it a standard for min-cost flow.

description: Analyze 'Graph Homomorphisms'. Which are true?

options.text: A homomorphism from G to H is a mapping that preserves adjacency (if (u,v) in E(G) then (f(u),f(v)) in E(H)).
Graph k-coloring is equivalent to finding a homomorphism to the complete graph Kk.
The existence of a homomorphism between two graphs is NP-complete.
If G and H are isomorphic, there exists a bijective homomorphism between them.

explanation: Homomorphisms generalize graph coloring and isomorphism. They study whether the structural constraints of one graph can be 'mapped' into another without violating the edge structure.

description: Regarding 'Chordal Graphs', what are the characteristics?

options.text: Every cycle of length 4 or more has a 'chord' (an edge connecting non-adjacent nodes in the cycle).
They are exactly the graphs that have a 'Perfect Elimination Ordering'.
The Maximum Clique and Chromatic Number can be found in polynomial time for chordal graphs.
They are a subclass of Perfect Graphs.

explanation: Chordal graphs have a rigid structure that makes many NP-hard problems solvable in linear time. They are often studied in the context of Gaussian elimination and sparse matrix factorizations.

description: Identify true statements about the 'Handshaking Lemma' in Graph Theory.

options.text: The sum of the degrees of all vertices is equal to twice the number of edges.
Every undirected graph must have an even number of vertices with odd degrees.
In a directed graph, the sum of in-degrees equals the sum of out-degrees.
It applies only to simple graphs without self-loops.

explanation: The Handshaking Lemma is a fundamental counting result. Since every edge has two endpoints, it must contribute exactly twice to the total sum of degrees, forcing the number of 'odd' nodes to be even.

description: The following code is intended to print the value of 'x', but it causes a compilation error. Identify the fix.

options.text: Change '%d' to '%f' in the printf statement.
Add a semicolon at the end of the 'int x = 10' line.
Initialize 'x' inside the printf function.
Remove the 'return 0;' statement.

explanation: In C, every statement must be terminated with a semicolon. The declaration of 'x' is missing one, preventing the compiler from moving to the next instruction.

description: This program should calculate the average of two numbers, but it always prints 'Average: 5.000000'. What is the bug?

options.text: The sum should be divided by 2.0 to force floating-point division.
The variables 'a' and 'b' must be declared as floats.
The printf format specifier should be '%d'.
The return type of main must be float.

explanation: In C, 'int / int' results in an integer. '11 / 2' is 5. By dividing by '2.0', the expression is promoted to a float, resulting in 5.5.

description: The programmer wants to read an integer from the user, but the program crashes during execution. Why?

options.text: Scanf cannot read integers; use gets() instead.
The variable 'num' was not initialized to zero.
Scanf needs the memory address of 'num', so it should be '&num'.
The format specifier '%d' is incorrect for integers.

explanation: The 'scanf' function requires a pointer to the variable where the data will be stored. Passing 'num' sends the value (which is garbage), causing scanf to try writing to an invalid memory address.

description: The code is intended to print 'Hello' if x is 5, but it prints 'Hello' regardless of the value of x. Fix the bug.

options.text: Change 'x = 5' to 'x == 5' in the if condition.
Add a semicolon after the if statement.
Put 'Hello' in single quotes.
Initialize x with a value other than 10.

explanation: The '=' operator is for assignment. The expression 'x = 5' assigns 5 to x and then evaluates to 5, which is 'truthy' in C. The '==' operator should be used for comparison.

description: The program is supposed to print numbers 1 and 2, but it results in an infinite loop. Identify the error.

options.text: The i++ should be replaced with i = i + 1.
The while loop condition is always false.
Remove the semicolon after the 'while' condition.
Declare 'i' as a float.

explanation: A semicolon immediately after the while condition creates an empty loop body. The loop checks the condition, does nothing, and repeats forever because 'i' is never incremented.

description: The code below fails to compile. Which fix will resolve the error regarding the PI constant?

options.text: Remove the 'const' keyword.
Change 'float' to 'int'.
Remove the line 'PI = 3.14159;' because constants cannot be reassigned.
Use '#define PI 3.14' inside the main function.

explanation: Variables marked with 'const' are read-only after their initial declaration. Any attempt to modify the value later in the code will result in a compilation error.

description: This code aims to store and print the string 'C Programming', but it prints garbage or crashes. Why?

options.text: Strings must be declared as 'string str;' in C.
You cannot assign a string literal to an already declared array using '='.
The array size is too small.
Printf needs '%s' to be in single quotes.

explanation: In C, you cannot use the assignment operator '=' to copy a string into a character array after it has been declared. You must use 'strcpy()' or initialize it at the moment of declaration.

description: The user expects the program to print 'Value is 10', but it produces a compilation error. What is wrong?

options.text: Variables cannot start with an underscore.
The printf statement is missing a comma before the variable name.
The variable name contains a space, which is illegal.
You must use 'var' to declare integers.

explanation: C variable names must be contiguous. Spaces are not allowed in identifier names. To fix this, use 'my_value' or 'myValue'.

description: The program should print 'Equal' if x is 10, but it fails to compile. Identify the syntax error.

options.text: The 'if' condition must be enclosed in parentheses.
The 'if' keyword should be capitalized.
There should be no space between 'if' and the condition.
The printf statement needs a semicolon inside the brackets.

explanation: In C, the condition for 'if', 'while', and 'for' must be wrapped in parentheses '()'.

description: This function is intended to double a number, but 'num' in main remains 5. What is the bug?

options.text: Functions cannot modify variables from main.
The function uses call-by-value; it should use pointers to modify the original variable.
The function return type should be int.
The multiplication operator is used incorrectly.

explanation: C uses call-by-value by default. The function 'doubleIt' receives a copy of 'num'. Changing the copy does not affect the original variable in main.

description: The program below is intended to print the characters 'A' and 'B', but it fails to compile. What is the fix?

options.text: Change double quotes to single quotes for character literals.
Use '%s' as the format specifier.
The variable names cannot be single letters.
Remove the 'char' keyword.

explanation: In C, string literals use double quotes (""), but individual character literals must use single quotes ('').

description: This switch statement prints 'Option ADefault'. How can we make it print only 'Option A'?

options.text: Change 'case 'A'' to 'case A'.
Add a 'break;' statement after the first printf.
The default case should be at the top.
Switch statements only work with integers, not characters.

explanation: Switch cases 'fall through' in C. If you don't use a 'break' statement, the execution continues into the next case regardless of the value.

description: Why does the following code print a large random number instead of 0?

options.text: The variable 'count' was not initialized to 0.
The increment 'count++' is not working.
Integers cannot be printed with '%d'.
The main function should return void.

explanation: In C, local variables are not initialized to zero automatically; they contain 'garbage values' from previous memory use. Always initialize variables before reading them.

description: The program should print 'Success', but it fails to compile due to the header. Fix it.

options.text: Change '#include stdio.h' to '#include <stdio.h>'.
The header should be '#import <stdio.h>'.
The extension should be '.c' instead of '.h'.
Remove the # symbol.

explanation: Pre-processor directives like #include require the library name to be enclosed in angle brackets '<>' or double quotes "".

description: The programmer wants to multiply 5 by 2 using a macro. Why does the code print 11 instead of 15?

options.text: Macros cannot perform addition.
The macro should be defined as '#define MULTIPLY(x, y) ((x) * (y))'.
The result is correct because 5 + 3 * 2 follows operator precedence (3*2 = 6, 6+5 = 11).
Macros only work with floats.

explanation: Macros perform simple text substitution. MULTIPLY(5, 3) becomes '5 + 3 * 2'. Due to operator precedence, multiplication happens first. Using parentheses in the macro definition fix this.

description: This program should calculate the area of a rectangle, but it fails to compile. What is the bug?

options.text: The variables must be declared before they are used.
Printf cannot use two variables at once.
The 'int area' line must come after length and width are assigned.
C does not support the 'int' data type.

explanation: C is a procedural language; code is executed line-by-line. Variables must be defined before they are used in expressions.

description: The program should print 'Hello' three times, but it prints nothing. Why?

options.text: The loop condition 'i > 3' is immediately false because i starts at 0.
The increment 'i++' is missing.
The loop should start at 1.
The printf statement is incorrect.

explanation: The loop condition 'i > 3' is checked at the start. Since 0 is not greater than 3, the loop body never executes. It should be 'i < 3'.

description: The following code is meant to print 'Valid', but it fails to compile. What is the error in the logical OR operator?

options.text: The operator for OR is '||', not 'OR'.
C does not support logical operators in if statements.
The variable x must be 0.
The printf needs a newline character.

explanation: C uses symbols for logical operations: '&&' for AND, '||' for OR, and '!' for NOT. The word 'OR' is not a keyword.

description: The program is intended to print 'Value is 5', but it prints 'Value is' followed by something else. What is missing?

options.text: The variable 'x' must be global.
The format specifier '%d' is missing from the printf string.
The '&' symbol should be used in printf.
The variable x should be a string.

explanation: To print a variable in printf, you must include a placeholder (format specifier) like '%d' inside the string to tell C where and how to display the value.

description: The code below produces a 'division by zero' error at runtime if the user enters 0. How should the programmer fix this?

options.text: Change 'int' to 'float'.
Use a try-catch block.
Add an 'if' check to ensure 'y != 0' before dividing.
There is no way to fix this in C.

explanation: C does not have built-in exception handling (try-catch) like Java or C++. Logic errors like division by zero must be prevented using standard conditional checks.

description: The programmer wants to swap two numbers using a function. Why do the values of 'a' and 'b' remain unchanged in the main function?

options.text: The temp variable should be global.
The function uses call-by-value, so it only swaps copies of the variables.
The swap function must return the values to main.
Integers cannot be swapped in C without using bitwise operators.

explanation: C uses call-by-value. To modify the actual variables in the calling scope, you must pass their memory addresses using pointers (e.g., swap(&a, &b)) and dereference them inside the function.

description: This code is intended to print the string 'Hello', but it results in undefined behavior or a crash. Identify the bug.

options.text: The array size is too small to accommodate the null terminator '\0'.
Strings must be declared as 'char *str' to use literals.
The printf format specifier for strings should be '%c'.
You cannot initialize a character array with double quotes.

explanation: The string "Hello" consists of 5 characters ('H','e','l','l','o') plus 1 hidden null terminator ('\0'), requiring a minimum array size of 6. Accessing or printing a non-null-terminated string causes C to read past the array boundary.
code:
char str[5] = "Hello";
printf("%s", str);

description: The program should print 'Equal', but it prints nothing even though x is 10. What is the bug?

options.text: Floating point numbers cannot be compared using '=='.
The value 10.0 is interpreted as a double, causing a precision mismatch with float x.
The if condition is missing a semicolon.
The variable x should be an integer.

explanation: In C, floating-point literals are 'double' by default. Comparing a float to a double can fail due to how precision is handled. It is safer to use '10.0f' or check if the difference is less than a small epsilon.

description: The user expects the program to print 'Value: 5', but it crashes. What is wrong with the scanf usage?

options.text: The pointer 'p' was never initialized to point to a valid memory address.
Scanf does not accept pointer variables.
The format specifier should be '%p'.
Pointers must be declared as 'int p*'.

explanation: The pointer 'p' is declared but contains a garbage address. Scanf attempts to write the input to that random memory location, leading to a segmentation fault. 'p' should point to an actual variable or allocated memory.

description: The following code aims to calculate 10 divided by 4, but it prints 'Result: 2.000000'. How can we fix it to get 2.5?

options.text: Change the return type of main to float.
Typecast at least one operand to (float) before the division.
Change '%f' to '%d' in printf.
Use the double division operator '///'.

explanation: Integer division (10/4) truncates the decimal, resulting in 2. This result is then promoted to 2.0 when assigned to the float. To keep the decimal, you must perform floating-point division using (float)a / b.

description: The program is supposed to print 'Even' if x is even and 'Odd' if it is odd, but it always prints 'Odd'. Fix the logic error.

options.text: Remove the semicolon after the 'if' condition.
Change 'x % 2' to 'x / 2'.
Move the 'else' block inside the 'if' block.
Use single '=' in the comparison.

explanation: The semicolon after the 'if' statement is an 'empty statement'. This makes the 'if' condition do nothing if true, and the next block (printf Even) executes unconditionally. However, the 'else' then fails to find an associated 'if', causing a compilation error (or logical failure if blocks are nested incorrectly).

description: This code snippet is intended to sum elements of an array, but it accesses memory incorrectly. What is the bug?

options.text: The loop runs one iteration too many, accessing arr[5] which is out of bounds.
Array indices in C start at 1.
The variable 'sum' should be a pointer.
The condition should be 'i == 5'.

explanation: A C array of size 5 has indices 0 through 4. The condition 'i <= 5' allows the loop to access 'arr[5]', which is outside the allocated memory for the array.

description: The programmer wants to use a macro to calculate the square of (3 + 1). Why does the code print 7 instead of 16?

options.text: Macros can only take a single integer as an argument.
The macro lacks parentheses around parameters in the expansion: '((x) * (x))'.
The 'printf' should use %f for macros.
3 + 1 is calculated after the multiplication.

explanation: Macros are simple text substitutions. SQUARE(3 + 1) expands to '3 + 1 * 3 + 1'. Due to operator precedence, this becomes 3 + (1*3) + 1 = 7. Correct macro: #define SQUARE(x) ((x)*(x)).
code:
#define SQUARE(x) x * x
printf("%d", SQUARE(3 + 1));

description: The following program uses a 'switch' but has a logical 'fall-through' bug. How can we ensure only 'Case 1' is printed?

options.text: Add a 'break;' statement at the end of each case.
Change 'case 1:' to 'if (val == 1)'.
Remove the 'default' case.
Change the variable 'val' to a char.

explanation: In C, execution flows from one case to the next unless interrupted by a 'break'. Without it, if val is 1, it will print 'Case 1', then 'Case 2', then 'Default'.

description: The program intends to print 'Hello' if the string input is 'admin'. Why does it fail even when the user types 'admin'?

options.text: The '==' operator compares memory addresses of strings, not their contents.
Strings in C must be compared using the 'strcmp()' function.
The input buffer 'str' is too small.
Both A and B are correct.

explanation: In C, strings are pointers to character arrays. Using '==' compares the address of the input buffer with the address of the literal string "admin". 'strcmp(str, "admin") == 0' must be used for content comparison.

description: The function 'get_value' is intended to return a pointer to a locally created variable. Why is this dangerous?

options.text: Local variables are stored on the stack and are destroyed when the function returns.
Pointers cannot point to integers.
The return type should be 'int' instead of 'int *'.
The variable 'x' should be initialized to 0.

explanation: Returning a pointer to a local (automatic) variable leads to 'Dangling Pointers'. Once the function exits, the memory for 'x' is reclaimed, and accessing that address in main is undefined behavior.

description: The program should iterate 10 times, but it results in a compilation error. What is wrong with the 'for' loop?

options.text: Variables cannot be declared inside a for loop in older C standards (C89/C90).
The semicolon is missing in the for loop header.
The variable 'i' must be a double.
For loops cannot use the '<' operator.

explanation: In original ANSI C (C89), loop counters must be declared at the beginning of the function scope, not within the 'for' statement itself. This was changed in C99, but many 'medium' level questions test this legacy constraint.

description: The programmer wants to allocate memory for 10 integers. Why does the code fail to compile or work?

options.text: Malloc returns a 'void *', which should ideally be cast to 'int *' in C (though not strictly required).
The 'malloc' function is in <stdlib.h>, which is missing.
The size should be '10 * sizeof(int)'.
All of the above are valid issues or improvements.

explanation: Malloc takes the total number of bytes. 'malloc(10)' only allocates 10 bytes, which is not enough for 10 integers (usually 40 bytes). Also, the required header is <stdlib.h>.

description: The following code is intended to print the size of the array, but it prints 'Size: 8' (on a 64-bit system) instead of 20. Why?

options.text: The array size is calculated incorrectly.
When passed to a function, an array 'decays' into a pointer to its first element.
Sizeof only works in the main function.
The parameter should be 'int arr[5]'.

explanation: In C, you cannot determine the size of an array from a pointer. When 'arr' is passed to 'printSize', it becomes a pointer. 'sizeof(arr)' then returns the size of the pointer (8 bytes on 64-bit systems) rather than the array.

description: The program should print 'Access Granted' if the PIN is 1234, but it always grants access. What is the bug?

options.text: The condition 'pin = 1234' is an assignment, which is always true (non-zero).
The pin variable must be a string.
Scanf failed to read the input.
The if statement needs curly braces.

explanation: A common 'Bugfixer' classic: using '=' (assignment) instead of '==' (equality). The code assigns 1234 to 'pin', and the resulting value (1234) is treated as true.

description: The code uses a pointer to modify a value, but it results in a 'segmentation fault'. Identify the fix.

options.text: Initialize ptr to NULL.
Change '*ptr = 20' to 'ptr = &val'.
The pointer must be declared as a 'const int *'.
Assign the address of 'val' to 'ptr' before dereferencing it.

explanation: You cannot dereference an uninitialized pointer. It points to a random memory address, and attempting to write '20' there violates memory protection. You must set 'ptr = &val;' first.

description: This loop is intended to print numbers 5 down to 1, but it results in an infinite loop. Why?

options.text: Unsigned integers can never be negative, so 'i >= 0' is always true.
The decrement 'i--' is not allowed for unsigned types.
The loop condition should be 'i > 0'.
The printf should use '%u' for unsigned integers.

explanation: An 'unsigned int' cannot hold negative values. When 'i' is 0 and is decremented ('i--'), it wraps around to the maximum possible value for an unsigned int (e.g., 4,294,967,295), which is still >= 0, causing an infinite loop.

description: The programmer wants to add two characters. Why is the output 'Result: 131' instead of a character?

options.text: The printf uses '%d', which prints the sum of the ASCII values ('A'=65, 'B'=66).
Characters cannot be added in C.
The result variable must be of type 'char'.
ASCII values for A and B are incorrect.

explanation: In C, 'char' is actually a small integer type. Adding 'A' (65) and 'B' (66) results in 131. To see the character result, you would need '%c', though 131 might not be a printable character in standard ASCII.

description: The code below fails to compile. What is the syntax error in the struct definition?

options.text: Struct names must be uppercase.
The semicolon is missing after the closing brace of the struct.
Variables inside a struct cannot be initialized.
The 'struct' keyword must be 'typedef'.

explanation: A struct definition is a statement in C and must be terminated with a semicolon after the closing brace '};'.

description: Why does 'sizeof(text)' return 6 while 'strlen(text)' returns 5?

options.text: Sizeof includes the hidden null terminator '\0', while strlen does not.
Strlen is always inaccurate for local arrays.
Sizeof returns the number of bits.
The text array is corrupted.

explanation: The 'sizeof' operator returns the total memory allocated for the array (including the null terminator). 'strlen' is a function that counts characters until it hits the null terminator, excluding it.
code:
char text[] = "Hello";
printf("%zu %zu", sizeof(text), strlen(text));

description: The user wants to find the maximum of two numbers using a ternary operator. Fix the logic.

options.text: The ternary operator uses '?' and ':' correctly, but the printf format is wrong.
The logic is reversed; it currently finds the minimum.
The ternary operator cannot be used in a printf.
The result should be stored in a variable first.

explanation: The condition 'a < b' checks if 'a' is smaller. If true, it returns 'a'. This logic identifies the minimum value, not the maximum.
code:
int a = 10, b = 20;
printf("Max is %d", (a < b) ? a : b);

description: The code fails to print the correct values for a 2D array. What is the bug in the indexing?

options.text: The indices should be [i][j], not [i, j].
The array should be declared as 'matrix[6]'.
A 2D array requires a triple nested loop.
The printf format specifier must be '%f'.

explanation: In C, multi-dimensional array access uses separate brackets for each dimension: 'matrix[i][j]'. Using '[i, j]' uses the comma operator, which evaluates to 'j', resulting in incorrect 1D-style access.

description: The programmer uses 'free(ptr)' but then tries to access 'ptr'. What is the error called?

options.text: Memory Leak.
Dangling Pointer access.
Stack Overflow.
Null Pointer Dereference.

explanation: After memory is freed, the pointer still holds the address, but that memory is no longer valid for the program. Accessing it is called a 'Dangling Pointer' error and leads to undefined behavior.

description: This code aims to print 'A' using a pointer. Why does it produce a compilation error?

options.text: The pointer 'p' should be 'char *' to match the type of '&c'.
You cannot point an integer pointer to a character.
The address operator '&' cannot be used on chars.
The printf format %c is only for literal chars.

explanation: C is statically typed. A pointer to an 'int' expects 4 bytes of data, but a 'char' is only 1 byte. Assigning '&c' (address of char) to 'int *p' is a type mismatch.

description: The code should read a full line including spaces, but 'scanf' only captures the first word. Why?

options.text: Scanf with %s stops at the first whitespace character.
The input buffer is too small.
Scanf requires the string to be pre-filled.
The format specifier should be %c.

explanation: The standard %s format in 'scanf' reads characters until it encounters a space, tab, or newline. To read a full line with spaces, one should use 'fgets()' or the specifier '%[^\n]'.

description: Why does this recursive function to calculate factorial fail for any input > 0?

options.text: The return type is too small.
The base case is missing, leading to infinite recursion (Stack Overflow).
Multiplication cannot be recursive.
The argument 'n' must be a pointer.

explanation: Recursive functions must have a terminating condition (base case). Without one, 'fact(5)' calls 'fact(4)', then 'fact(3)'... into negative numbers forever until the memory stack is exhausted.

description: The program should print '10', but it prints '11'. Identify the bug related to increment operators.

options.text: Postfix '++' increments the value after the current expression is evaluated.
The increment happened twice.
The printf should use '++x'.
Variables should not be incremented inside printf.

explanation: Wait, the code actually uses prefix '++x', which increments the value *before* passing it to printf. To print the original '10' then increment, postfix 'x++' should have been used.
code:
int x = 10;
printf("%d", ++x);

description: The program attempts to compare a signed and unsigned integer. Why is the output 'Unexpected'?

options.text: Signed integers are automatically converted to unsigned, making -1 a very large positive number.
The '<' operator doesn't work with unsigned types.
Unsigned integers are converted to signed.
C cannot compare different data types.

explanation: In C, if an operation involves both signed and unsigned integers, the signed value is promoted to unsigned. -1 becomes 4,294,967,295, which is much larger than 10.

description: The code is meant to print 'Success' if the file exists, but it results in a 'null pointer dereference' if the file is missing. How to fix?

options.text: Remove the asterisk from 'FILE *fp'.
Add a check: 'if (fp != NULL)' before using the file.
Use 'open()' instead of 'fopen()'.
Files must be in the same directory.

explanation: fopen returns NULL if the file cannot be opened. Attempting to use a NULL pointer in other file functions causes a crash. Always validate the pointer first.
code:
FILE *fp = fopen("test.txt", "r");
fgetc(fp);

description: The programmer expects 'result' to be 1, but it is 0. What is wrong with the logical AND?

options.text: Single '&' is a bitwise AND; '&&' should be used for logical AND.
Logical AND only works with booleans.
The result variable must be a float.
5 and 10 are both zero in C.

explanation: The '&' operator performs bitwise AND on the binary representations of 5 (0101) and 10 (1010), which results in 0000 (0). '&&' evaluates the truthiness of both numbers, which would result in 1.
code:
int result = (5 & 10);
printf("%d", result);

description: The programmer wants to implement a flexible array member within a structure to store a variable number of integers. Why does the code fail to compile or result in undefined behavior?

options.text: Flexible array members must be the last member of a struct with at least one other named member.
The array size must be initialized to 0 inside the struct definition.
Flexible array members are only allowed in C++, not C.
You cannot use 'sizeof' on a struct containing a flexible array member.

explanation: According to the C99 standard, a flexible array member (e.g., 'int data[]') must be the last member of the struct. Furthermore, the struct must contain at least one other named member before it. The compiler needs this fixed-size prefix to determine the base size of the structure.

description: The code is intended to check if two strings are identical using a custom pointer-based logic. Why does it return 'Equal' even when strings differ?

options.text: The condition '*s1++ == *s2++' increments the pointers before the comparison occurs.
The loop terminates immediately because of the semicolon after the 'while' condition.
The logic fails to check if the characters are null terminators.
The comparison only checks the first character due to operator precedence.

explanation: The semicolon after 'while(*s1++ == *s2++);' creates an empty loop body. The loop will continue as long as characters are equal. Once it finds a mismatch, the loop terminates, and the *next* line (printf) executes regardless of why the loop stopped. It lacks a post-loop validation.

description: This code attempts to modify a string literal using a pointer. What is the cause of the runtime crash (Segmentation Fault)?

options.text: String literals are typically stored in read-only memory segments (like .rodata).
The pointer 'p' was not allocated using malloc.
Character pointers cannot be indexed like arrays.
The character 'h' must be converted to an integer first.

explanation: In C, a string literal like "hello" is constant. When you assign it to a 'char *', the pointer points to a read-only section of memory. Attempting to write to this memory (p[0] = 'H') triggers a protection fault by the OS.

description: The following program uses a function pointer to call 'add', but it fails to compile. Identify the error in declaration.

options.text: The parentheses around '*ptr' are missing, making it a function returning an 'int *'.
Function pointers cannot be assigned to existing functions.
The arguments in the call must be passed as addresses.
Function pointers must be declared inside the main function.

explanation: Operator precedence in C makes '()' higher than '*'. 'int *ptr(int, int)' is interpreted as a function declaration returning a pointer to an int. To declare a pointer to a function, use 'int (*ptr)(int, int)'.

description: The programmer wants to define a structure where the size of 'name' is determined at runtime. What is wrong with the 'sizeof' calculation for allocation?

options.text: Sizeof(struct User) already includes the size of the pointer 'name', so extra memory is allocated.
You cannot use malloc to allocate memory for structure members.
The pointer 'u' must be initialized to NULL before malloc.
Malloc should be called for the struct and name separately to avoid alignment issues.

explanation: The code allocates memory for the struct but doesn't allocate memory for the characters the 'name' pointer should point to. Accessing 'u->name[0]' results in a crash because 'u->name' contains garbage memory, not a valid buffer.

description: The code intends to print 'True', but it prints 'False' or produces a warning. Why does the 'sizeof' comparison behave unexpectedly?

options.text: Sizeof returns an 'unsigned' value (size_t), which causes -1 to be promoted to a very large positive number.
Sizeof(int) is 0 on some systems.
The comparison operator '<' is not defined for size_t.
Sizeof is a runtime function and cannot be used in 'if' conditions.

explanation: The result of 'sizeof' is 'size_t', which is an unsigned type. In an expression with signed and unsigned types, C promotes the signed value to unsigned. -1 becomes the maximum possible value of size_t, which is much larger than 4.

description: This code attempts to perform a bitwise shift, but the result is unexpected. Why?

options.text: Shifting a 32-bit integer by 32 or more positions is Undefined Behavior in C.
The result should always be 0.
The '<<' operator only works on unsigned integers.
C integers wrap around during shifts.

explanation: According to the C standard, if the value of the right operand is negative or is greater than or equal to the width of the promoted left operand, the behavior is undefined. Most CPUs implement this as 'shift % 32', so '1 << 32' might result in 1.

description: The user defined a union to store either an int or a float. Why does printing 'u.i' after setting 'u.f' produce a strange number?

options.text: Unions share the same memory location for all members; setting one overwrites the other.
Unions can only store one data type at a time.
The float bits are being interpreted as integer bits.
Both A and C are correct.

explanation: A union allocates enough space for its largest member, and all members start at the same address. When you set 'u.f', the bit pattern of the float is stored there. Printing 'u.i' reads those same bits and treats them as an integer, which is called 'type punning'.

description: The function 'create_array' is intended to return a pointer to a new array. What is the bug?

options.text: The array 'arr' is local to the function and is destroyed on return, leaving a dangling pointer.
Pointers cannot point to arrays in C.
The size 10 is too large for the stack.
The return type should be 'int arr[]'.

explanation: In C, local arrays have automatic storage duration. They are allocated on the stack and deallocated when the function finishes. Returning the address of a local array is an error because the memory is no longer valid for use by the caller.

description: The programmer wants to increment the value pointed to by 'p'. Why does the code fail to produce 11?

options.text: The '++' operator has higher precedence than '*', so it increments the pointer address first.
Pointers cannot be incremented.
The variable 'val' is not volatile.
You must use 'p++' to increment the value.

explanation: Suffix '++' has higher precedence than dereference '*'. The expression '*p++' is evaluated as '*(p++)'. It increments the address stored in 'p' and then dereferences the *old* address. To increment the value, use '(*p)++'.

description: The code uses a 'double pointer' to allocate memory for an integer, but it crashes. Identify the logic error.

options.text: The function 'alloc' modifies a local copy of the pointer instead of the one in main.
Double pointers cannot be used with malloc.
The dereference '*p = ...' should be 'p = ...'.
The malloc size is too small.

explanation: In 'alloc(ptr)', a copy of the pointer 'ptr' (which is NULL) is passed. The function allocates memory and assigns it to the local copy 'p', but 'ptr' in main remains NULL. To fix this, pass the address of the pointer (&ptr) and use a double pointer 'int **p'.

description: Why does the following macro-based code print 20 instead of 25?

options.text: The macro expansion results in '4 + 1 * 4 + 1' which evaluates to 4 + 4 + 1 = 9. Wait, the logic is different.
The code is correct; 5 * 4 is 20.
The macro expansion is 'a * 4 + 1', evaluating to 5 * 4 + 1 = 21.
The macro expansion lacks parentheses around the argument: 'x * x' becomes '4 + 1 * 4 + 1'.

explanation: Macros are literal text substitutions. SQUARE(4 + 1) becomes '4 + 1 * 4 + 1'. Multiplication occurs first: '4 + (1 * 4) + 1' = 9. Wait, if the code prints 20, the macro was likely 'x * 5' or similar. Assuming standard SQUARE(x) x * x, it should be ((x) * (x)).
code:
#define SQUARE(x) x * x
printf("%d", SQUARE(4 + 1));

description: The programmer is using 'realloc' to expand an array, but the original data is occasionally lost. What is the dangerous practice in the code?

options.text: Reallocating to the same pointer ('ptr = realloc(ptr, ...)') causes a memory leak if realloc fails and returns NULL.
Realloc cannot be used for expanding memory.
Original data is always deleted by realloc.
The size should be a power of two.

explanation: If 'realloc' fails, it returns NULL but the original memory block remains allocated. By assigning the result directly back to 'ptr', you overwrite the only reference to the original block with NULL, making it impossible to free or access.
code: ptr = realloc(ptr, new_size);

description: This program expects to print the second element of the array using pointer arithmetic. Why does it fail?

options.text: The expression '*(p + 1)' increments by 1 byte instead of the size of an int.
Pointers to arrays cannot be incremented.
'p' points to the entire array (int (*)[5]), so 'p + 1' skips the whole array.
The dereference should be '**p'.

explanation: The address-of operator '&arr' produces a pointer to the entire array, not a pointer to the first element. 'p + 1' adds 'sizeof(int[5])' to the address, effectively jumping past all five elements.

description: The user wants to swap the nibbles (4-bit groups) of a byte. What is wrong with the bitwise logic?

options.text: The shifts should be 4, not 8.
The bitwise OR operator should be replaced with AND.
Single '&' is required for nibbles.
Input must be a signed char.

explanation: A byte has 8 bits. To swap the two 4-bit halves (nibbles), you must shift the lower nibble left by 4 and the upper nibble right by 4, then combine them. Shifting by 8 would move bits entirely out of the byte.
code:
unsigned char x = 0xAB;
unsigned char res = (x << 8) | (x >> 8);

description: The code uses 'setjmp' and 'longjmp' for error handling, but a variable doesn't retain its updated value after the jump. Why?

options.text: Local variables changed after 'setjmp' but before 'longjmp' must be 'volatile' to preserve their value.
Longjmp resets all variables to their initial state.
Setjmp/Longjmp only works within the same function.
The jmp_buf is corrupted.

explanation: According to the C standard, the values of non-volatile local variables in a function that called 'setjmp' are indeterminate after a 'longjmp' if they were modified between the two calls.

description: Identify the bug in this multi-file project setup where a variable is shared.

options.text: The variable should be declared as 'extern' in the header to avoid multiple definitions.
Global variables must be 'static' to be shared.
Headers cannot contain variables.
The linker will automatically merge global variables with the same name.

explanation: If 'int x = 10;' is in a header file included by two .c files, the linker will find two definitions of 'x' and throw an error. The header should contain 'extern int x;' and exactly one .c file should define it.
code:
// vars.h
int global_counter = 0;

description: The code intends to print the string 'Bye', but it prints 'Hi' and then crashes. What is wrong with the pointer assignment?

options.text: The pointer points to a constant string; you cannot change the string contents, only the pointer itself.
Strings must be copied using strcpy, not assignment.
The array is out of bounds.
The pointer 's' is not pointing to the array 'str'.

explanation: The code uses 'char *s = "Hi"', where "Hi" is in read-only memory. Then it tries to copy "Bye" into that read-only memory using strcpy. It should instead make 's' point to the newly allocated "Bye" or use a local char array.

description: The programmer wants to align a memory address to the nearest 8-byte boundary. What is wrong with the mask logic?

options.text: The bitwise NOT (~) should be applied to 7, not 8.
You must use bitwise OR to align addresses.
The address should be divided by 8.
Addition must come after the AND operation.

explanation: To align to an 8-byte boundary, you need to clear the lower 3 bits (0, 1, 2). The mask for this is ~(8-1) or ~7. Using ~8 (which is ...11110111) clears the 4th bit, which is incorrect.
code:
uintptr_t addr = 1005;
uintptr_t aligned = (addr + 7) & ~8;

description: This loop is supposed to process bits of a 32-bit integer. Why does it fail for negative numbers?

options.text: Right-shifting a signed negative integer is implementation-defined and often fills with the sign bit (arithmetic shift), causing an infinite loop.
Negative numbers have no bits.
The condition 'x != 0' is never false for negative numbers.
Modulus operator '%' doesn't work on negatives.

explanation: When shifting a signed 'int' that is negative, most C compilers perform an arithmetic shift, meaning the left-most bit is filled with 1s to preserve the sign. If x is -1, 'x >> 1' remains -1 forever.

description: The function uses a 'static' local variable to count calls. Why is this count incorrect in a multi-threaded environment?

options.text: Static variables are not thread-safe by default; increments are not atomic.
Static variables are shared across all instances of the function.
The variable 'count' is re-initialized on every call.
Threads have their own copies of static variables.

explanation: Static local variables are stored in global memory. If multiple threads call the function simultaneously, they all attempt to read, increment, and write to the same memory location, leading to race conditions.

description: The program should output the string 'Success', but it outputs 'Uuccess'. What is wrong with the char literal logic?

options.text: The expression "Success"[0] returns 'S', and adding ('a' - 'A') to it converts it to lowercase 's'.
The indexing is 1-based.
Addition on strings is not allowed.
The logic is correct; the output is expected.

explanation: Wait, the code subtracts ('a' - 'A') which is 32. 'S' is 83. 83 - 32 = 51, which is the ASCII for '3'. The logic intended to change case, but addition/subtraction must be carefully applied to the correct character.

description: The code uses 'scanf' with a width limit to prevent overflow, but it fails to null-terminate the string if the input is too long. Fix the bug.

options.text: Scanf always null-terminates the string if there is space in the buffer.
The buffer size must be 10, not 9.
Scanf width limit '9' does not include the null terminator.
You must manually add '\0' after every scanf.

explanation: The width limit in scanf (e.g., %9s) specifies the maximum number of characters to read. Scanf will then append a null terminator *after* those characters. If the buffer is size 9 and you read 9 chars, the null terminator will be written to index 9, which is out of bounds.
code:
char buf[9];
scanf("%9s", buf);

description: Why does this program fail to compare two floats properly using a precision epsilon?

options.text: The 'fabs' function for floating point absolute value is in <math.h>, which is missing.
Floats cannot be subtracted.
The epsilon value is too large.
The result of subtraction is always an integer.

explanation: To use math functions like fabs(), you must include the math library header. Without it, the compiler may assume fabs() returns an integer, leading to incorrect comparison logic.
code:
float a = 0.1, b = 0.2, c = 0.3;
if (fabs((a + b) - c) < 0.0001) printf("Ok");

description: The code uses 'strncpy' to copy a string safely, but the resulting string is corrupted when printed. Why?

options.text: Strncpy does not null-terminate the destination if the source length is >= N.
The destination buffer is too small.
Strncpy only works for short strings.
The source string is in read-only memory.

explanation: Strncpy is often misunderstood. If the source string length is equal to or greater than the limit N, strncpy copies N characters but does NOT append a null terminator. You must manually set 'dest[N-1] = '\0';'.

description: The program should print '5', but it prints '4' due to a sequence point issue. Fix the bug.

options.text: Modifying a variable multiple times between sequence points (like 'i = i++') results in undefined behavior.
The assignment happens before the increment.
Postfix increment is lower precedence than assignment.
C does not support i++.

explanation: The expression 'i = i++' attempts to modify 'i' twice (once via increment, once via assignment) without an intervening sequence point. This is Undefined Behavior; the final value of 'i' depends entirely on the compiler implementation.

description: This code tries to calculate the length of a string using a pointer, but it returns a massive number. Why?

options.text: The pointer 'p' is initialized with the value of the character 'H' (72) instead of the address of the string.
The while loop condition is wrong.
Strings in C are not null-terminated.
The return type should be size_t.

explanation: The assignment 'char *p = str[0]' attempts to treat the first character ('H') as a memory address. 'p' now points to memory address 72, which is invalid. It should be 'char *p = str;' or 'char *p = &str[0];'.

description: Regarding 'struct padding', why is 'sizeof(struct Mixed)' 12 instead of 6 on a 32-bit system?

options.text: The compiler adds padding to align the 4-byte 'int' on a 4-byte boundary.
The 'char' array size is rounded up to the nearest power of 2.
Shorts are always 4 bytes.
Structs have a fixed minimum size of 16 bytes.

explanation: To optimize CPU access, members must be aligned. After 'char a', there are 3 bytes of padding so that 'int b' starts at an address divisible by 4. After 'short c', 2 more bytes of padding are added so the total struct size is a multiple of the largest alignment requirement (4).

description: The programmer wants to read a character after reading an integer, but the program skips the character input. Why?

options.text: The newline character '\n' from the first 'Enter' is still in the input buffer and is read by '%c'.
Scanf cannot mix integers and characters.
The character variable must be an integer.
The buffer is full.

explanation: When you enter '10\n', 'scanf("%d")' reads the 10 but leaves the '\n' in the buffer. The subsequent 'scanf("%c")' reads that newline immediately, making it appear as though the input was skipped. Use 'scanf(" %c")' (with a space) to skip whitespace.

description: The function aims to calculate 'x' to the power of 2 using a bitwise trick for performance. Why does it only work for powers of 2?

options.text: The expression '(x & (x-1))' is a trick to check if a number IS a power of two, not to square it.
Bitwise AND cannot be used for exponentiation.
The variable 'x' must be unsigned.
Parentheses are missing around x-1.

explanation: The 'x & (x-1)' logic is a classic bitwise pattern that evaluates to 0 if 'x' is a power of two (only one bit set). It has nothing to do with squaring a number.

description: The following C program is intended to print the first element of an array, but it fails to compile. What is the bug?

options.text: Array indices in C start at 1, not 0.
The printf format specifier for integers should be %f.
A semicolon is missing at the end of the array declaration.
The array size must be specified inside the brackets during declaration.

explanation: In C, every statement must end with a semicolon. The array declaration 'int arr[] = {10, 20}' is missing one, which prevents the compiler from recognizing the end of the instruction.

description: This code is meant to assign the value 5 to the third element of the array, but it produces a compilation error. Identify the fix.

options.text: Change 'arr(2)' to 'arr[2]' using square brackets for indexing.
Change the index to 3 because it is the third element.
Declare the array as a float instead of an int.
Use the 'set' keyword to assign values.

explanation: In C, array elements are accessed using square brackets '[]'. Parentheses '()' are reserved for function calls, so the compiler thinks 'arr' is a function.

description: The program should print '20', but it prints a garbage value or crashes. What is the error in the indexing?

options.text: Indices are 0-based; 'arr[2]' accesses the 3rd element which doesn't exist.
The array must be initialized with 0.
Printf cannot print array elements directly.
The array size should be 3.
