explanation: When you initialize with a list of characters, C does exactly what you tell it: it creates a 5-byte array. Unlike "Hello", it does not add a 6th byte for the null terminator. Printf will overflow reading memory.

description: Identify the issue with 'strtok' in a multi-threaded program.

options.text: Strtok uses an internal static buffer to track the current position, making it thread-unsafe (use strtok_r instead).
Strtok only works on the main thread.
The delimiters must be global constants.
Strtok modifies the return type to int.

explanation: Because 'strtok' maintains state between calls using a static variable, if another thread calls 'strtok' simultaneously, the internal pointer is overwritten, leading to corrupted results for both threads.
code: char *token = strtok(str, " ");

description: Why is 'return "Hello"' safe, while 'return char arr[] = "Hello"' is not?

options.text: String literals have static storage duration; local arrays have automatic (stack) duration.
Literals are allocated on the heap.
The compiler copies literals to the caller's stack.
Static keywords are required for literals.

explanation: String literals exist for the entire life of the program in a special data segment. Local arrays are created on the stack when the function is called and destroyed when it returns, leaving a dangling pointer.

description: Identify the bug in this 'Lowest Common Ancestor' (LCA) function specifically for a BST.

options.text: The search logic is reversed: it recurses left when both values are greater than the root, and right when both are smaller.
BST LCA cannot be recursive.
The 'if' statements should use '||' instead of '&&'.
It fails if one of the nodes is the root.

explanation: If $p, q < root$, the LCA is in the left subtree. If $p, q > root$, it's in the right. This code incorrectly goes left for larger values and right for smaller values.

description: Identify the bug in this function meant to calculate the 'Maximum Path Sum' between any two leaf nodes.

options.text: The function correctly calculates height-based paths but fails to handle nodes with only one child, incorrectly treating them as if they can form a leaf-to-leaf path.
Leaf nodes should return 0.
The sum calculation is $O(N^2)$.
The data values must be positive.

explanation: A leaf-to-leaf path must involve two children. If a node has only a left child, you cannot calculate a 'max leaf-to-leaf' path through it. The code must explicitly check for the presence of both children before updating the global result.

description: Identify the bug in this function that adds an edge to an undirected graph using an adjacency list.

options.text: The function only adds the edge from 'u' to 'v', making it a directed graph instead of an undirected one.
Adjacency lists must use 'std::set' to prevent duplicates.
The parameters 'u' and 'v' should be passed by reference.
Vectors cannot be used to store graph edges in C++.

explanation: For an undirected graph, an edge between 'u' and 'v' must be represented in both adj[u] and adj[v]. This code only adds it to one.

description: Why does this BFS traversal function enter an infinite loop or crash?

options.text: It uses a Stack instead of a Queue.
The 'visited' array is never updated to 'true', so the algorithm repeatedly processes the same nodes.
Queues cannot store integers in C++.
The starting node 's' must be 0.

explanation: In BFS, you must mark a node as visited when it is pushed into the queue (or popped) to ensure the same node isn't processed multiple times.

description: Identify the bug in this recursive DFS implementation.

options.text: The function is missing the base case to stop recursion when a node has no neighbors.
The 'visited' array is passed by value, meaning changes aren't tracked across recursive calls, leading to redundant processing and potential cycles.
DFS cannot be implemented with recursion in C++.
The loop should start from 'v = 1'.

explanation: By passing 'visited' by value, every recursive call gets a copy. Marking a node as visited in one copy doesn't affect the others, effectively making the 'visited' check useless.

description: What is the issue with this Adjacency Matrix initialization for a graph with V vertices?

options.text: The matrix is initialized with 'true' instead of 'false'.
The 2D vector is not properly sized, leading to an 'out of range' error when accessing matrix[i][j].
Matrices are only for directed graphs.
The size should be V+1.

explanation: Declaring 'vector<vector<bool>> matrix;' creates an empty vector of vectors. You must specify the dimensions (e.g., 'matrix(V, vector<bool>(V, false))') before accessing indices.

description: Identify the bug in this logic that counts the degree of a vertex in an undirected graph (Adjacency List).

options.text: It returns the size of the 'adj' array instead of the specific list at 'u'.
The degree should be multiplied by 2.
Actually, this code is correct for an undirected graph represented by an adjacency list.
Degrees can only be calculated using an adjacency matrix.

explanation: In an adjacency list for an undirected graph, the degree of vertex 'u' is simply the number of neighbors stored in 'adj[u]'. This code correctly returns that count.

description: Why does this function fail to find if an edge exists in an adjacency list?

options.text: The loop uses 'adj[v]' instead of 'adj[u]' to check for an edge from 'u' to 'v'.
Adjacency lists do not support edge existence checks.
The return type should be 'int'.
The loop condition 'x != v' is backwards.

explanation: To see if there is an edge from 'u' to 'v', you must iterate through the neighbors of 'u', which are stored in 'adj[u]'.

description: Identify the bug in this code intended to print all neighbors of a vertex.

options.text: It prints the index of the vertex instead of the values in the adjacency list.
The loop iterates through the wrong range (0 to V) instead of iterating over the neighbors of 'u'.
Neighbors must be sorted before printing.
The adjacency list is empty.

explanation: To print neighbors of 'u', the loop should be 'for(int v : adj[u])'. Iterating from 0 to V is only useful if you are using an adjacency matrix.

description: What is the issue with this 'visited' array initialization for a graph with vertices numbered 1 to N?

options.text: The vector size is N, which means index N is out of bounds (indices are 0 to N-1).
Boolean vectors are not supported in C++11.
The default value should be 'true'.
The vector should store 'int' instead of 'bool'.

explanation: If vertices are 1-indexed (1 to N), a vector of size N only goes up to index N-1. Accessing 'visited[N]' will cause an error. Use 'vector<bool> v(N + 1, false);'.

description: Identify the bug in this BFS implementation starting from vertex 0.

options.text: The queue is popped before the front element is accessed.
It uses 'q.back()' instead of 'q.front()' to get the next node.
The 'visited' check is performed after the node is printed.
The code uses 'q.push' inside the 'while' loop.

explanation: In BFS, 'q.back()' returns the most recently added element (LIFO behavior like a stack), whereas BFS requires the oldest element (FIFO) using 'q.front()'.

description: Why does this function fail to count edges in a directed graph?

options.text: It should divide the total count by 2.
The loop only checks the first vertex.
Actually, the code correctly sums the sizes of all adjacency lists to get the edge count for a directed graph.
It only counts vertices with self-loops.

explanation: In a directed graph, the number of edges is exactly the sum of the out-degrees of all vertices. This code is correct.

description: Identify the bug in this Adjacency Matrix edge addition.

options.text: It uses '==' instead of '=' for assignment.
Indices 'u' and 'v' must be strings.
The value should be '0' for an existing edge.
It only sets the matrix at [v][u], missing [u][v] for an undirected graph.

explanation: The condition 'if(matrix[u][v] == 1)' is a comparison, not an assignment. To add an edge, you must use 'matrix[u][v] = 1;'.

description: Identify the bug in this function that checks if a vertex is isolated (degree 0).

options.text: It checks if the adjacency list pointer is null, which is not how you check for an empty list.
Isolated vertices don't exist in C++.
The function should return 'true' if size is greater than 0.
It uses '.empty()' on a single integer.

explanation: To check if a vertex is isolated in an adjacency list representation, you should check if the vector at 'adj[u]' is empty using 'adj[u].empty()' or 'adj[u].size() == 0'. Checking 'adj == nullptr' is invalid for the logic.

description: What is the issue with this DFS initialization for a disconnected graph?

options.text: It only calls DFS for vertex 0, so it will not visit nodes in other connected components.
Disconnected graphs cannot be traversed.
The visited array should be a global variable.
The loop should be inside the DFS function.

explanation: To ensure all nodes in a disconnected graph are visited, you must iterate through all vertices 0 to V-1 and call DFS for any vertex that hasn't been visited yet.

description: Identify the bug in this Edge List representation attempt.

options.text: A 'pair<int, int>' cannot store an edge.
The vector 'edges' is never given a size, so 'push_back' is the only way to add elements, but the code uses index 'i'.
Edge lists must be 2D arrays.
Integers are too small for edge weights.

explanation: An empty vector has size 0. Accessing 'edges[i]' is an out-of-bounds error. You must use 'edges.push_back({u, v})' or resize the vector first.

description: Why does this graph print function skip the last vertex?

options.text: The loop condition 'i < V - 1' stops before the last index.
Vertex indices are 1-based.
Adjacency lists are null-terminated.
The 'cout' statement is inside a comment.

explanation: Vertex indices range from 0 to V-1. The condition 'i < V - 1' means the loop runs for 0 to V-2, skipping the final vertex.

description: Identify the bug in this Adjacency Matrix check for a self-loop at vertex 'u'.

options.text: It checks matrix[u][0] instead of matrix[u][u].
Self-loops are not allowed in adjacency matrices.
The value '1' indicates no edge.
The matrix should be 3D.

explanation: A self-loop at vertex 'u' is represented by an edge from 'u' to 'u'. Therefore, the correct index to check is 'matrix[u][u]'.

description: Identify the bug in this BFS search for a target value.

options.text: The function returns 'false' as soon as it checks the first neighbor of the starting node.
BFS cannot find specific values.
The 'while' loop only runs once.
The target must be a pointer.

explanation: The 'return false' statement is placed inside the 'while' loop. If the first node popped doesn't match the target, the function immediately exits and returns false, skipping the rest of the graph.

description: What is the issue with this Adjacency List clearing function?

options.text: It deletes the pointer to the array instead of clearing the vectors.
Calling '.clear()' on a vector deletes the graph structure from the stack.
Actually, this code correctly removes all edges from the graph while keeping the vertices.
Vectors cannot be cleared in a loop.

explanation: This code is correct. Iterating through the array of vectors and calling '.clear()' on each removes all elements (edges) while keeping the capacity for vertices.

description: Identify the bug in this function meant to add a weight to an edge.

options.text: Adjacency lists of 'int' cannot store weights; you need a vector of 'pair<int, int>' to store both neighbor and weight.
Weights must be stored in a separate matrix.
The weight should be passed as the first argument.
Weights in C++ can only be 0 or 1.

explanation: A 'vector<int> adj[]' only stores the destination vertex. To store a weight, each element in the vector needs to be a structure or a pair like 'pair<int, int> {destination, weight}'.

description: Why does this code crash when accessing the adjacency list?

options.text: The array 'adj' is declared with a size smaller than the number of vertices V.
Vertices must be strings.
The index must be 'V - 1'.
Adjacency lists are read-only.

explanation: If 'V' nodes are used, the array needs to be at least size 'V'. Declaring 'adj[5]' and trying to access 'adj[10]' (if V=10) will cause a segmentation fault.

description: Identify the bug in this function that checks for a path of length 1 (direct edge).

options.text: It uses '==' for assignment in the 'if' statement.
It checks if the vector 'adj[u]' itself is equal to 'v', rather than checking the elements inside the vector.
Adjacency lists don't support equality.
The 'if' statement is missing.

explanation: 'adj[u]' is a 'vector<int>', and 'v' is an 'int'. You cannot compare a vector to an integer directly. You must search the vector for the value 'v'.

description: Identify the bug in this DFS call within a main function.

options.text: The 'visited' array is not initialized with 'false'.
The DFS function is called without the 'adj' parameter.
Actually, this code is missing the declaration of the 'visited' vector entirely before passing it.
DFS should return a value.

explanation: A vector must be declared and sized (e.g., 'vector<bool> visited(V, false);') before it can be passed to a function as a reference or value.

description: What is the issue with this Adjacency Matrix transpose function?

options.text: It only swaps elements on the diagonal.
The nested loops iterate in a way that swaps elements twice, resulting in the original matrix.
Transpose is not possible for matrices.
The 'swap' function is not in C++.

explanation: When you swap 'matrix[i][j]' and 'matrix[j][i]' for all 'i' and 'j', you swap them once when the loop hits (i, j) and then swap them back when it hits (j, i). To fix this, the inner loop should start from 'j = i'.

description: Identify the bug in this BFS implementation for counting nodes.

options.text: The counter is incremented every time a neighbor is seen, regardless of whether it was already visited.
BFS can't count nodes.
The counter should be a float.
The return statement is inside the loop.

explanation: To count unique nodes in a connected component, you should only increment the counter when a node is marked as visited for the first time.

description: Why does this Adjacency Matrix check return true for every pair?

options.text: The matrix is full of 1s.
The check uses '=' (assignment) instead of '==' (comparison), so it sets the value to 1 and the 'if' condition evaluates to true.
Boolean values are not allowed in matrices.
The indices are swapped.

explanation: In C++, 'if (x = 1)' assigns 1 to 'x' and then evaluates the truthiness of 1 (which is true). To check equality, you must use '=='.

description: Identify the bug in this function that prints a BFS path.

options.text: It uses 'endl' too many times.
The path is printed in reverse order (from target to source) because it follows parent pointers backwards.
Parent pointers can't be stored in vectors.
The 'curr' variable should be a pointer.

explanation: By starting at 'target' and moving to 'parent[curr]', the code prints the path backwards. To print it correctly, you should store it in a container and reverse it, or use recursion.

description: Identify the bug in this Adjacency List to Adjacency Matrix conversion.

options.text: The matrix is never initialized to 0, so it contains garbage values.
The inner loop should go to V.
It only works for graphs with 10 nodes.
Adjacency lists are incompatible with matrices.

explanation: If the matrix is not initialized to 0 (e.g., 'vector<vector<int>> mat(V, vector<int>(V, 0));'), then any cell without an edge will contain a random value from memory.

description: Why does this function always return 0 for the number of vertices?

options.text: It returns the size of the first adjacency list instead of the array size.
Size of arrays cannot be determined this way in C++.
The array is empty.
Adjacency lists are zero-indexed.

explanation: Actually, this code is correct if 'adj' is an array of size 5. It returns the number of vectors in the array. However, if 'adj' was a pointer, this would fail.

description: Identify the bug in this function that adds a weighted edge.

options.text: It pushes three separate values into the vector instead of a single pair/struct.
Weight must be the first element.
The destination 'v' and weight 'w' are added as independent neighbors, doubling the size of the list incorrectly.
Vectors can only store one type of data.

explanation: By calling 'push_back' twice, the adjacency list now thinks there are two neighbors: one named 'v' and one named 'w'. To store a weighted edge, you must push a single 'pair<int, int>'.

description: What is wrong with this BFS queue pop logic?

options.text: It tries to pop from an empty queue if not careful.
The 'pop()' function in C++ 'std::queue' does not return the value; it only removes it. Accessing the value requires 'front()'.
Pop should be called at the end of the loop.
The queue size is fixed.

explanation: In C++, 'q.pop()' is a void function. To get the value of the node, you must call 'int u = q.front();' before calling 'q.pop();'.

description: Identify the bug in this function that checks for a cycle in a directed graph using DFS.

options.text: The function only uses a 'visited' array, which is sufficient for undirected graphs but cannot distinguish between a cycle and a cross-edge in a directed graph.
The recursive calls are missing the 'adj' parameter.
Directed graphs cannot have cycles by definition.
The visited array should be initialized to 'true'.

explanation: In a directed graph, a cycle is detected if we encounter a node that is currently in the recursion stack. A simple 'visited' array only tells us if a node was seen before, which could happen with cross-edges in a DAG.

description: Why does this Dijkstra's algorithm implementation fail to find the shortest path?

options.text: The priority queue is a max-heap by default, so it picks the longest distance first instead of the shortest.
Dijkstra's cannot work with weighted graphs.
The 'dist' array should be initialized with 0.
The weight 'w' should be subtracted from the distance.

explanation: In C++, 'priority_queue<T>' is a max-heap. For Dijkstra's, we need a min-heap, which requires 'priority_queue<T, vector<T>, greater<T>>'. Picking the largest distance first breaks the greedy property.

description: Identify the bug in this Kahn's Algorithm implementation for Topological Sort.

options.text: The algorithm adds nodes to the result even if their in-degree is not zero.
The in-degree of neighbors is not decremented, causing the queue to empty prematurely.
Topological sort requires a stack, not a queue.
The loop condition 'while(q.empty())' is backwards.

explanation: After popping a node 'u' from the queue, Kahn's algorithm must decrement the in-degree of all its neighbors 'v'. If a neighbor's in-degree becomes zero, it should then be pushed into the queue.

description: What is the issue with this implementation of finding the number of connected components?

options.text: The 'visited' array is reset in every iteration of the loop, causing the function to overcount components.
Connected components can only be found in trees.
The DFS call is missing the start node.
The 'count' variable should start at 1.

explanation: By redeclaring or resetting 'visited' inside the loop, the algorithm 'forgets' which nodes were visited in previous components, treating every node as a new component.

description: Identify the bug in this Bipartite Graph check using BFS (Graph Coloring).

options.text: It fails to check for Bipartiteness across all connected components.
The condition 'color[v] == color[u]' correctly detects an edge between nodes of the same color, but the 'return false' only breaks the inner loop.
The color array must use 'float' values.
Nodes are assigned the same color as their parent.

explanation: This code is missing the logic to handle disconnected components. It only checks the component containing node 0. If another component is not bipartite, the function will still return true.

description: Why is this Bellman-Ford implementation incomplete?

options.text: It lacks the N-th relaxation step required to detect negative weight cycles.
It only relaxes edges once.
Bellman-Ford only works on DAGs.
The distance to the source should be infinity.

explanation: Bellman-Ford requires $V-1$ relaxations to find shortest paths. To detect negative cycles, a $V$-th relaxation is performed; if any distance still decreases, a negative cycle exists. This code stops after $V-1$.

description: Identify the bug in this function that checks if a directed graph is a tree.

options.text: It only checks for the absence of cycles but fails to check if all nodes are reachable from a single root (connectivity).
Directed trees must have exactly 2*V edges.
Trees cannot have directed edges.
It uses BFS instead of DFS.

explanation: A directed tree (or arborescence) must be acyclic AND have exactly one root from which all other nodes are reachable. Just checking 'isCyclic' is not enough; the graph could be a forest or have disconnected components.

description: What is wrong with this Prim's Algorithm setup for MST?

options.text: The 'key' array should be initialized with '0' to pick the first node.
It uses a priority queue of 'int' but needs 'pair<int, int>' to track both the weight and the vertex ID.
Prim's doesn't work on undirected graphs.
The 'inMST' array is not necessary.

explanation: To pick the edge with the minimum weight, the priority queue must store the weight as the first element of a pair so it can be sorted, and the vertex ID as the second element to know which node to visit.
code:
priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>> pq;
pq.push(0); // Only pushes weight?

description: Identify the bug in this Kosaraju's Algorithm (Strongly Connected Components) step 1.

options.text: Nodes should be pushed to the stack *after* the recursive DFS calls to ensure they are ordered by finish time.
Kosaraju's uses a Queue instead of a Stack.
The graph should be transposed before the first DFS.
The visited array must be cleared after every push.

explanation: In Kosaraju's, we need nodes ordered by their completion time. This requires pushing a node to the stack only after all its neighbors have been fully explored (Post-order).

description: Why does this BFS-based 'Shortest Path in Unweighted Graph' fail?

options.text: The parent of the source node should be 's' instead of '-1'.
BFS cannot determine paths, only connectivity.
The code updates the parent pointer every time an edge is seen, even if the node was already visited, leading to incorrect paths.
The queue should be a stack.

explanation: In an unweighted graph, the first time BFS reaches a node, it has found the shortest path. Parent pointers should only be set once when the node is first marked 'visited'.

description: Identify the bug in this Floyd-Warshall Algorithm implementation.

options.text: The loops are in the wrong order: the intermediate vertex 'k' must be the outermost loop.
The distance matrix should be initialized with 1.
It uses 3D space unnecessarily.
The condition 'dist[i][k] + dist[k][j] < dist[i][j]' is only for directed graphs.

explanation: Floyd-Warshall is a dynamic programming algorithm where we consider each vertex 'k' as an intermediate point. For the DP state to transition correctly, 'k' must be the outermost loop.

description: What is the bug in this Cycle Detection function for Undirected Graphs?

options.text: The function fails to ignore the parent of the current node, leading it to treat the back-and-forth edge of a single undirected link as a cycle.
Undirected graphs cannot have cycles.
It uses a recursion stack array which is only for directed graphs.
The visited array should be local to the DFS.

explanation: In an undirected graph, if we are at node 'u' and see a neighbor 'v' that is already visited, it is only a cycle if 'v' is NOT the parent of 'u'.

description: Identify the bug in this Kruskal's Algorithm (MST) edge sorting step.

options.text: The edges are sorted by destination vertex instead of by weight.
Sorting is O(N log N), which makes the algorithm too slow for MST.
Kruskal's requires edges to be in a priority queue, not a vector.
The weight 'w' should be the second element in the tuple.

explanation: Kruskal's is a greedy algorithm that must process edges in non-decreasing order of their weights. Sorting by vertex IDs doesn't help find the MST.

description: Why does this 'Count Paths between Two Nodes' DFS fail?

options.text: The 'visited' status is never reset, so the function only finds one path and ignores others that reuse nodes in different configurations.
DFS cannot count paths, only BFS can.
The return type should be 'bool'.
It results in an infinite loop for DAGs.

explanation: To find ALL paths, we need to unmark the node as 'visited' (backtrack) after exploring all paths through it. This allows the node to be part of different paths starting from other neighbors.

description: Identify the bug in this 'Find Mother Vertex' (Kosaraju-based logic) implementation.

options.text: The code finds a candidate mother vertex but fails to perform a second DFS to verify if all other vertices are actually reachable from it.
Mother vertices only exist in undirected graphs.
The last finished vertex in DFS is always a mother vertex by default.
It uses a stack which is not required for mother vertex.

explanation: The last vertex to finish in a DFS is a potential mother vertex. However, in a disconnected graph or specific directed structures, it might not reach everyone. A verification DFS is mandatory.

description: What is the bug in this Tarjan's Algorithm (SCC) low-link update?

options.text: The low-link value of 'u' should be updated using 'disc[v]' for back-edges, not 'low[v]', to avoid using cross-edges from other SCCs.
Tarjan's algorithm doesn't use low-link values.
The disc array should be updated using the time variable.
It only works for trees.

explanation: When encountering an already visited node 'v' that is in the current recursion stack (back-edge), we update 'low[u]' using 'disc[v]'. Using 'low[v]' here is a common error that can lead to incorrect SCC results.

description: Identify the bug in this DSU (Disjoint Set Union) 'Find' function with path compression.

options.text: The parent assignment 'parent[i] = find(parent[i])' is missing, so the tree depth is not reduced, resulting in O(N) operations.
Path compression is only for 'Union', not 'Find'.
The function will cause a stack overflow for large sets.
The base case should return 0.

explanation: Path compression works by making every node in the path point directly to the root during the find operation. Without 'parent[i] = ...', it just performs a standard recursive search.

description: Why does this 'Check if Graph is Eulerian' function for undirected graphs fail?

options.text: It checks if all vertices have even degrees, but fails to check if all non-zero degree vertices are connected.
Eulerian graphs must have all odd degrees.
It only works for bipartite graphs.
The degree should be checked using an adjacency matrix.

explanation: For an undirected graph to be Eulerian, all vertices must have even degrees AND all vertices with non-zero degree must belong to a single connected component.

description: Identify the bug in this Dijkstra's distance update logic.

options.text: The condition 'dist[u] + weight < dist[v]' correctly identifies a shorter path, but the code fails to push the new distance into the priority queue.
The old distance should be removed from the priority queue.
Distances should be updated using multiplication.
The 'dist' array cannot be modified during iteration.

explanation: Dijkstra's is a priority-queue based algorithm. When a shorter path to 'v' is found, the new distance must be pushed to the PQ so it can be evaluated in the correct order.

description: What is the bug in this BFS-based 'Level of each node' calculation?

options.text: The level of neighbors is calculated as 'level[u] + 1', but it is updated for already visited nodes, leading to incorrect values.
Levels can only be found in trees.
The queue should be cleared at every level.
The source level must be 1.

explanation: Level should only be set once when a node is first reached (transition from unvisited to visited). If updated multiple times, it no longer represents the shortest distance from the source.

description: Identify the bug in this Transitive Closure implementation.

options.text: It uses DFS from each node but fails to reset the 'visited' array before each DFS call, making the reachability matrix incomplete.
Transitive closure requires Dijkstra's algorithm.
The matrix should be 1-indexed.
It only works for acyclic graphs.

explanation: To find all nodes reachable from node 'i', we must perform a fresh traversal. If 'visited' is not reset, the DFS for node 'i+1' will think all nodes visited by 'i' are already accounted for.

description: Why does this 'Is Graph Connected' check for directed graphs using BFS fail?

options.text: It only checks if all nodes are reachable from vertex 0, which is necessary for 'strong' connectivity but not sufficient for 'weak' connectivity.
BFS cannot check connectivity in directed graphs.
The graph must be converted to an undirected graph first.
Both A and C are valid depending on the definition of 'connected' used.

explanation: In directed graphs, 'connected' usually implies 'Strongly Connected' (everyone reaches everyone). Checking from node 0 only proves node 0 can reach everyone, not that they can reach node 0 or each other.

description: Identify the bug in this Prim's Algorithm MST result calculation.

options.text: It sums the 'key' values of all nodes, but the source node's key is 'INT_MAX' initially and never updated, leading to an overflow.
MST weight is the product of edges, not the sum.
The keys of nodes not in the MST should be ignored.
Prim's returns the number of edges, not the weight.

explanation: The 'key' for the starting node must be set to 0 before the loop. Otherwise, its value remains 'INT_MAX', and the total MST weight will be incorrect/overflow.

description: What is the issue with this Kahn's Algorithm cycle detection logic?

options.text: The function returns true if the number of visited nodes is less than V, but it calculates the count inside the queue loop which is correct (No bug).
Kahn's algorithm cannot detect cycles.
The count should be compared to E (edges) instead of V (vertices).
Actually, the code provided is correct for detecting cycles in a directed graph.

explanation: This is a trick question. In Kahn's algorithm, if the number of nodes in the topological sort is equal to V, there is no cycle. If it's less than V, there is a cycle. The logic is correct.
code:
if (count != V) return true; // Has Cycle
else return false;

description: Identify the bug in this DFS-based 'Node Discovery Time' recording.

options.text: The time variable is passed by value, so every node in the recursion tree gets the same discovery time.
Discovery time must be recorded after the neighbors are visited.
Time should be measured in milliseconds.
The discovery array should be local.

explanation: For discovery time to be unique and incremental, the time counter must be shared across all recursive calls. This requires passing it by reference ('int& time').

description: Why does this 'Check if Graph is a DAG' function using DFS fail?

options.text: It fails to unmark the node from the recursion stack after returning, causing it to incorrectly detect cycles in non-cyclic graphs.
DAGs cannot be checked with DFS.
The 'visited' array is sufficient for DAG detection.
The stack should store the node data.

explanation: A node should only be in the 'recursion stack' while its descendants are being explored. After the loop over neighbors finishes, the node must be removed from the stack to avoid false cycle detection in future branches.

description: Identify the bug in this Dijkstra's priority queue storage.

options.text: The pair is stored as {vertex, distance}, but since priority_queue sorts by the first element, it will sort by vertex ID instead of the shortest distance.
Pairs are not allowed in priority queues.
The distance should be a negative value.
Priority queues only work with integers.

explanation: To ensure the priority queue behaves like a min-heap (or max-heap) based on path length, the distance must be the first element of the pair: '{distance, vertex}'.
code: pq.push({u, dist[u]});

description: What is the issue with this 'Find Path' BFS return?

options.text: The function returns 'true' as soon as it adds the destination to the queue, but it fails to mark it as visited, leading to redundant work.
Actually, the code is missing the actual return 'true' statement when the target is found inside the loop.
BFS cannot return booleans.
The return statement should be outside the while loop.

explanation: If 'v == target', we've found a path. The function should return 'true' immediately. This code pushes the target into the queue but never checks if it's the target when popping.

description: Identify the bug in this Adjacency List building for a weighted graph.

options.text: The weight is added as a separate integer neighbor, effectively doubling the number of edges and vertices in the graph's internal representation.
Weights cannot be stored in vectors.
The 'pair' constructor 'make_pair' is deprecated.
The second element in the pair should be the vertex ID.

explanation: For a weighted adjacency list, each entry should be a 'pair<int, int>'. This code pushes 'v' and 'w' separately, so the list for 'u' now contains two unrelated neighbors.

description: Why does this 'Smallest Weight Edge' search fail?

options.text: The 'minWeight' variable is initialized to 0, which is smaller than any positive edge weight, so the condition 'e.w < minWeight' is never true.
Edge weights are always negative.
It only checks the first edge.
The 'Edge' struct doesn't exist in C++.

explanation: To find a minimum, you must initialize the comparison variable to a very large value (like 'INT_MAX'). Initializing to 0 only works for finding maximums (if all values are positive).

description: Identify the bug in this Tarjan's algorithm implementation for finding Strongly Connected Components (SCCs).

options.text: The low-link value update for a back-edge should use 'disc[v]' instead of 'low[v]' to avoid incorrectly pulling up values from nodes that are part of a different SCC traversal branch.
The function fails to push the node onto the stack before visiting neighbors.
SCCs can only be found in undirected graphs using this method.
The 'disc' array must be initialized to 0.

explanation: In Tarjan's, when we encounter a node 'v' that is already on the stack (a back-edge), we must update the current node's low-link value with 'disc[v]'. Using 'low[v]' here is a common error that can lead to incorrect SCC groupings in complex graphs.

description: Why does this Dinic's Algorithm BFS function for finding levels fail in a network flow graph?

options.text: It fails to check if the residual capacity of the edge is greater than 0, potentially creating levels through saturated edges.
Dinic's algorithm requires DFS for level construction.
The 'level' array should be initialized to -1 for the sink only.
BFS cannot be used to find levels in a flow network.

explanation: A level graph in Dinic's must only consist of edges that can actually carry more flow. If the residual capacity 'cap - flow' is 0, that edge cannot be part of the level graph.

description: Identify the bug in this DSU (Disjoint Set Union) 'Union by Rank' function.

options.text: The rank should only be incremented when two trees of the same rank are merged.
The function incorrectly uses 'parent[u] = v' instead of finding the roots first, causing union operations to fail on non-root nodes.
Rank must be decremented for the smaller tree.
Both A and B are critical bugs in this snippet.

explanation: Union operations must be performed on the root representatives of the sets. Additionally, the rank of the new root only increases if the two merging trees had equal rank; otherwise, the taller tree's rank remains sufficient.

description: What is wrong with this 'Find Bridges' (Articulation Edges) implementation using DFS?

options.text: The condition 'low[v] > disc[u]' correctly identifies a bridge, but the code fails to update 'low[u]' with 'low[v]' after the recursive call.
Bridges can only be found in directed graphs.
The 'disc' and 'low' values should be initialized to infinity.
The root of the DFS tree is always a bridge.

explanation: In Tarjan's bridge-finding algorithm, after exploring a neighbor 'v', the current node 'u' must update its 'low[u]' value based on 'low[v]' to propagate the discovery of back-edges back up the recursion tree.

description: Identify the bug in this Edmonds-Karp implementation for Max Flow.

options.text: The BFS correctly finds an augmenting path, but the code fails to update the flow of the reverse (residual) edges, violating the flow conservation principle.
Edmonds-Karp requires DFS to find the shortest path.
The bottleneck capacity should be added to the residual edges instead of subtracted.
BFS must start from the sink.

explanation: When an augmenting path is found, you must subtract the bottleneck capacity from the forward edges AND add it to the reverse edges to allow the algorithm to 'undo' flow decisions in later iterations.

description: Why does this Kosaraju's Algorithm implementation for SCCs produce incorrect results?

options.text: The second DFS should be performed on the transposed (reversed) graph, but this code uses the original adjacency list.
The stack must be cleared after the first DFS.
Kosaraju's only works for DAGs.
The first DFS should be iterative.

explanation: Kosaraju's requires: 1) DFS to get finish times, 2) Transpose the graph, 3) DFS on the transposed graph in the order of the finish times (popping from the stack).

description: Identify the bug in this Prim's Algorithm implementation using a priority queue.

options.text: The 'visited' check must be performed immediately after popping from the priority queue to avoid processing nodes multiple times with stale distances.
Prim's algorithm only works for directed graphs.
Priority queue must use 'less<int>' for MST.
Edge weights must be negative.

explanation: In the priority queue version of Prim's, a node might be pushed into the queue multiple times with different weights. We must check 'if (visited[u]) continue;' after popping to ensure we only process the minimum weight version.

description: What is the issue with this 'Lowest Common Ancestor' (LCA) implementation using Binary Lifting?

options.text: The jump loop 'i from LOG to 0' is correct, but it incorrectly moves 'u' and 'v' if 'up[u][i] == up[v][i]', which might overshoot the LCA.
Binary lifting only works for binary trees, not general graphs.
The preprocessing of the 'up' table should be done in O(N^2).
The jump should be 'i from 0 to LOG'.

explanation: When jumping 'u' and 'v' up, we should only jump if 'up[u][i] != up[v][i]'. If they are equal, it means 'up[u][i]' is an ancestor, but not necessarily the *lowest* common ancestor. We stop just below the LCA.

description: Identify the bug in this implementation of Floyd-Warshall.

options.text: The intermediate vertex 'k' loop must be the outermost loop; placing it inside 'i' and 'j' fails to correctly propagate shortest paths discovered in previous iterations.
The diagonal 'dist[i][i]' must be initialized to infinity.
Floyd-Warshall is O(V^2), not O(V^3).
It fails if the graph has positive weight cycles.

explanation: Floyd-Warshall is a DP algorithm. The state depends on having considered all possible intermediate nodes up to 'k'. If 'k' is inner, the paths won't be updated correctly.

description: Why does this Bellman-Ford implementation fail to detect negative cycles?

options.text: It lacks the V-th iteration check; if any distance can still be relaxed after V-1 iterations, a negative cycle exists.
Bellman-Ford only detects negative cycles for source-reachable nodes.
The distance matrix should be used instead of a distance array.
Both A and B are valid concerns for a robust implementation.

explanation: Standard Bellman-Ford performs $V-1$ relaxations. If a $V$-th relaxation still reduces a distance, that edge must be part of or reachable from a negative cycle. Also, it only detects cycles reachable from the source.

description: Identify the bug in this Hierholzer’s Algorithm for finding an Eulerian Circuit.

options.text: The current node should be pushed to the 'circuit' stack *after* all its edges are explored (post-order), not before entering the loop.
It uses a Queue instead of a Stack for the final circuit.
The graph must be a tree.
It fails if the graph has an even number of edges.

explanation: Hierholzer’s uses a backtracking approach. You explore as far as possible, and only when a node has no remaining edges do you add it to the circuit. This ensures loops are merged correctly.

description: What is the issue with this Tarjan's Articulation Points implementation?

options.text: The special case for the DFS root is missing; a root node is an articulation point if and only if it has more than one child in the DFS tree.
Articulation points cannot be found in undirected graphs.
The condition 'low[v] >= disc[u]' should be 'low[v] > disc[u]'.
Low-link values should be initialized to 0.

explanation: A root node 'r' has no 'low[v] >= disc[r]' ancestors by definition. It is an articulation point solely based on whether its removal splits the tree into multiple components (more than 1 child).

description: Identify the bug in this Hopcroft-Karp algorithm for Maximum Bipartite Matching.

options.text: In the DFS phase, the algorithm fails to mark nodes as 'visited' within the same DFS run, leading to redundant paths and $O(E^2)$ behavior.
BFS must be used for the matching phase.
Hopcroft-Karp is only for perfect matchings.
Distances should be calculated from the right side nodes.

explanation: While DFS finds the augmenting paths, if you don't 'visited' nodes during the DFS, you might traverse the same edges multiple times in a single phase, losing the efficiency of the algorithm.

description: Why does this 'Cycle Detection in Undirected Graph' using DSU fail?

options.text: The code fails to check if 'u' and 'v' are already in the same set before performing 'unite'; if they are, an edge between them forms a cycle.
DSU cannot detect cycles in undirected graphs.
Path compression breaks cycle detection.
Edges must be sorted by weight.

explanation: To detect a cycle with DSU, for every edge $(u, v)$, you check 'find(u) == find(v)'. if true, a cycle is found. If false, you 'unite(u, v)'.

description: Identify the bug in this Suurballe's Algorithm implementation for disjoint paths.

options.text: After the first Dijkstra, the edge weights are not modified to 'w_uv - d(s, v) + d(s, u)', which is necessary to ensure non-negative weights for the second Dijkstra.
Suurballe's only works for DAGs.
The first path should be removed from the graph entirely.
Negative weights are handled by Bellman-Ford, not Dijkstra.

explanation: Suurballe's uses a coordinate transformation (reduced costs) based on the first shortest path distances to ensure that when we reverse edges, we don't create negative weight edges for the second pass.

description: What is the bug in this LCA preprocessing (Binary Lifting)?

options.text: The nested loops are in the wrong order; the jump power 'i' must be the outer loop to ensure that 'up[u][i-1]' is already fully computed for all 'u'.
The jump 'up[u][i]' should be 'up[up[u][i-1]][i-1]'.
Log N is too small for 10^5 nodes.
Both A and B are correct.

explanation: To calculate the $2^i$ jump, you need the $2^{i-1}$ jump to be ready for *all* nodes. If 'u' is the outer loop, 'up[up[u][i-1]][i-1]' might refer to an 'i-1' jump of a parent that hasn't been processed yet.

description: Identify the bug in this 'Topological Sort' using DFS.

options.text: The function fails to detect cycles; if the graph is not a DAG, the topological sort is invalid, and this DFS will potentially hang or produce garbage.
Topological sort requires BFS (Kahn's).
The result should be reversed at the end.
Nodes should be pushed to the stack before the recursive call.

explanation: A topological sort only exists for DAGs. A medium/hard bug is failing to use a 'recursion stack' array to identify back-edges and throw an error if a cycle exists.

description: Why does this 'Check Bipartite' function using DFS fail on specific graphs?

options.text: It fails to check adjacent nodes that are already colored; if they have the same color as the current node, the graph is not bipartite.
DFS cannot check for bipartiteness; BFS is required.
It only works for planar graphs.
The 'color' array should be initialized to 1.

explanation: The core of the bipartiteness check is 'if color[v] == color[u] return false'. This code only recurses into uncolored nodes but ignores potential conflicts with already colored neighbors.

description: Identify the bug in this implementation of 'Johnson's Algorithm'.

options.text: The algorithm fails to add a dummy source node connected to all other nodes with 0-weight edges before running the initial Bellman-Ford.
Johnson's algorithm is O(V^4).
It cannot handle negative weights.
Dijkstra's is run on the original graph weights.

explanation: Johnson's requires re-weighting edges to be non-negative. To find the 'potential' for each vertex, we need a source that reaches everyone. Without the dummy node, we can't get potentials for disconnected components.
code:
bellman_ford(source_node); 
// Bug: No dummy node added

description: What is the bug in this 'Centroid Decomposition' preprocessing?

options.text: The subtree size is not recalculated after a centroid is removed, leading to incorrect centroid selection in subsequent recursive steps.
Centroid decomposition is only for binary trees.
The time complexity is O(N^2).
It fails on graphs with cycles.

explanation: Centroid selection depends on knowing the current size of the *remaining* tree. If you don't run 'dfs_size' after splitting the tree, you'll pick the wrong centroid, potentially breaking the $O(log N)$ depth guarantee.

description: Identify the bug in this 'Maximum Weight Independent Set' on a Tree (DP).

options.text: When a node is 'included' in the set, its children MUST be 'excluded'; however, if a node is 'excluded', its children can be EITHER 'included' or 'excluded' (take the max).
Independent sets cannot be found on trees using DP.
The weights must be sorted.
The base case for leaves is missing.

explanation: If 'dp[u][0]' (excluding 'u'), the value is $sum max(dp[v][0], dp[v][1])$. If 'dp[u][1]' (including 'u'), the value is $weight[u] + sum dp[v][0]$. This code forces the children to be included when the parent is excluded.
code:
dp[u][0] += dp[v][1]; // Bug: forced inclusion
dp[u][1] += dp[v][0];

description: Why does this 'All-Pairs Shortest Path' using N-Dijkstras fail on graphs with negative weights?

options.text: Dijkstra's algorithm is greedy and fails to correctly re-evaluate nodes once a shorter path is found via a negative edge.
It should use BFS instead.
Negative weights require the graph to be bipartite.
It works if the negative weights are only on self-loops.

explanation: Dijkstra's assumes that once a node is visited, its shortest path is found. Negative edges violate this because a much longer path of positive edges followed by a very negative edge could be shorter.
code: for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) dijkstra(i);

description: Identify the bug in this 'Heavy-Light Decomposition' (HLD) path query.

options.text: When jumping between chains, the code fails to account for the fact that 'u' and 'v' might be on the same chain but 'u' is lower than 'v'; it must process the range between them.
HLD requires a segment tree per node.
Chain heads should be updated using BFS.
The depth check 'dep[head[u]] < dep[head[v]]' is reversed.

explanation: Once both 'u' and 'v' are on the same chain, you must still perform a query on the segment tree for the range of indices between 'u' and 'v' (the vertical segment on that chain).

description: What is the issue with this 'Multi-source BFS' implementation?

options.text: The 'dist' of the sources must be initialized to 0 BEFORE pushing them into the queue, otherwise the first level of neighbors will have incorrect distances.
Sources must be pushed one by one with a clear queue in between.
It only works for undirected graphs.
Multi-source BFS is just a single BFS from a dummy node.

explanation: Standard BFS logic: mark distance and visited status at the moment of discovery (pushing). If you wait until popping, you might push the same node multiple times from different sources.
code:
for (int s : sources) q.push(s);
// Bug: dist[s] not set to 0

description: Identify the bug in this 'Min-Cost Max-Flow' (SPFA-based) implementation.

options.text: The algorithm fails to update the 'in_queue' flag after popping from the SPFA queue, leading to nodes not being re-added when a shorter (cheaper) path is found.
SPFA cannot handle negative costs.
Flow should be updated using DFS.
It uses a priority queue instead of a deque.

explanation: SPFA (Shortest Path Faster Algorithm) relies on the 'in_queue' flag to ensure we don't redundantly add nodes, but we must set it to false upon popping so the node can be updated again if an even better cost appears.
code:
int u = q.front(); q.pop();
// Missing: in_queue[u] = false;

description: Why is this '2-SAT' implementation using SCCs bugged?

options.text: The assignment is incorrect: if 'x' and 'not x' are in the same SCC, the formula is unsatisfiable; however, the code fails to check this condition.
The edges should be 'x -> y' instead of 'not x -> y'.
SCCs must be processed in decreasing order of finish time.
2-SAT is NP-hard and cannot be solved with graphs.

explanation: The fundamental theorem of 2-SAT states that a formula is satisfiable iff no variable and its negation are in the same SCC. This is the primary check.
code: if (scc[x] == scc[not_x]) return false; // Missing this check

description: Identify the bug in this 'Stoer-Wagner' Min-Cut algorithm logic.

options.text: The algorithm correctly finds the 'Minimum Cut-of-the-Phase', but it fails to contract the last two nodes into a single node before the next phase.
Stoer-Wagner only works for directed graphs.
The weights should be binary (0 or 1).
It uses max-flow internally.

explanation: Stoer-Wagner works by repeatedly finding a cut between two nodes 's' and 't', then merging 's' and 't' into one node. This reduces the search space until only one node remains.
code:
min_cut = min(min_cut, phase_cut);
// Bug: nodes not merged

description: What is the issue with this 'Dominator Tree' (Lengauer-Tarjan) semi-dominator step?

options.text: When calculating the semi-dominator of 'w', the algorithm incorrectly uses 'semi[v]' for nodes 'v' that are already processed but have a higher discovery time than 'w'.
Dominator trees are only for undirected graphs.
The 'semi' values must be initialized to 0.
It fails if the graph is not a DAG.

explanation: Semi-dominator calculation is subtle. You look at ancestors in the DFS tree and predecessors. The property involves 'min(semi[v])' across a specific set of paths. Errors usually stem from incorrect node ordering or DSU path compression handling.
code: semi[w] = min(semi[w], semi[eval(v)]);

description: Identify the bug in this 'Bron-Kerbosch' algorithm for Maximal Cliques.

options.text: The pivot selection is missing; without a pivot, the algorithm explores many redundant subproblems, leading to exponential time complexity even for sparse graphs.
The 'P' (potential) set should be a stack.
Bron-Kerbosch only finds cliques of size 3.
It fails on weighted graphs.

explanation: The 'with pivoting' version of Bron-Kerbosch significantly reduces the number of recursive calls by picking a node 'u' and only exploring neighbors of 'u' that are in 'P' but not in 'X'.

description: Why is this 'Floyd-Warshall' check for negative cycles bugged?

options.text: A negative cycle is present if 'dist[i][i] < 0' for ANY 'i'; the code checks only the sum of the diagonal, which is incorrect.
Floyd-Warshall cannot detect negative cycles.
Negative cycles only matter if they are on the path from source to sink.
The check should be 'dist[i][j] < 0'.

explanation: If 'dist[i][i]' becomes negative, it means there is a path from 'i' back to 'i' with negative total weight. This is the definitive indicator of a negative cycle in Floyd-Warshall.

description: Identify the bug in this TypeScript variable declaration intended to hold only numbers.

The type annotation ': number' is missing, allowing the variable to potentially hold any type of data later.
The code is correct; TypeScript uses automatic type inference here.
Numbers must be wrapped in quotes in TypeScript.

However, since TypeScript infers 'number' here, option C is technically true, but usually, 'bugfixer' implies enforcing strictness.

The function must return a string.

description: Why does this 'this' narrowing fail in a class method?

description: Identify the bug in this 'Nominal Typing' simulation using branding.
